BrianPugh/cyclopts#238.body:
Hello @BrianPugh,

Thank you so much for the project—it's truly amazing and simplifies many tasks!

I have recently started my journey with Cyclopts, and I've found that testing my CLIs can be quite cumbersome due to the lack of mechanisms that facilitate this.

For example, with a simple app:

```python
from cyclopts import App

app = App()

@app.command
def buzz(n: int):
    print(f"BUZZ: {n}")
```

Testing requires some workarounds, such as using `capsys`:

```python
def test_fizz(capsys):
    app('buzz 5')
    assert capsys.readouterr().out.strip() == 'BUZZ: 5'
```

While I understand it's possible to return the value before printing, as seen in [App Calling & Return Values](https://cyclopts.readthedocs.io/en/latest/app_calling.html), I believe this process could be simplified.

For instance, Typer has a testing client called [CLIRunner](https://typer.tiangolo.com/tutorial/testing/#test-the-app), which is inherited from the [CLIRunner](https://click.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/api/#click.testing.CliRunner) in Click. Additionally, libraries like Cleo also provide a [testing client](https://cleo.readthedocs.io/en/latest/introduction.html#testing-commands).

Implementing something similar in Cyclopts would greatly facilitate application development.

Thank you in advance for considering this suggestion!
BrianPugh/cyclopts#238.title: A Testing Client

PrefectHQ/prefect#6990.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar request and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this feature.

### Prefect Version

2.x

### Describe the current behavior

To perform a healthcheck, manual API requests must be made.

### Describe the proposed behavior

We should provide a CLI command to perform healthchecks against the configured server: `prefect server status`

This command should return a 0 or 1 status code depending on the availability of the server. It can also display information about the server if it is available.

This command should include a `--wait` flag that will attempt to connect until the service responds with a healthy value.

This command should include a `--timeout <seconds>` option that will fail the healthcheck after a total number of seconds.

### Example Use

This is especially useful in combination with `--detach` as proposed in #6989 

```
prefect orion start --detach
prefect orion status --wait --timeout 60
```

Possible output

```
$ prefect orion status
Connecting to <address>...
Server available.
...
```

Also useful when run in a Docker container


### Additional context

This would replace the script at https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/scripts/wait-for-server.py
PrefectHQ/prefect#6990.title: Add `prefect server status`

PrefectHQ/prefect#8124.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue.
- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

Running the flow below should result in the `cleanup_task` being always executed. The example below works in Prefect 2.7.6 (and earlier), but not in 2.7.7, which could be related to changes made to annotations 

cc @madkinsz 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86264395/211579895-09f9158e-cba0-4aff-b733-d36562034d88.png) 



### Reproduction

```python3
from prefect import flow, task, get_run_logger, allow_failure


@task
def extract():
    return [1, 2, 3]


@task
def add_one(x):
    if x == 2:
        raise Exception("Something is not right")
    return x + 1


@task
def add_two(x):
    return x + 2


@task
def cleanup_task():
    get_run_logger().info("Cleaning up e.g. removing temp Ray cluster")


@flow
def map_with_cleanup_task():
    a = extract()
    b = add_one.map(a)
    c = add_two.map(b)
    cleanup_task.submit(wait_for=[allow_failure(c)])


if __name__ == "__main__":
    map_with_cleanup_task()
```


### Error

_No response_

### Versions

```Text
Version:             2.7.7
API version:         0.8.4
Python version:      3.10.8
Git commit:          e8ca30b8
Built:               Fri, Jan 6, 2023 4:25 PM
OS/Arch:             darwin/arm64
Profile:             stg
Server type:         cloud
```


### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#8124.title: `allow_failure()` doesn't work as expected with mapped tasks in 2.7.7

PrefectHQ/prefect#8177.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I refreshed the page and this issue still occurred.
- [X] I checked if this issue was specific to the browser I was using by testing with a different browser.

### Bug summary

The order of fields in the Blocks' is not preserved between the code and the UI. As per pydantic [documentation](https://docs.pydantic.dev/usage/models/#field-ordering):

> As of v1.0 all fields with annotations (whether annotation-only or with a default value) will precede all fields without an annotation. Within their respective groups, fields remain in the order they were defined.


### Reproduction

1. Create a `CustomBlock` class in `custom.py`:
```python
from typing import Optional

from prefect.blocks.core import Block
from pydantic import Field

class CustomBlock(Block):
    a: Optional[int] = Field(default=None)
    b: str = Field(default="abc")
    c: Optional[str] = Field(default=None)
```
2. Register using `prefect block register -m mypackage.custom`
3. Modify the code such as we now have:
```python
from typing import Optional

from prefect.blocks.core import Block
from pydantic import Field

class CustomBlock(Block):
    b: str = Field(default="bca") # to ensure block was properly registered again
    c: Optional[str] = Field(default=None)
    a: Optional[int] = Field(default=None)
```

### Error

After the first register, the order of the fields is correct.
![Capture1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3171960/212716474-daa1ff3c-48b8-4715-a0ed-18bd305a1bc3.JPG)

After the second register, despite re-arranging the fields in the code, the initial order is preserved.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3171960/212716490-932ce651-8031-4577-8310-27d67f347aff.jpeg)


### Browsers

- [ ] Chrome
- [ ] Firefox
- [ ] Safari
- [X] Edge

### Prefect version

```Text
Version:             2.7.8
API version:         0.8.4
Python version:      3.10.5
Git commit:          75d2e146
Built:               Thu, Jan 12, 2023 1:41 PM
OS/Arch:             linux/x86_64
Profile:             default
Server type:         hosted
```


### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#8177.title: Order of Blocks' forms is not preserved

PrefectHQ/prefect#8216.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue.
- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

I have a Prefect flow that runs inside a `DockerContainer` infrastructure block where `image_pull_policy` is set to `ALWAYS`. Recently the flow started to fail following an update to the Docker image and we found that:
1. The disk on the machine running Prefect Agent was full;
2. The Prefect agent was attempting to pull the latest Docker image, but was unable to do so because the disk was full;
3. Instead of raising an error, the Prefect agent would run the flow in the last available image, and then fail.

By running `docker pull` manually we were able to diagnose the problem, but it would be helpful if Prefect was able to raise an error to indicate the problem, rather than silently running the flow in an old container.

### Reproduction

```python3
Can be reproduced with any `hello-world` flow running in a DockerContainer block.
```


### Error

_No response_

### Versions

```Text
Version:             2.6.9
API version:         0.8.3
Python version:      3.9.16
Git commit:          014d093e
Built:               Tue, Nov 22, 2022 2:14 PM
OS/Arch:             linux/x86_64
Profile:             prod
Server type:         cloud
```


### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#8216.title: Docker pull fails silently if disk is full

PrefectHQ/prefect#8248.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar request and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this feature.

### Prefect Version

2.x

### Describe the current behavior

Per this discussion ([slack](https://prefect-community.slack.com/archives/CL09KU1K7/p1674581989933539)), we discovered that the stacklevel on `[print_as_log](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/prefect/logging/loggers.py#L221)()` isn't appropriate if you use custom formatting.

I used this formatter:
```
formatters:
    simple:
        format: "%(asctime)s.%(msecs)03d | %(message)s"
        datefmt: "%H:%M:%S"

    standard:
        (): prefect.logging.formatters.PrefectFormatter
        format: "%(asctime)s.%(msecs)03d | %(levelname)-7s | %(name)s | %(filename)s:%(lineno)s - %(funcName)20s() - %(message)s"
        flow_run_fmt: "%(asctime)s.%(msecs)03d | %(levelname)-7s | Flow run %(flow_run_name)r | %(filename)s:%(lineno)s - %(funcName)20s() | %(message)s"
        task_run_fmt: "%(asctime)s.%(msecs)03d | %(levelname)-7s | Task run %(task_run_name)r | %(filename)s:%(lineno)s - %(funcName)20s() | %(message)s"
        datefmt: "%H:%M:%S"
```

But as you can see in the log below, the logger just prints out `print_as_log()` rather than my calling function.

```
14:17:33.136 | INFO    | Flow run 'practical-jackrabbit' | loggers.py:221 -         print_as_log() | Config: ...
```

### Describe the proposed behavior

change `log.info()` to `log.info(..., stacklevel=2)` or something on [print_as_log](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/prefect/logging/loggers.py#L221.

```
14:17:33.136 | INFO    | Flow run 'practical-jackrabbit' | my_modele.py:221 -         my_function_which_has_a_print() | Config: ...
```

### Example Use

Helps print out more useful information, such as where the print statement was invoked.

### Additional context

see above for slack message.
PrefectHQ/prefect#8248.title: Improve @flow(log_prints=True) to include appropriate stacklevel so it can output proper function names

PrefectHQ/prefect#8753.body: ### First check - [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue. - [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies. ### Versions ```Text Version: 2.8.3 API version: 0.8.4 Python version: 3.9.12 Git commit: 07de1ac4 Built: Thu, Feb 23, 2023 4:31 PM OS/Arch: darwin/x86_64 Profile: default Server type: server ``` ### Bug summary I was noticing that my tasks which have decorators were failing after reporting that kwargs were being passed in as args. Here's a full code reproduction that worked for @zzstoatzz [here](https://prefect-community.slack.com/archives/CL09KU1K7/p1678271799353059) ```python from functools import wraps from prefect import flow, get_run_logger, task def decorator(fn): @wraps(fn) def wrapper(a, b, **kwargs): print(f"kwargs are {kwargs}") return fn(a, b, **kwargs) return wrapper @task @decorator def add_noargs(a, b, logger=None): result = a + b if logger is not None: logger.info(f"Adding {a} and {b} to get {result}") return result @flow(log_prints=True) def an_example_flow(): logger = get_run_logger() logger.info("Starting an ECS task") a, b = 1, 2 add_noargs(a, b, logger=logger) logger.info("Everything is done!") if __name__ == "__main__": from loguru import logger logger.info("Run the flow locally") an_example_flow() ``` Which produces: ```bash 2023-03-08 21:39:19.387 | ERROR | __main__::63 - add_noargs() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given ``` Diving a bit deeper we can reproduce the underlying issue with only a few lines: ### Reproduction ```python3 from prefect.utilities.callables import parameters_to_args_kwargs, get_call_parameters def fn(a, b, c=3): return a + b + c parameters = get_call_parameters(fn, (1, 2), {"c": 100}) print(parameters) args, kwargs = parameters_to_args_kwargs(fn, parameters) print(args, kwargs) ``` We start with args and kwargs, and get back only args. This is a tricky one. Because naively the above code does confuse args and kwargs, but it runs fine *because* the kwarg can be accepted as an arg. However, as soon as you wrap it in a decorator with `@wraps` on it to mimck the underlying signature, you can't stuff the kwarg in as an arg any more, as it actually needs to be consumed by the `**kwargs`. You can force this by adding `*args` to the signature, but its not that intuitive. Or to boil it down even simpler, in `parameters_to_args_kwargs`, the signature is determined via: ```python inspect.signature(fn) ``` When in reality a decorated function needs args and kwargs that are consistent with **both**: ```python inspect.signature(fn) inspect.signature(fn, follow_wrapped=False) ``` And right now the fact that these can be different is the cause of the issue. As far as I can see, there are two ways forward with this, given how low level all this code is. 1. Explicitly don't support decorators that don't have an `*args` in their signature, as the *args will cause the bound signature parameter kind for kwargs to correct be set to `VAR_KEYWORD` 2. Update `parameters_to_args` to move args to kwargs if the bound argument has a default Option two also has the benefit of being more "correct" in that all kwargs will actually be passed as kwargs, regardless of if they would have worked as args in general cases. As to how to implement option two, using the example given in the standard library [here](https://docs.python.org/3/library/inspect.html#inspect.Parameter.kind) about how to check for defaults, I believe this code change would work (see the commented bit of code near the bottom). The TLDR is, if python says you can specify it as a keyword, *and* if it has a default value, then yeah, make sure it goes in as a keyword instead of going in as an arg. ```python def parameters_to_args_kwargs(fn: Callable, parameters: Dict[str, Any]) -> Tuple[Tuple[Any, ...], Dict[str, Any]]: """ Convert a `parameters` dictionary to positional and keyword arguments The function _must_ have an identical signature to the original function or this will return an empty tuple and dict. """ function_params = dict(inspect.signature(fn).parameters).keys() # Check for parameters that are not present in the function signature unknown_params = parameters.keys() - function_params if unknown_params: raise SignatureMismatchError.from_bad_params(list(function_params), list(parameters.keys())) signature = inspect.signature(fn) # This block here will enforce that potential kwargs, as specified by their default, # are passed in as kwargs. This is necessary in case something like functool.wraps has obfuscated # the actual underlying signature of the function. strict_params = [] for param in signature.parameters.values(): if param.kind == inspect.Parameter.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD and param.default is not inspect.Parameter.empty: param = param.replace(kind=inspect.Parameter.KEYWORD_ONLY) strict_params.append(param) bound_signature = signature.replace(parameters=strict_params).bind_partial() bound_signature.arguments = parameters return bound_signature.args, bound_signature.kwargs ``` With the above changes made to my local prefect, you can see that the failing decorator now works correctly. In addition, *all* functin parameters which were passed into the task as kwargs, will now actually continue to be passed in as kwargs. On that note, this feels more like an issue with Python itself than prefect, and I've actually raised with CPython as well [here](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/102551) as well.
PrefectHQ/prefect#8753.title: Prefect Task arg/kwarg binding is passing in kwargs as args, which can break things in decorators

### Bug summary

If you create an automation with a custom trigger and then go back to edit that automation, when the trigger screen loads the Trigger Type field defaults to 'Flow Run State' rather than your custom trigger you created.  If you change the dropdown to 'Custom' then the custom trigger is shown correctly on the form.

The issue is that if you don't change the trigger dropdown you can lose your custom trigger settings if you submit the form.  For example I just went in to add a tag to my automation with a custom trigger and I submitted the form without remembering to change the trigger type.  The trigger was reverted to 'Flow Run State' and I lost the custom trigger I had previously created.

The form should populate with the current settings when editing an automation.

Self explanatory

None

- [ ] Edge

_No response_

PrefectHQ/prefect#9747.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue.
- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

I have a dataclass, a task, and a flow. 

In the dataclass, it checks one of its own parameters and initializes that parameter to a value if it's a `None` inside the __post_init__(). If the __post_init__() gets called a second time, then it will think that the same parameter is no longer `None` and the parameter gets set a different value.

A lead software engineer from Prefect addressed and confirmed this behavior. It is due to when `futures` objects get passed down the DAG, the parameters get "resolved", and that's why __post_init__() was called again. This is a pretty "silent" behavior that can catch people off guard, that's why I'm still posting this issue with reproducible code below. 

TL;DR: Be careful of the "state" of your objects as they get passed around the DAG!

### Reproduction

```python3
import prefect
from prefect import flow, task
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class testDC:
    frodo: int
    baggings: int = None

    def __post_init__(self):
        print("post_init triggered")
        if self.baggings is None:
            print("self.baggings is None triggered")
            self.baggings = self.frodo * 2
        else:
            print("self.baggings should not be checked again. This should never be triggered.")
            self.baggings = 420

# Simple task that takes in an instance of the test dataclass above and prints it. Does no manipulation.
@task(log_prints=True)
def testTask(dc):
    print(dc)

# Simple flow that takes in a parameter, instantiates the test dataclass, and pass the dataclass to the task.
@flow(log_prints=True)
def testFlow(a: int):

    foo = testDC(a)
    testTask(foo)

if __name__ == "__main__":

    # Run the flow with a simple parameter
    testFlow(a=1)
    print(prefect.__version__)
```


### Error

```python3
12:32:41.624 | INFO    | prefect.engine - Created flow run 'russet-doberman' for flow 'testFlow'
12:32:41.775 | INFO    | Flow run 'russet-doberman' - post_init triggered
12:32:41.777 | INFO    | Flow run 'russet-doberman' - self.baggings is None triggered
12:32:41.811 | INFO    | Flow run 'russet-doberman' - Created task run 'testTask-0' for task 'testTask'
12:32:41.812 | INFO    | Flow run 'russet-doberman' - Executing 'testTask-0' immediately...
12:32:41.817 | INFO    | Flow run 'russet-doberman' - post_init triggered
12:32:41.818 | INFO    | Flow run 'russet-doberman' - self.baggings should not be checked again. This should never be triggered.
12:32:41.879 | INFO    | Task run 'testTask-0' - testDC(frodo=1, baggings=420)
12:32:41.923 | INFO    | Task run 'testTask-0' - Finished in state Completed()
12:32:41.969 | INFO    | Flow run 'russet-doberman' - Finished in state Completed('All states completed.')
2.8.6
```


### Versions

```Text
2.8.6
```


### Additional context

Thank you Zanie for the quick response on Prefect Slack!
PrefectHQ/prefect#9747.title: Dataclass `__post_init__` gets invoked again during task call

PrefectHQ/prefect#12367.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar request and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this feature.

### Prefect Version

2.x

### Describe the current behavior

Users must currently either pip install a `prefect-*` integration package at worker startup time, or build their own worker images, for the following worker types:

AWS Elastic Container Service: `prefect-aws`
Azure Container Instances: `prefect-azure`
Google Cloud Run: `prefect-gcp`
Google Cloud Run V2: `prefect-gcp`
Google Vertex AI: `prefect-gcp`

Installing packages at startup time can lead to a few undesirable outcomes, depending on the startup command they choose for their worker.
1. The integration package requires a newer version of prefect than is on the chosen image and the install is attempted by prefect itself, leading to errors.
2. The integration package requires a newer version of prefect than is on the chosen image and is installed prior to worker startup, making the running prefect version no longer in sync with the chosen image

The alternative of asking users to build their own images to maintain prefect and integration package compatibility feels like a bumpy experience for maintaining workers.

### Describe the proposed behavior

Build a `version.number-python3.x-aws`, `version.number-python3.x-azure`, and `version.number-python3.x-gcp` image per release, where 3.x is strictly one python version. Whether it's 3.11 or 3.12 I'll leave to the experts, but our Helm chart points to `2-python3.11-kubernetes`.

This should significantly reduce potential for worker startup related issues, and make onboarding workers significantly easier, especially as we approach agent deprecation. It has the added benefit of pairing Prefect versions with compatible integration packages, as our Kubernetes images already do.

If this is taken up, we should also update any documentation or guides for hosting workers that reference startup commands and images.

### Example Use

image: `2-python3.11-gcp`
command: `prefect worker start -p my-cloud-run-v2-pool`

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#12367.title: Build one `aws`, `azure`, and `gcp`-flavored image per release

PrefectHQ/prefect#15008.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Currently, Prefect supports many user interfaces that we attempt to maintain as compatible with both *synchronous* execution as well as *asynchronous* execution. [A popular example of this is `Block.load`](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/cc4ba866dad93b0fd62315d5b6487f574f874f8e/src/prefect/blocks/core.py#L822). The way this is achieved is through [an internal utility](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/cc4ba866dad93b0fd62315d5b6487f574f874f8e/src/prefect/utilities/asyncutils.py#L329) that "magically" - and more critically: **quietly** - attempts to decide on the user's behalf whether the user will be able to await the coroutine or not.

This works well in certain situations such as methods / utilities called *within* Prefect tasks and flows - this is because we explicitly track whether that task or flow is synchronous or asynchronous.

However, this can turn out *very poorly* in situations where something about the runtime environment changes between local development and production. A few examples to make the point:

* [https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14712](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14712)
* [https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14625](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14625)
* [https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/13181](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/13181)

Prefect 3.0 exposes a new keyword argument on these special methods / functions for explicitly setting the behavior a user wants: to continue with the block loading example, users can now specify `Block.load(**kwargs, _sync=True)` for enforcing synchronous execution and `await Block.load(**kwargs, _sync=False)` for enforcing asynchronous execution. This is useful as an escape hatch, but under the hood it still engages with complex event loop and threading logic that risks performance degradation and more difficult to inspect failure modes.

Clearly there is room for improvement here along a few dimensions:

* warning users who are relying on implicit magic so they can more easily self-debug / harden their code
* improving Prefect performance by not managing its own event loops / threads for running coroutines
* exposing `_sync=True/False` behavior in a more first class way for discoverability

### Describe the proposed behavior

To achieve the goals outlined above, I propose first expanding the `sync_compatible` interface in two ways:

* allowing for `sync_compatible(sync_version=sync_method)` that explicitly provides an alternative synchronous implementation to dispatch between; note this *does* mean expanding the codebase into both synchronous and asynchronous implementations. The decorator and our current "magic" will allow us to take [a strangler fig](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strangler_fig_pattern) approach and incrementally add these duplicate implementations as we develop
* exposing a mirror utility `async_compatible(async_version=async_method)` (the need for this will become clear below when I discuss naming conventions)

For any decorated function / method that has a dual implementation, Prefect can begin issuing a warning whenever the user relies on behavior for which that function / method is dispatching to another implementation. For example:

```python
await Block.load(**kwargs)
## warning is issued that directs the user to use `Block.aload(**kwargs)` explicitly
```

To make sure this part is not glossed over: this will ultimately result in Prefect maintaining *two* implementations for a large class of user interfaces (primarily those that interact with the Prefect client / API).

### Naming Convention

To achieve this, we will rely on the following naming conventions:

* classes for which the core implementation will change between sync / async will be sychronous by default, and the async versions will prefix their class name with `Async`; e.g., `PrefectClient` vs. `AsyncPrefectClient`
* whenever the class remains the same, synchronous methods will be the default and asynchronous methods will implemented with an `a` prefix: e.g., `Block.load` vs. `Block.aload`
* for top-line functions, the same will be true: implementations will be synchronous first, and async implementations will have an `a` prefix: e.g., `run_deployment` vs `arun_deployment`

There is one edge case with this, which is `.wait` methods on Prefect futures; for this we will make the corresponding *class* awaitable for async waits.

# contributing
for anyone willing to contribute to this effort, here's roughly how you can help

## removing `@sync_compatible`

`sync_compatible` breaks typing, so we want to introduce explicit (type-safe) async methods. the typing will still be broken on the `async_dispatch` function, but only until we can remove that compatibility layer (then it will be an explicit sync function)

## before
```python
@sync_compatible
async def some_fn_or_method(...): ...
```

## after
```python
async def some_async_fn_or_method(...): ...


@async_dispatch(some_async_fn_or_method)
def some_fn_or_method(...): ...
```



### Remaining uses of `@sync_compatible`

#### `prefect`

`prefect.artifacts`

- [x] `Artifact.create`
- [x] `Artifact.get`
- [x] `Artifact.get_or_create`
- [x] `create_link_artifact`
- [x] `create_markdown_artifact`
- [x] `create_table_artifact`
- [x] `update_progress_artifact`
- [x] `create_image_artifact`

`prefect.filesystems`

- [x] `LocalFileSystem.write_path`

PrefectHQ/prefect#16116.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Hey!
If something strange happens with a prefect-flow started by a `ecs`-worker, it is a little cumbersome to find the full `cloud-watch`-logs. 

For instance in a case like this: 
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/411e6d5f-14be-4cac-8926-1979f5a0b3de)

Here the task crashed withouth outputing the full logs to the prefect-ui, and we need to dig into the `cloud-watch`-logs.
But finding the correct `cloud-watch`-stream is currently not straight forward. We either need to search for something like the flow-name (might not be unique), or around the timestamp the issue occured. If there are a lot of other streams around the same time, this is more timeconsuming than it needs to be.

### Describe the proposed behavior

For instance extend https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/integrations/prefect-aws/prefect_aws/workers/ecs_worker.py#L796 with the `task_arn`. With this we would have a precise way of finding the correct log-stream

### Example Use

```
aws logs get-log-events \
    --log-group-name '<MY_KNOWN_LOG_GROUP_NAME>' \
    --log-stream-name '<MY_KNOWN_LOG_STREAM_PREFIX>/<MY_UNKNOWN_TASK_ID>' \
    --start-from-head
```

We could then use the `MY_UNKNOWN_TASK_ID` logged in the prefect-cloud-ui by the worker, to see if there are some more log-events in cloudwatch

### Additional context

If all logs from the log-stream could be delivered to the prefect-ui, that would also be a nice alternative
PrefectHQ/prefect#16116.title: Prefect-aws: Extend worker log with aws-task-id

PrefectHQ/prefect#16800.body:
[This](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/deploy/infrastructure-concepts/prefect-yaml#concurrency-limit-fields) concurrency limit fields section of the docs suggests that the deployment YAML has the following structure:

```yaml
deployments:
  ...
  concurrency_limit:
    limit: <int>
    collision_strategy: <str>
```

When in fact it has the following structure:

```yaml
deployments:
  ...
  concurrency_limit: <int>
  concurrency_options:
    collision_strategy: <str>
```

This also slightly conflicts with the documentation [here](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/deploy/index#concurrency-limiting).
PrefectHQ/prefect#16800.title: Fix inconsistent deployment concurrency limit documentation

PrefectHQ/prefect#16998.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Currently, `entrypoint` needs to be a path that is relative to the root of your github directory. _At the same time_, it needs to be relative to the CWD that you are running `prefect deploy` from, which leads to: **you must always call `prefect deploy` from the root of your directory**

This isn't always the best setup for repos (like multi-project repos), and it's also just a bit more confusing than it needs to be: relative-path routing is hard :D 



### Describe the proposed behavior

I'm proposing that `entrypoint` allow absolute module paths in python in addition to file paths, keyed internally in prefect by the presence of `.` in the value. If I have a repo like so
```
repo
|_ project
|_ _ Makefile
|_ _ pyproject.toml
|_ _ prefect.yaml
|_ _ flows
|_ _ _ my_flow.py
```
now, I can simply put 
`entrypoint: flows.my_flow.main` instead of having to think about paths. This way, I can guarantee that what i want to be run is being run, and itll work from anywhere.

This can take inspiration from modal, which offers the same `modal deploy src/project/app.py` or `modal deploy project.app` both work, and for production i always use the latter. 

### Example Use

```
deployments:
- name: my-flow
  version:
  tags: []
  description: Run my flow at some cadence
  entrypoint: my_app.my_flow.main
  ....
```

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#16998.title: allow `entrypoint` in a prefect.yaml to be a python module instead of a path

PrefectHQ/prefect#17388.body:
### Describe the current behavior

## Problem
There are many ways to use the task decorator, whether on functions or instance methods,  which today we try to handle all this with overloads.

In particular, using keyword arguments like `return_state` and `wait_for` in task calls makes proper type checking (nearly, if not) impossible. These kwargs modify the return type of a task (e.g., changing from `R` to `State[R]` when `return_state=True`), which breaks static type checking in `mypy` and `pyright`. This creates frustrating developer experiences and makes it difficult to rely on type checkers for preventing bugs.

```python
state = my_task(arg1, return_state=True)  # we can't quite handle all cases with overloads
```

### Describe the proposed behavior

Create explicit methods on `Task` objects that clearly indicate their return type, e.g.
- `call_and_return_state`
- `submit_and_return_state`
- existing `__call__`
- existing `submit`

### Example Use

```python
# Use explicit methods that return exactly what they say
state = my_task.call_and_return_state(...)
state = my_task.submit_and_return_state(...)
```

### Additional context

We'll want to re-use some interface so we don't have to maintain even more methods in different places

related to #17379
PrefectHQ/prefect#17388.title: create explicit methods for ambiguously typed `Task` kwargs

PrefectHQ/prefect#17415.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Currently, Prefect implements global concurrency limits that allow tasks to acquire locks on a number of slots from a shared pool. These limits can optionally be configured with a "leaky bucket" algorithm that gradually returns slots to the pool over time, effectively functioning as a rate limit.

When the slot decay is not configured (standard concurrency limit), there are scenarios where tasks can indefinitely hold onto concurrency slots:

1. If the container running a task dies unexpectedly
2. In some instances when a parent flow is canceled
3. When a task fails in a way that prevents proper cleanup

These orphaned concurrency slots become unavailable to other tasks, gradually depleting the pool. This situation is particularly difficult to debug as there's no clear indication of which tasks are holding onto slots or when they were acquired, leading to mysterious "concurrency limit reached" errors that require manual intervention to resolve.



### Describe the proposed behavior

To address these issues, we propose implementing a "leased lock" paradigm, common in distributed concurrency systems:

1. Each concurrency limit would enforce a lease duration for acquired slots
2. Tasks that acquire slots would need to periodically renew their lease while they're still running
3. If a task fails to renew its lease within the specified timeframe, the slots would be automatically released back to the pool

This approach provides several benefits:

- **Self-healing:** Orphaned slots automatically return to the pool after the lease expires
- **Visibility:** The renewal pattern provides a heartbeat mechanism that can be monitored
- **Configurable safety:** Lease durations can be tuned based on workload patterns
- **Compatibility:** Can work alongside existing slot decay configurations

## Implementation Details

The leased lock system would include:

- A configurable `lease_duration` parameter for concurrency limits (defaulting to a reasonable value like 5 minutes)
- A background renewal process that automatically extends leases for running tasks
- A cleanup mechanism that scans for expired leases and returns slots to the pool
- Logging and observability improvements to track lease acquisitions and releases

Users would be able to:
- Specify different lease durations for different concurrency limits
- Monitor lease status through the Prefect UI
- Configure alert policies for frequently expired leases (which might indicate infrastructure issues)

## Migration Path

This change would be backward compatible with existing concurrency limit configurations. By default, the lease duration would be set sufficiently high to prevent unexpected slot releases for most workloads, but low enough to recover from common failure scenarios.

For users experiencing frequent orphaned slots, enabling shorter lease durations would provide immediate relief without requiring changes to their task code.

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#17415.title: Prevent Orphaned Slots in Concurrency Limits

PrefectHQ/prefect#17749.body:
### Describe the current behavior

First let me say I’m very happy to see my project (`whenever`) is useful to you. I check in on new adopters of the library to gain valuable feedback on how it’s used in the real world.

Reading prefect’s datetime code using `whenever`, I see you already take care to account for DST.
I had a suggestion to improve things a bit more.

In `server/schemas/schedules.py`, it says:

>             # break the interval into `days` and `seconds` because the datetime
>             # library will handle DST boundaries properly if days are provided, but not
>             # if we add `total seconds`. Therefore, `next_date + self.interval`
>             # fails while `next_date.add(days=days, seconds=seconds)` works.

Yes—but it’s a bit more nuanced. The problem more precisely is that `datetime.timedelta` always assumes 24-hour days. So `timedelta(days=2)` and `timedelta(hours=48)` are indistinguishable:

```python
>>> timedelta(hours=48)
datetime.timedelta(days=2)
>>> timedelta(days=2)
datetime.timedelta(days=2)
```

Due to this, Prefect users would still have problems scheduling “exactly 48 hours”, since the code in `schedules.py` will silently assume this means 2 calendar days. But, of course, assuming the opposite would hurt users that specify `timedelta(days=2)`.

Below an example where this distinction matters. So long as you can’t distinguish the two durations, you can’t have consistently correct code.

```
2023-03-25T10[Europe/Amsterdam] + 48 hours -> 2023-03-27T11[Europe/Amsterdam]
2023-03-25T10[Europe/Amsterdam] + 2 days -> 2023-03-27T10[Europe/Amsterdam]
```



### Describe the proposed behavior

In the end, this can’t be fixed using `datetime.timedelta` (or `pendulum.Duration`), since they don’t distinguish calendar days from 24-hour periods.

Datetime libraries in other languages have solved this problem by separating calendar days from exact hours. Drawing inspiration from these libraries, `whenever` defines the `DateTimeDelta` type:

```python
>>> from whenever import DateTimeDelta
>>> DateTimeDelta(days=2)
DateTimeDelta(P2D)
>>> DateTimeDelta(hours=48)
DateTimeDelta(PT48H)
```

`ZonedDateTime(…).add(delta)` will then work as expected, distinguishing calendar days from exact hours.

Now, I'm not suggesting to *replace* `timedelta` with `DateTimeDelta` 😉, but supporting it as an option could help users be explicit when they need to be.

*PS: of course, let me know if there are any other suggestions/complaints about `whenever` so far*

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#17749.title: Improving Daylight-Savings-Time robustness in scheduler

PrefectHQ/prefect#18508.body:
### Describe the current behavior

The ECS worker provided by `prefect-aws` currently polls the ECS API to handle task state changes. This polling approach has limitations, including performance overhead and potential missed events if the worker crashes during task monitoring.

### Describe the proposed behavior

Updating the ECS worker to utilize AWS EventBridge and SQS to capture ECS task state changes (e.g., RUNNING, STOPPED) via AWS EventBridge and stream these events to the Prefect API. Events received via EventBridge and SQS can also be used to enhance crash detection for Prefect flows running in ECS tasks.

### Implementation Details

Update the ECS worker to:

- Configure an EventBridge rule targeting ECS task state changes on startup.
- Set up an SQS queue on startup to receive events from EventBridge.
- Consume messages from SQS and emit corresponding Prefect events.
- Update flow run states in Prefect according to the received ECS events.

### Benefits

- Improved performance by eliminating continuous polling of the ECS API.
- Enhanced robustness, ensuring ECS task failures are captured even if the ECS worker crashes.
- Enhanced visibility into ECS task execution within the Prefect UI.

### Additional context

This functionality will mirror enhancements made to the Kubernetes worker in https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/pull/18004.
PrefectHQ/prefect#18508.title: Implement event-driven ECS task state observation for ECS worker

PrefectHQ/prefect#18744.body:
### Bug summary

Long-running flow runs, ~2hrs+, remain in a cancelling state and the related flow run continues to run after attempting to cancel the run through the UI/API.

The runs for all intents and purposes appear to still be running without issue, as far as I can tell, but at a certain point the Runner no longer seems to be picking up cancelling states from the server so the cancellation request is ignored. I've only been able to reproduce this in containerized environment so far, this doesn't seem to apply locally or when using serve.

Code Example
```python
import asyncio, os

from prefect import flow, task, get_run_logger
from prefect.futures import wait


@task
async def long_running_task():
    logger = get_run_logger()
    try:
        while True:
            await asyncio.sleep(5)

    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        logger.warning(f"got cancellation signal!")
        raise

    except Exception as ex:
        logger.error(f" got unexpected exception: {ex}")
        raise


@flow(log_prints=True)
async def long_running_flow():
    logger = get_run_logger()
    tasks = []
    results = []

    for _ in range(0, 10):
        task = long_running_task.submit()
        tasks.append(task)
    logger.info(f"All tasks submitted!")

    wait(tasks)    

    try:
        for task in tasks:
            results.append(task.result())
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        logger.warning(f"main flow got manual cancellation! Notifying all tasks...")
        for task in tasks:

            task.cancel()
        raise
    except Exception as ex:
        logger.error(f"main flow unexpected failed: {ex}")
        raise
```

On recent versions of prefect 3.4.11 and websockets 13.0 or later after this executes for an extended period of time I'm no long able to cancel the flow run and it remains stuck, nothing super specific pops up in the logs beyond this when this occurs

PrefectHQ/prefect#18751.body: ### Bug summary The [description](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/2183e320998e3c0d0044ee4dc7fa131722396f0f/src/prefect/client/schemas/objects.py#L891-L893) for `total_run_time` on the `TaskRun` object notes that the value will be summed if a task run executes multiple times. When testing with a task that requires a retry to succeed, `total_run_time`'s value only contains the run time for the last execution, not the sum of both runs. MRE: ```python from random import randint from time import sleep from prefect import flow, task from prefect.context import get_run_context @task(retries=1) def task_that_fails_first() -> None: sleep_duration = randint(1, 10) print(f"sleeping for {sleep_duration} seconds") sleep(sleep_duration) context = get_run_context() print(f"run count: {context.task_run.run_count}") if context.task_run.run_count % 2 == 1: raise Exception("even run count, raising exception") print("done sleeping, returning") @flow(log_prints=True) def run_a_task() -> None: task_that_fails_first() if __name__ == "__main__": run_a_task.serve( name="run_a_task", ) ``` Grab the task run from the API or inspect "Duration" in the UI (this appears to be the ceiling of `total_run_time`). ### Version info ```Text Version: 3.4.12 API version: 0.8.4 Python version: 3.12.9 Git commit: 35e04f52 Built: Fri, Aug 08, 2025 10:41 PM OS/Arch: darwin/arm64 Profile: sbx-rowdy-cloud Server type: cloud Pydantic version: 2.11.7 Integrations: prefect-gitlab: 0.3.1 prefect-databricks: 0.3.0 prefect-github: 0.3.1 prefect-snowflake: 0.28.3 ``` ### Additional context Screenshot for reference Since task runs are orchestrated client-side in Prefect 3, this may be something we're missing?

PrefectHQ/prefect#18753.body:
## context

prefect's background services can run separately from the main api server (via `prefect server services start`). this is great for deployment flexibility, especially in containerized environments like kubernetes and docker compose.

however, there's a critical gap: **not all background services are safe to run with multiple replicas**, which users with high availability (HA) deployments reasonably want.

## current state

the background services include:
- `CancellationCleanup`
- `Foreman` 
- `MarkLateRuns`
- `FailExpiredPauses`
- `Repossessor`
- `RecentDeploymentsScheduler`
- `Scheduler`
- `TaskRunRecorder`
- `Telemetry`
- `ProactiveTriggers`
- `ReactiveTriggers`
- `Actions`


## problems

1. **undocumented safety**: users don't know which services can be horizontally scaled
2. **all-or-nothing deployment**: `prefect server services start` runs everything together, preventing selective scaling
3. **blocked ha deployments**: helm charts and kubernetes deployments are stuck at `replicas: 1`

## going forward

0. **audit all services**

1. **document service scalability**: create clear documentation showing which services are:
   - safe to scale horizontally (stateless, idempotent)
   - unsafe to scale (require singleton execution)
   - conditionally safe (e.g., with redis messaging/locking)

2. (if necessary) **separate cli entrypoints**: expose different service groups:
   ```bash
   prefect server services start --safe-only     # only horizontally scalable services
   prefect server services start --singleton-only # services requiring single instance
   ```

3. **improve service architecture**: investigate converting unsafe services to be horizontally scalable where possible

## acceptance criteria

- [ ] documentation clearly identifies each service's scalability characteristics
- [ ] users can deploy ha-safe services with multiple replicas
- [ ] helm charts can properly configure replicas based on service safety
- [ ] migration path exists for making more services horizontally scalable

## related

- prefecthq/prefect-helm#523 (redis messaging support)
- prefecthq/prefect-helm#512 (distributed deployment architecture)
- https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/discussions/18150
PrefectHQ/prefect#18753.title: document and improve horizontal scalability of background services

PrefectHQ/prefect#18796.body:
### Bug summary

**Bug Description**

When starting a Prefect worker for an `ecs` work pool, the process is stuck after discovering the work pool type. This occurs specifically with `prefect-aws` versions `0.5.14` and `0.5.15`. The last known working version is `0.5.13`.

The workers, also running on ECS, use a startup command that installs the latest version of prefect-aws: `pip install 'prefect[aws]' && prefect worker start --pool ecs-fargate-spot`. After a server restart today, this command pulled version 0.5.15 and the worker process now hangs indefinitely after logging  `Discovered type 'ecs' for work pool ...`

The worker successfully connects to the Prefect API and identifies the work pool, but fails to proceed with its own initialization and never starts polling for runs. No errors or further logs are produced, even with `PREFECT_LOGGING_LEVEL=DEBUG`.

The current workaround is to explicitly pin the dependency to the last known working version 0.5.13


PrefectHQ/prefect#18799.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Currently for scaling prefect server, the [documentation](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/advanced/self-hosted) outlines scaling using multiple instances of the prefect server. This requires other tools such as kubernetes and docker compose with a load balancer in order to orchestrate the workers. This makes the deployment and setup slightly more complex in order to run multiple instances. 

### Describe the proposed behavior

For a more simplified approach I want to be able to run a multi-worker server by adding an optional `--workers <N>` flag to `prefect server start` to automatically launch one or more Prefect workers alongside the server process.

I believe that prefect uses fastapi under the hood. fastapi has [multi-worker](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/deployment/server-workers/) already so integration shouldn't be difficult 

### Example Use

```bash
prefect server start --workers 3
```

### Additional context

This makes scaling slightly easier to setup where users would just need to modify the command and they will have "automatic" balancing. The client would just need to talk to one port and it allows us to use multiple CPU cores for scaling.
PrefectHQ/prefect#18799.title: Vertical scale prefect server with workers

PrefectHQ/prefect#18835.body:
### Bug summary

### Context
I am using Prefect OSS on Kubernetes and want to have consistent Kubernetes pod labels as I specify them but Prefect does not set this consistently and also lowercases them by default.

### Details
In my prefect server and prefect worker I have specified the following attributes for setting pod labels: `commonLabels`, `server.podLabels` and `worker.podLabels` based on [this](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect-helm/blob/main/charts/prefect-server/values.yaml#L8-L9), [this](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect-helm/blob/main/charts/prefect-server/values.yaml#L186-L187
), [this ](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect-helm/blob/main/charts/prefect-worker/values.yaml#L8-L9
) and [this ](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect-helm/blob/main/charts/prefect-worker/values.yaml#L267-L268
) spec


Example in Helm chart here
```
prefect-worker:
  commonLabels:
    mylabel: "LABEL"
  worker:
    podLabels:
      mylabel: "LABEL"
```
and my **server and worker pod labels** show the label as expected ✅

```
Labels:  
  mylabel=LABEL
  app.kubernetes.io/component=server
  app.kubernetes.io/instance=prefect
  app.kubernetes.io/managed-by=Helm
  app.kubernetes.io/name=prefect-server
  app.kubernetes.io/version=3-latest
  helm.sh/chart=prefect-server-0.0.0
  pod-template-hash=67885796c5
  prefect-version=3-latest
```

Now I want to apply the same label on all my **job pods** so I have tried 2 ways to do this with the same (unexpected) result.

Adding the label in the updated [baseJobTemplate ](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect-helm/blob/main/charts/prefect-worker/values.yaml#L123-L140) in the Helm chart like

```
  config:
      workPool: "my-pool"
      baseJobTemplate:
        configuration: |
          {
            "variables": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
				....
                "labels": {
                  "type": "object",
                  "title": "Labels",
                  "description": "Labels applied to infrastructure created by a worker.",
                  "additionalProperties": {
                    "type": "string"
                  },
                  "default": {
                    "mylabel": "LABEL"
                  }
                },
			....
			}
```
I also tried to add it via code in the deployment with the `job_variables` option based on [this](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/how-to-guides/deployments/customize-job-variables#use-the-deploy-method)

```
my_flow.to_deployment(
        name=name,
        description=description,
        job_variables={
            "labels": {"mylabel": "LABEL"}
        }
    )
```

In both these cases the **job pod shows the label in lowercase** ❌

```
Labels:           
  mylabel=label
  batch.kubernetes.io/controller-uid=625464c8-b1b0-404e-801c-53f776506ecd
  batch.kubernetes.io/job-name=omega4-mensae-m-mq7qn
  controller-uid=625464c8-b1b0-404e-801c-53f776506ecd
  job-name=omega4-mensae-m-mq7qn
  prefect.io/deployment-id=9eb36c36-3899-41b1-ac6e-6e5437e41451
  prefect.io/deployment-name=my_deployment
  prefect.io/deployment-updated=2025-09-02t10-56-25.204490-00-00
  prefect.io/flow-id=2213482b-95bc-4753-b876-5acb84f54423
  prefect.io/flow-name=sap-odp
  prefect.io/flow-run-id=b2e57cd5-989e-498b-bc08-3400abcd4533
  prefect.io/flow-run-name=omega4-mensae-m
  prefect.io/version=3.4.4
  prefect.io/work-pool-id=5b43d5f3-0b20-4981-8c74-aa591445436
  prefect.io/work-pool-name=kubernetes-pool
  prefect.io/worker-name=kubernetesworker-56665e37-668a-44bf-bcb8-d27563435
```
### Issues reported:
- the `commonLabels` attribute didn't seem to apply any labels to the pods so the `podLabels` was needed for prefect server and prefect worker
- the label in the job pods is being lowercases by default (which is not desired if my label is uppercase) and creates an inconsistency with the server, worker pods

### Version info

```Text
Version:             3.4.4
API version:         0.8.4
Python version:      3.12.10
Git commit:          0367d7aa
Built:               Thu, May 29, 2025 09:40 PM
OS/Arch:             linux/x86_64
Profile:             ephemeral
Server type:         ephemeral
Pydantic version:    2.11.5
Server:
  Database:          postgresql
Integrations:
  prefect-kubernetes: 0.6.1
  prefect-redis:     0.2.2
```

### Additional context

_No response_

I experience zombie pods on Kubernetes that idle around after the flow has been marked Crashed due to a failed lease renewal. I believe the flow handler is not exiting properly in this case and is stuck. Unfortunately, I don't have a reliable reproduction script yet. I guess this case could also be handled the kopf observer in the worker

PrefectHQ/prefect#18867.body:
### Describe the current behavior

When a job runs through Kubernetes with a backoff limit of 0 we automatically reschedule the flow run based on the value of this env variable `PREFECT_FLOW_RUN_EXECUTE_SIGTERM_BEHAVIOR`

This is set automatically if the backoff_limit is 0 here even if the value is set as part of the job configuration through the work pool or job variables on a deployment. https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/1ff07e6653f35501c20963eb2bec5f23d7773bb0/src/integrations/prefect-kubernetes/prefect_kubernetes/worker.py#L430-L441



### Describe the proposed behavior

Ideally a user would be able to set `PREFECT_FLOW_RUN_EXECUTE_SIGTERM_BEHAVIOR` to some value other than reschedule to opt out of this behavior.

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#18867.title: Allow users to opt-out of SIGTERM rescheduling on Kubernetes worker

### Bug summary

I understand that this ticket is a near-duplicate of several others that report `sqlite3.OperationalError`. I also understand that seeing this error does (in most cases?) not impact Prefect's functioning. However, I am making this ticket because I observe this even when running very basic examples from the documentation. For example, I just tried out this very straightforward code snippet from [here](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/how-to-guides/workflows/write-and-run#create-tasks-and-child-workflows):

```py
import random

from prefect import flow, task


@task
def generate_a_number():
    return random.randint(0, 100)


@flow
def is_number_even(number: int):
    return number % 2 == 0


@flow
def even_or_odd():
    number = generate_a_number()
    if is_number_even(number):
        print("The number is even")
    else:
        print("The number is odd")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    even_or_odd()
```

I read in other issues that this is not a problem and Prefect will retry. Then why doesn't Prefect swallow this exception instead of very verbosely logging it at ERROR level? From my perspective as a relatively experienced Pythonista who wanted to give Prefect a try, this is just one of the many erors and confusions that I am drowning in after a few hours of trying to follow the docs. I haven't gotten to the bottom of all the other problems I am running into, but this seems like an unnecessary "sharp edge" in the onboarding journey that should be relatively easy to smoothen.

PrefectHQ/prefect#19007.body:
### Bug summary

### Description
When using the latest Prefect versions (`>=3.4.18`) our deployments that don't have a specified schedule fail to deploy via Github Actions.

### Observed Behavior
We have a `prefect.yaml` that defines many deployments which have been unchanged for a few months, defined as:
```
- name: deployment-name-1
  tags: *common_tags
  schedule: {}
  parameters: {}
  entrypoint: path/to/flow.py:flow_name
  work_pool: our-work-pool
  build: *build_steps
- name: deployment-name-2
  tags: *common_tags
  schedule: "0 4 * * *"
  parameters: {}
  entrypoint: path/to/other_flow.py:other_flow_name
  work_pool: our-work-pool
  build: *build_steps
```

When running the Prefect deploy action (`PrefectHQ/actions-prefect-deploy`) the `deployment-name-2` that has a schedule provided as a CRON string will successfully deploy, but `deployment-name-1` that isn't scheduled will fail with the following error message:

```
The specified config file contains invalid fields. Validation error: Input 
should be a valid dictionary or instance of RawScheduleConfig
```

### Version info

```Text
prefect: 3.4.19
actions-prefect-deploy: v4.2.0
```

### Additional context

- This may be a separate issue, but the first line of the `actions-prefect-deploy` `prefect-deploy` step is `pip install prefect -U "prefect>=3,<4"` which will always install the latest prefect version >=3. So even if I specify another prefect version in my lock file e.g. `prefect==3.4.10` the deployment action will use a more recent version of prefect if it exists, so I can't avoid this bug in my deployments by using an older version of prefect.
- We have also tried leaving the `schedule: ` empty and omitted the `schedule` field entirely, but we are seeing the same error message.
- We have gone through the documentation on schedules [here](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/concepts/schedules) and [here](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/how-to-guides/deployments/create-schedules), but didn't see an example for how to define an unscheduled deployment in `prefect.yaml` that we haven't attempted.
- We can deploy via CLI since it prompts for "do you want to add a schedule (y/n)", and selecting "no" allows the deployment to continue, but that isn't a scalable solution and we need this to work through Github Actions.
PrefectHQ/prefect#19007.title: Creating Unscheduled Deployments

PrefectHQ/prefect#19026.body: ### Bug summary We are observing in our GCP environment that starting more than 3.5K deployed flows causes a huge spike in the prefect worker CPU. At which point, it starts throwing errors and crashing repeatedly, leaving flows stuck in a pending state (without submitting them to GKE) effectively turning them into zombie flows. We are surmising that the large number of k8s events generated by the large amount of pods is overwhelming the prefect worker. A workaround that we are currently pursuing is to disable the kopf observer for all workers and starting another secondary worker that points to an empty work pool and purely processes k8s events. We would like to avoid this as a long term solution as the single observer worker will continue to have this problem. It would be nice to be able to parallelize kopf observer across all events somehow. ``` Handler '_replicate_pod_event' failed with an exception. Will ignore. #18004 introduced a new Kubernetes worker that uses [kopf](https://github.com/nolar/kopf) to observe flow runs and forward their events to the Prefect server. When using a `json` formatter for Prefect's console logging handler, `kopf` attempts to log when its configured handlers complete (such as [_mark_flow_run_as_crashed](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/integrations/prefect-kubernetes/prefect_kubernetes/observer.py#L239)). A `ThreadPoolExecutor` is passed into the logging call, raising a TypeError. This happens immediately on worker start-up if any jobs matching with the label `prefect.io/flow-run-id` are present in the namespace, and upon completion of flow runs the worker is observing.

To reproduce:
Set `PREFECT_LOGGING_HANDLERS_CONSOLE_FORMATTER` to `json` as an env var on a `prefect-kubernetes` worker and kick off a flow run. On completion, the worker will raise the `TypeError`.

PrefectHQ/prefect#19320.body:
### Bug summary

## Summary

The Prefect ECS worker fails to pass the `cluster` and `launchType` parameters to AWS ECS `run_task` API calls, despite these being correctly configured in the work pool. This causes flows to fail with cryptic errors like "cluster 'default' not found" or "Assign public IP is not supported for this launch type."

## Environment

- **Prefect Version**: 3.5.0
- **prefect-aws Version**: 0.6.0 (also confirmed in 0.5.13)
- **Python Version**: 3.13
- **Prefect Deployment**: Self-hosted server at custom URL
- **AWS Region**: eu-central-1
- **Launch Type**: FARGATE

## Bug Description

When the ECS worker submits a flow run to AWS ECS, it seems as if it fails to include critical parameters in the `run_task` API call:

1. **Missing `cluster` parameter**: Despite configuring the cluster ARN in the work pool, the worker doesn't pass it to AWS. The worker falls back to a hardcoded `"default"` cluster name.
2. **Missing `launchType` parameter**: Despite configuring `launch_type: "FARGATE"` in the work pool, this parameter is not included in the API call, causing AWS to potentially default to EC2 launch type.

## Steps to Reproduce

### 1. Create ECS Cluster
```bash
aws ecs create-cluster --cluster-name my-prefect-cluster --region eu-central-1
```

### 2. Configure Work Pool with Cluster ARN
```bash
# Create ECS work pool
prefect work-pool create my-ecs-pool --type ecs

# Update work pool with cluster ARN
cat > work-pool-config.json <<EOF
{
  "variables": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "cluster": {
        "type": "string",
        "default": "arn:aws:ecs:eu-central-1:123456789012:cluster/my-prefect-cluster"
      },
      "launch_type": {
        "type": "string",
        "default": "FARGATE"
      }
    }
  },
  "job_configuration": {
    "cluster": "{{ cluster }}",
    "launch_type": "{{ launch_type }}"
  }
}
EOF

prefect work-pool update --base-job-template work-pool-config.json my-ecs-pool
```

### 3. Start Worker and Deploy Flow
```bash
# Start worker
prefect worker start --pool my-ecs-pool &

# Deploy and run a simple flow
cat > test_flow.py <<EOF
from prefect import flow

@flow
def hello():
    print("Hello World")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    hello.deploy(
        name="test-deployment",
        work_pool_name="my-ecs-pool",
        image="prefecthq/prefect:3-latest",
        push=False,
        build=False
    )
EOF

python test_flow.py
prefect deployment run 'hello/test-deployment'
```

### 4. Observe the Error

Check the worker logs with debug output enabled:
```bash
PREFECT_LOGGING_LEVEL=DEBUG prefect worker start --pool my-ecs-pool
```

You'll see in the debug logs that the `run_task` API call is missing the `cluster` and `launchType` parameters:

```json
{
  "taskDefinition": "arn:aws:ecs:...",
  "networkConfiguration": { ... },
  "tags": [ ... ]
  // ❌ Missing: "cluster": "arn:aws:ecs:eu-central-1:123456789012:cluster/my-prefect-cluster"
  // ❌ Missing: "launchType": "FARGATE"
}
```

## Expected Behavior

The worker should include **both** parameters in the `run_task` API call:

```json
{
  "cluster": "arn:aws:ecs:eu-central-1:123456789012:cluster/my-prefect-cluster",
  "launchType": "FARGATE",
  "taskDefinition": "arn:aws:ecs:...",
  "networkConfiguration": { ... },
  "tags": [ ... ]
}
```

## Actual Behavior

The `run_task` call omits these parameters, resulting in:

1. **Cluster Error**: 
   ```
   RuntimeError: Failed to run ECS task, cluster 'default' not found. 
   Confirm that the cluster is configured in your region.
   ```

2. **Launch Type Error** (when cluster is named "default" as workaround):
   ```
   InvalidParameterException: An error occurred (InvalidParameterException) 
   when calling the RunTask operation: Assign public IP is not supported 
   for this launch type.
   ```
   
   This occurs because without specifying `launchType: FARGATE`, AWS may default to EC2 launch type, which doesn't support the `assignPublicIp: ENABLED` setting used with awsvpc network mode.

## Code Location

Based on the stack traces, the issue appears to be in:
- `prefect_aws/workers/ecs_worker.py` line ~1793 in `_create_task_run()`
- The method that builds the `run_task` kwargs doesn't properly include the `cluster` and `launchType` from the job configuration

## Workarounds

### Workaround 1: Name cluster "default"
If you name your ECS cluster exactly `"default"`, the hardcoded fallback will match:
```bash
aws ecs create-cluster --cluster-name default
```

This fixes the cluster error but doesn't resolve the missing `launchType` parameter.

### Workaround 2: Use private subnets
If you can't fix the `launchType` issue and are getting the "Assign public IP not supported" error, use private subnets with NAT gateway and set `assignPublicIp: DISABLED`.

## Impact

This bug makes it impossible to:
- Use ECS clusters with custom names
- Reliably use FARGATE launch type with public IP assignment
- Follow AWS best practices for resource naming and organization

## Additional Context

- This issue was confirmed in both `prefect-aws==0.5.13` and `prefect-aws==0.6.0`
- The work pool configuration correctly stores both the cluster ARN and launch type
- The task definition correctly includes `requiresCompatibilities: ["FARGATE"]`
- The issue is specifically in how the worker constructs the `run_task` API call parameters

## Proposed Fix

In `prefect_aws/workers/ecs_worker.py`, the `_create_task_run()` method should:

1. Extract the `cluster` value from the resolved job configuration
2. Extract the `launch_type` value from the resolved job configuration  
3. Include both in the kwargs passed to `ecs_client.run_task()`

Example pseudo-code:
```python
run_task_kwargs = {
    "taskDefinition": task_definition_arn,
    "networkConfiguration": network_configuration,
}

# Add cluster if configured
if cluster := configuration.get("cluster"):
    run_task_kwargs["cluster"] = cluster

# Add launchType if configured
if launch_type := configuration.get("launch_type"):
    run_task_kwargs["launchType"] = launch_type

# ... rest of the parameters

response = ecs_client.run_task(**run_task_kwargs)
```

## Related Issues

This may be related to or duplicate of any existing issues regarding ECS worker parameter passing or cluster configuration.


### Version info

```Text
Version:              3.5.0
API version:          0.8.4
Python version:       3.13.9
Git commit:           7d86c932
Built:                Fri, Oct 31, 2025 04:59 PM
OS/Arch:              darwin/arm64
Profile:              ephemeral
Server type:          server
Pydantic version:     2.12.3
Server:
  Database:           sqlite
  SQLite version:     3.50.4
Integrations:
  prefect-aws:        0.6.0
```

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#19320.title: ECS Worker Missing Required Parameters in run_task API Call

PrefectHQ/prefect#19410.body: ### Describe the current behavior Prefect Cloud recently added a concurrency grace period field on the edit deployment page to configure the initial lease duration for a flow run when it's "Pending" but has not had its infra spun up and transitioned to "Running" yet. This is useful when you anticipate that your infrastructure will take a lengthy amount of time to start the flow run (e.g, more than the previous 5/10 minute defaults, which caused crashes in some cases where the run would then start, but try to grab the concurrency lease and fail to since it had already expired). ### Describe the proposed behavior Add support for this to the OSS server and client, allowing users to configure the grace period on self-hosted Prefect Servers and set it client-side when using Prefect Cloud. ### Example Use sample usage in a prefect.yaml file: ```yaml deployments: - name: business_critical_deployment entrypoint: business_critical_deployment.py:business_critical_flow work_pool: name: process-workpool concurrency_limit: collision_strategy: "ENQUEUE" limit: 1 grace_period_seconds: 600 ``` ### Additional context Current usage today
PrefectHQ/prefect#19410.title: Add support for concurrency grace period

PrefectHQ/prefect#19469.body:
### Bug summary

When calling `@task` decorated async functions from within an `@flow` decorated async function with
proper `await` keywords, mypy 1.18.2 incorrectly reports `unused-coroutine` errors. The code runs
correctly at runtime, but mypy's type checking fails because it doesn't understand that Prefect's
`@task` decorator transforms the function signature.

Mypy reports errors like:
```
error: Value of type "Coroutine[Any, Any, dict[str, str]]" must be used  [unused-coroutine]
note: Are you missing an await?
```

Even though the `await` keyword is present and the code executes correctly.

## Minimal Reproducible Example

```python
"""Minimal reproducible example for Prefect mypy type checking issue."""

import prefect
from prefect import flow, task


@task
async def async_task_with_return() -> dict[str, str]:
    """An async task that returns a value."""
    return {"status": "completed"}


@task
async def async_task_no_return() -> None:
    """An async task that returns None."""
    print("Task executed")


@flow
async def example_flow() -> None:
    """Flow that calls async tasks."""
    # This produces mypy error: Value of type "Coroutine[Any, Any, dict[str, str]]" must be used
    await async_task_with_return()

    # This produces mypy error: Value of type "Coroutine[Any, Any, None]" must be used
    await async_task_no_return()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import asyncio
    asyncio.run(example_flow())
```

## mypy Output

```
test_prefect_mypy_issue.py:23: error: Value of type "Coroutine[Any, Any, dict[str, str]]" must be used  [unused-coroutine]
test_prefect_mypy_issue.py:23: note: Are you missing an await?
test_prefect_mypy_issue.py:26: error: Value of type "Coroutine[Any, Any, None]" must be used  [unused-coroutine]
test_prefect_mypy_issue.py:26: note: Are you missing an await?
Found 2 errors in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```

## Expected Behavior

mypy should recognize that:
1. The `await` keyword is correctly used
2. The `@task` decorator properly handles async function execution within flows
3. No type checking errors should be reported for this valid Prefect code pattern

## Current Workaround

The current workaround is to:
1. Assign the return value to a variable (even if unused): `_ = await async_task_with_return()`
2. Add type ignore comments: `await async_task()  # type: ignore[unused-coroutine]`
3. Add explicit type annotations with ignore: `result: dict[str, str] = await async_task()  # type: ignore[assignment]`

### Version info

```Text
- **Prefect Version**: 3.6.2
- **Python Version**: 3.11
- **mypy Version**: 1.18.2 (latest stable as of January 2025)
- **Operating System**: Linux (Docker container)
```

### Additional context

This issue affects codebases using mypy for type checking with Prefect 3.6.* async flows and tasks. It appears that mypy doesn't have proper type stubs or understanding of how the `@task` decorator transforms function signatures when called from within a `@flow` context.

**Note on mypy version**: This issue persists on mypy 1.18.2 (the latest stable release as of January 2025). A review of the mypy changelog from versions 1.15-1.18 shows no specific fixes for `unused-coroutine` errors with decorators. This is not a mypy bug - mypy is correctly analyzing the code based on the type information it has. The root cause is that mypy doesn't understand how Prefect's `@task` decorator transforms the function's calling behavior at runtime.

**The solution needs to come from Prefect** through one of these approaches:
1. **Enhanced type stubs** - Update Prefect's type annotations to properly describe the `@task` decorator's behavior when called from flows
2. **mypy plugin** - Create a mypy plugin to handle Prefect's decorator transformations (similar to what Django, SQLAlchemy, and other frameworks provide)
3. **Advanced decorator typing** - Use `typing.overload`, `typing.ParamSpec`, or protocols to better describe how the decorator modifies the callable

## Related Issues

This is similar to how other frameworks with decorator-based transformation (like pytest fixtures, FastAPI dependencies) needed special mypy plugins or enhanced type stubs to work correctly with type checkers. For example:
- Django has django-stubs with a mypy plugin
- SQLAlchemy provides sqlalchemy2-stubs
- pytest has type stubs that understand fixture decorators

Prefect would benefit from a similar solution to provide proper type checking support for its decorator-based API.
PrefectHQ/prefect#19469.title: mypy incorrectly reports "unused-coroutine" errors for @task decorated functions called from @flow

PrefectHQ/prefect#19483.body:
several CLI commands produce table or list output but don't support `--output json` / `-o json` for machine-readable output. the pattern already exists in commands like `flow-run ls`, `deployment ls`, `artifact ls`, and `variable ls` — each gap just needs to follow the same approach.

## how to contribute

pick an unchecked command from the list below, open a PR, and reference this issue (e.g. "related to #19483"). no need to create a child issue — just claim it in a comment here and open the PR.

the existing implementations in `flow-run ls` and `deployment ls` are good references for the pattern:
- add `--output` / `-o` option (accepts `"json"`)
- when `output == "json"`, serialize the response objects with `orjson.dumps(..., option=orjson.OPT_INDENT_2)` and print
- when not set, render the existing Rich table as before
- add tests for both output modes

## already done

- [x] `flow-run ls` — #19484
- [x] `flow-run inspect`
- [x] `block ls` — #20195
- [x] `block type ls` — #20196
- [x] `deployment ls` — #20198
- [x] `deployment ls`
- [x] `deployment inspect`
- [x] `deployment schedule ls` — #20197

- [x] `work-pool preview` — #20986

PrefectHQ/prefect#19510.body:
### Describe the current behavior

There is no client-side mechanism to alert when an event sent to a Prefect server exceeds the maximum supported size. While the OSS server enforces a [PREFECT_EVENTS_MAXIMUM_SIZE_BYTES](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/d3c3ed500fdd1c48eef83269cd75327e3aacad0b/src/prefect/server/events/messaging.py#L52), this does not apply to the client, which can cause silent failures in systems that rely on events (automations and assets).

### Describe the proposed behavior

Log a warning when the events client tries to write an event that is too large - the same for assets.

### Example Use

_No response_

PrefectHQ/prefect#19544.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Per https://redis.io/docs/latest/commands/xreadgroup/, count configures how many messages are read via `xreadgroup`. This is hard-coded to 1 right now. Depending on client use cases, and to facilitate e.g. https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/discussions/18150 via https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/18753, reading more than one message at a time is desirable.

### Describe the proposed behavior

Parametrize the current hardcoded value of 1 for `xreadgroup` in `Consumer`, and add a setting for it for `EventPersister` service.

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#19544.title: Consumers should be able to parametrize xreadgroup batch size

Instead of the `prefect-worker` waiting on `postgres` and `redis` services, it can be made to wait on the `prefect-services` via API healthcheck

`prefect-server` also waits on `postgres` and `redis`, and can be made to wait on `prefect-services` as well

`prefect-worker` can then use the `service_healthy` rather than `service_started`, which will allow it to properly start up in correct sequence


PrefectHQ/prefect#19581.body:
### Bug summary

After upgrading from Prefect 2.12.20 to 3.4.19, we noticed that our jobs that used to map are taking much longer to run than they used to. After reviewing a MRE with Nate, I've created a small MRE repo with Github actions that show that tasks take 2x as long to be created on Windows than Linux. This is affecting our business as we use Prefect to pull out data from our accounting software on time and processes are running 2-5 times slower than they used to.

MRE Github repo and actions can be found here: https://github.com/Octacon100/mre_slow_windows_mapping_prefect/actions/runs/19706302931/job/56454673317


### Version info

```Text
Prefect version is 3.4.19 to 3.6.4. Seems to happen no matter which version is used.
```

### Additional context

It's tough to explain to my bosses and the investment research team that an upgrade is making things slower. One research team member really wants to use airflow so this is making it hard for me to stop him from changing everything.
PrefectHQ/prefect#19581.title: Prefect is 2x slower to create tasks when mapping or submitting on Windows than Linux.

Currently, EventBeingProcessed is defined in prefect redis as well as in server.events.ordering.memory. This can be unified.

### Describe the proposed behavior

Unify the EventBeingProcessed type between the two classes.

PrefectHQ/prefect#19596.body:
### Bug summary

Triggering a dbt cloud job using the async def [trigger_dbt_cloud_job_run_and_wait_for_completion](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/26aa8c69a0e5288a78ff5a1a01efad28b7ea7bc1/src/integrations/prefect-dbt/prefect_dbt/cloud/jobs.py#L249) function utilizes cached results on the [get_dbt_cloud_run_info](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/26aa8c69a0e5288a78ff5a1a01efad28b7ea7bc1/src/integrations/prefect-dbt/prefect_dbt/cloud/runs.py#L279) task when retrieving job information after it's initially created so the job status never successfully updates, adjusting the code here to disable caching on the task allows the run information to update successfully 

i.e.
```python
        run_data_future = await get_dbt_cloud_run_info.with_options(cache_policy=NO_CACHE)(
            dbt_cloud_credentials=dbt_cloud_credentials,
            run_id=run_id,
            wait_for=wait_for,
        )
```


MRE
```python
import asyncio

from prefect import flow
from prefect.variables import Variable
from prefect_dbt.cloud import DbtCloudCredentials
from prefect_dbt.cloud.jobs import (
    trigger_dbt_cloud_job_run_and_wait_for_completion,
)


DBT_TEST_JOB_ID = Variable.get("dbt_test_job_id")
DBT_CLOUD_CREDS = DbtCloudCredentials.load("dbt-creds")

@flow
async def trigger_dbt_cloud_job_ex() -> None:
    await trigger_dbt_cloud_job_run_and_wait_for_completion(
        dbt_cloud_credentials = DBT_CLOUD_CREDS,
        job_id = DBT_TEST_JOB_ID,
        )

# Trigger the Prefect flow
if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(trigger_dbt_cloud_job_ex())
```
