PrefectHQ/prefect#19902.body:
### Bug summary

We are running Prefect on GKE (server, worker, and flow runs). We periodically see this exception that the websocket handshake timed out. This fails the entire pod and flow on startup. We have automatic job retries enabled but sometimes this happens multiple times in a row and fails our flow runs entirely. Otherwise it is quite noisy for our alerting.

I dug around in the code and see two paths forward (or both), which I would be happy to implement:
1. allow the event subscriber multiple attempts at reconnection
2. expose more prefect settings for the websocket connection (i.e open timeout, connection timeout, reconnection limit etc)
3. falling back to the polling on failure to connect to the websocket on the first attempt

PrefectHQ/prefect#19961.body:
### Describe the current behavior

### Problem

`PrefectDbtRunner` builds a Prefect DAG from dbt execution events.
dbt emits these events for all nodes, including `ephemeral` models.

In dbt, `ephemeral` models are inlined into downstream SQL and are not executed or materialized as standalone nodes.
However, Prefect currently treats them as real tasks, which leads to:

* `ephemeral` models appearing in the DAG
* downstream models losing their true upstream dependencies
* incorrect asset lineage

---
### Example

dbt logical graph:

```
model_a ──▶ ephemeral_x ──▶ model_b
```

Expected:

```
model_a ──▶ model_b
```
---
Current Prefect DAG:

* `ephemeral_x` appears as a task
* `model_b` has no dependency on `model_a`


### Describe the proposed behavior

### Solution

To make `ephemeral` models transparent in `PrefectDbtRunner`:

* No Prefect tasks are created for `ephemeral` models
* When a node depends on an `ephemeral` model, its upstream dependencies are recursively propagated

This removes `ephemeral` nodes from the DAG and restores correct lineage, matching dbt’s execution model.

P.S. I’m creating a PR for this change and will post it shortly. I have tested it in my dbt project but can’t share results due to confidential data.

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#19961.title: Incorrect handling of dbt ephemeral models in PrefectDbtRunner

PrefectHQ/prefect#20199.body: ### Problem description PrefectDbtRunner creates Prefect task runs too early based on dbt’s `NodeStart` events, which can be emitted before dbt has determined whether a node will actually run or be skipped due to upstream failures. In dbt, it is valid for a downstream node to emit `NodeStart` even if an upstream dependency later fails. When that happens, dbt will eventually mark the downstream node as skipped and emit a `NodeFinished` event for it. PrefectDbtRunner tracks skipped nodes and intentionally ignores `NodeFinished` events for any node recorded as skipped. This creates the following problematic sequence: 1. dbt emits `NodeStart` for a node → Prefect creates a task run. 2. An upstream dependency fails → dbt marks the node as skipped. 3. dbt emits `NodeFinished` for the skipped node. 4. Prefect ignores this `NodeFinished` event because the node is in `_skipped_nodes`. As a result, the Prefect task run that was created at `NodeStart` is never completed, failed, or marked as skipped. It remains in a running (“zombie”) state indefinitely. The root cause is that `NodeStart` is emitted before dbt has finalized dependency resolution, while Prefect’s skip-handling logic later suppresses the corresponding `NodeFinished` event. This combination leads to task runs being created for nodes that will never meaningfully execute and will never be properly closed. ### Version info ```Text prefect-dbt: 0.7.14 prefect: 3.6.8 dbt-core: 1.10.17 ```
PrefectHQ/prefect#20199.title: Using NodeStart leads to zombie Prefect tasks when dbt skips nodes

PrefectHQ/prefect#20236.body:
### Bug summary

at https://docs.prefect.io/v3/concepts/schedules#interval it shows a code example

```yaml
schedule:
  interval: 600
  timezone: America/Chicago
```

and the documentation for the interval property says "datetime.timedelta indicating the time between flow runs. (Required)"

In this case, there is no documentation that this value is in seconds. It seems that the timedelta option is for creating this schedule via code, not via yaml. These two items should be clarified.

Additionally, [I asked Marvin about this](https://prefect-community.slack.com/archives/C04DZJC94DC/p1767641937001269) and it suggested that intervals can also be a ISO 8601 duration string, which from my understanding is completely wrong and cannot be used.


PrefectHQ/prefect#20652.body:
## Current Behavior

Setting `PREFECT_DEBUG_MODE` on workers causes all flow runs executed by those workers to also log at debug level.

## Desired Behavior

Provide a way to enable debug mode for workers independently from flow runs, so workers can run in debug mode without forcing debug logging on all flows they execute.

## Workaround

Currently, setting `PREFECT_DEBUG_MODE=false` in the work pool's environment variables overrides the worker setting and prevents flow runs from inheriting debug mode. However, this requires manual configuration and isn't intuitive.
PrefectHQ/prefect#20652.title: Allow separate debug mode configuration for workers vs flow runs

PrefectHQ/prefect#20690.body:
### Bug summary

  When Redis stream pending IDs exist but the underlying stream entry is missing, Prefect's Redis  consumer can receive a claimed message with `message=None`. The consumer then crashes with  `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable`, and reactive automations stop being evaluated.

  Observed behavior
  1. `process_pending_messages` calls `_handle_message` for each claimed message without checking if  `message` is `None`.
  2. `_handle_message` immediately accesses `message["data"]` and `message["attributes"]`.
  3. This raises `TypeError` for orphan pending entries.
  4. `Consumer.run` only catches `RedisError`, so this non-Redis exception escapes and kills the  consumer loop.
  5. `ReactiveTriggers` then stops processing events, so automations (like heartbeat-based crash  detection) do not fire.

  Expected behavior
  - Orphan pending entries should be handled safely (e.g., skip + ACK or skip + log).
  - Consumer loop should not permanently die on non-Redis message-shape errors.

  Impact
  Reactive event automations can silently stop working until the service/pod is restarted and/or  pending entries are manually cleaned.

  Affected files
  - `src/integrations/prefect-redis/prefect_redis/messaging.py`
    - `process_pending_messages` (no None guard before `_handle_message`)
    - `_handle_message` (direct `message["data"]` / `message["attributes"]`)
    - `run` (only `except RedisError`)
  - `src/prefect/server/events/services/triggers.py`
    - `ReactiveTriggers.start` awaits consumer task; if consumer exits on uncaught exception,  reactive trigger processing is effectively down.

  Suggested fix
  1. In `process_pending_messages`, guard against `message is None`; ACK orphan IDs (or explicitly drop) and continue.
  2. In `run`, catch broader exceptions around message handling loop, log, and continue/restart safely.
  3. Add an integration test for orphan pending entries returned via `XAUTOCLAIM`.

  File addresses (GitHub):

  - https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/integrations/prefect-redis/prefect_redis/messaging.py#L363
  - https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/integrations/prefect-redis/prefect_redis/messaging.py#L462
  - https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/integrations/prefect-redis/prefect_redis/messaging.py#L448
  - https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/prefect/server/events/services/triggers.py#L35

  File addresses (runtime path in your pod):


### Version info

```Text
3.6.17
```

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#20690.title: ReactiveTriggers can die on orphan Redis pending entries (None message) and stop all automation evaluation

PrefectHQ/prefect#20742.body:
### Bug summary

If one is using a UNIX Domain socket setting to connect to the Postgres server (same host), the
async listener appears to expect a TCP style path. For security reasons, we prefer to use a UNIX
domain socket. The DB connection works (were able to migrate the db), but the listener fails
to connect and eventually the server exits out. Using TCP setting works around the problem,
but (for the above reason) is not desirable.

Here is an example setting using a UNIX domain socket 

 PREFECT_API_DATABASE_CONNECTION_URL="postgresql+asyncpg:///prefect?host=/tmp/.SOSHUB&port=25432"

PrefectHQ/prefect#20747.body:
## Summary

The `CustomWebhookNotificationBlock` in `prefect/blocks/notifications.py` does not call `validate_restricted_url()` before making HTTP requests to user-configured webhook URLs. This contrasts with the standard `Webhook` block which includes `allow_private_urls` protection.

## Affected Code

**Missing protection:** `prefect/blocks/notifications.py` lines 973-983

```python
async def anotify(self, body, subject=None):
    request_args = self._build_request_args(body, subject)
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(...) as client:
        resp = await client.request(**request_args)  # No validate_restricted_url() call
```

**Existing protection in Webhook block:** `prefect/blocks/webhook.py` lines 46-69

```python
allow_private_urls: bool = Field(default=True, ...)

async def call(self, payload=None):
    if not self.allow_private_urls:
        validate_restricted_url(self.url.get_secret_value())  # Protected
```

The `url` field at line 874 is a plain `str` with no URL validation.

## Impact

An authenticated user with block creation permissions can configure a `CustomWebhookNotificationBlock` targeting internal endpoints (cloud metadata at 169.254.169.254, internal services, etc). The block fires on automation/notification triggers, POSTing configurable data to the target.

## Recommended Fix

Add `allow_private_urls` field and `validate_restricted_url()` call to `CustomWebhookNotificationBlock`, matching the `Webhook` block pattern:

```python
allow_private_urls: bool = Field(default=True, description="Whether to allow notifications to private URLs.")

async def anotify(self, body, subject=None):
    if not self.allow_private_urls:
        validate_restricted_url(self.url)
    request_args = self._build_request_args(body, subject)
    ...
```

CWE-918: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
PrefectHQ/prefect#20747.title: Security: SSRF in CustomWebhookNotificationBlock - missing validate_restricted_url

PrefectHQ/prefect#20785.body:
The observer uses snake_case keys to access pod status fields, but kopf passes raw Kubernetes API JSON which uses camelCase. This causes two bugs:

  1. **`container_statuses` → should be `containerStatuses`** (L171): Eviction detection never runs, so OOMKilled pods emit `prefect.kubernetes.pod.failed` instead of `prefect.kubernetes.pod.evicted`. Any automation listening for `pod.evicted`
  events will never fire.

  2. **`restart_count` → should be `containerStatuses[*].restartCount`** (L120): `restart_count` is not a top-level pod status field, so event ID generation always uses 0. Minor impact on deduplication.

  ### Reproduction

  Create an automation that listens for `prefect.kubernetes.pod.evicted` with `kubernetes.reason: OOMKilled`, then trigger a flow run that OOMs. The automation never fires. Querying `/api/events/filter` confirms the pod emitted
  `prefect.kubernetes.pod.failed`, not `prefect.kubernetes.pod.evicted`.

  ### Root cause

  kopf's `Status` is a `MappingView` over raw API JSON with no key conversion ([source](https://github.com/nolar/kopf/blob/main/kopf/_cogs/structs/bodies.py#L178-L180)). The Kubernetes API returns `containerStatuses` (camelCase), but the
  observer looks up `container_statuses` (snake_case), which always returns `[]`.

  The unit test has the same bug in its mock data, so it passes despite the mismatch.

  ### Version info

  ```
  prefect-kubernetes >= 0.6.2
  kopf 1.38.0+ (confirmed on 1.38.0 and 1.43.0)
  ```
PrefectHQ/prefect#20785.title: Kubernetes observer never emits pod.evicted events due to camelCase key mismatch

PrefectHQ/prefect#20819.body:
### Bug summary

*Apologies for this less-than-thorough bug report; I've already lost significant time so can't invest more at this stage, wanted to flag this ASAP - hopefully others can reproduce/MRE/etc.*

Running `prefect` in a local test environment with a fresh `venv` today I observed the following exception as soon as a sub-flow was launched:
```
prefect.exceptions.PrefectHTTPStatusError: Client error '422 Unprocessable Entity' for url 'http://127.0.0.1:4200/api/task_runs/'
Response: {'exception_message': 'Invalid request received.', 'exception_detail': [{'type': 'model_attributes_type', 'loc': ['body'], 'msg': 'Input should be a valid dictionary or object to extract fields from', 'input': '{"state":{"type":"PENDING","name":"Pending","message":null,"state_details":
(...)
```

After bumping back/forward versions, I discovered this is tied to FastAPI 0.132.0, which was published 14 hours ago, and does list "Breaking Changes" in its [changelog](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/releases/tag/0.132.0).

Downgrading to FastAPI 0.131.0 (leaving all other packages the same) resolves the issue.

PrefectHQ/prefect#20954.body:
### Bug summary

From what I am able to see, there is an additional bug in the Redis causal ordering implementation -- please take a look, and let me know if i'm mistaken. When running multiple background services, followers processed by leaders are never marked as seen via a Redis key, resulting in follower events being parked until `PRECEDING_EVENT_LOOKBACK` and proliferation of Redis keys over time.                                                                                                                                                           

Related to: https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/20381

## Summary                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   

  There are two issues in the Redis causal ordering implementation for the task run recorder:

  1. Followers are never marked as seen: When a leader event completes and picks up its parked followers via complete_event_and_get_followers, those followers are processed by calling record_task_run_event directly — a plain DB write. They never go through event_is_processing, which is the only code path that calls complete_event_and_get_followers (the Lua script that SETs the seen:X key). This means any subsequent event in the chain that follows the follower will find seen:X → False, park itself, and become orphaned. For a typical 4-event task run chain (SCHEDULED → PENDING → RUNNING → COMPLETED), a single out-of-order arrival orphans every remaining event in the chain for PRECEDING_EVENT_LOOKBACK (15 minutes).
  2. record_lost_follower_task_run_events never calls forget_follower: When the lost follower recovery path processes an orphaned event, it calls record_task_run_event but never calls forget_follower. Since get_lost_followers only removes the event from the waitlist sorted set, the event:X and followers:Y keys are left permanently in Redis with no TTL and no code path that will ever reference them again.

The first issue compounds the second. Every out-of-order pair generates orphans that hit the lost follower recovery path, and their keys are never cleaned up. With multiple background services replicas, out-of-order delivery is common, and the leaked keyset grows unbounded over time.

## Reproduction

Same setup as #20381 — any workload that produces enough concurrent task runs to get out-of-order event delivery over the message queue. After running for a while, check Redis:

  redis-cli --scan --pattern "task-run-recorder:event:*" | wc -l
  redis-cli --scan --pattern "task-run-recorder:followers:*" | wc -l

These counts will continue to grow

### Test to demonstrate issue:
[test_ordering_chain_bug.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25730521/test_ordering_chain_bug.py)

## Example
```
  Four events for a single task run:

  - Event S — SCHEDULED (no follows)
  - Event P — PENDING (follows S)
  - Event R — RUNNING (follows P)
  - Event C — COMPLETED (follows R)

  Arrival order: P, S, R, C (PENDING arrives before SCHEDULED).

  ---
1)  Event P arrives (PENDING, follows S)

  handle_task_run_events([P]) enters preceding_event_confirmed(record_task_run_event, P, depth=0):

  1. event_is_processing(P): SET processing:P 1 EX 16 NX → True, yields completion
  2. wait_for_leader(P):
    - event_has_been_seen(S) → EXISTS seen:S → False
    - event_has_started_processing(S) → EXISTS processing:S → False
    - record_follower(P) → SET event:P <json>, SADD followers:S P.id, ZADD waitlist P.id <ts>
    - Double-check: event_has_been_seen(S) → still False
    - raise EventArrivedEarly(P)
  3. event_is_processing.__aexit__: success=False → DEL processing:P

  Redis state:
  ┌─────────────┬────────────┬──────┐
  │     Key     │   Value    │ TTL  │
  ├─────────────┼────────────┼──────┤
  │ event:P     │ P's JSON   │ none │
  ├─────────────┼────────────┼──────┤
  │ followers:S │ {P.id}     │ none │
  ├─────────────┼────────────┼──────┤
  │ waitlist    │ {P.id: ts} │ none │
  └─────────────┴────────────┴──────┘
  ---
2) Event S arrives (SCHEDULED, no follows)

  handle_task_run_events([S]) enters preceding_event_confirmed(record_task_run_event, S, depth=0):

  1. event_is_processing(S): SET processing:S 1 EX 16 NX → True, yields completion
  2. wait_for_leader(S): S.follows is None → returns immediately
  3. yield → to_insert.append(S)
  4. event_is_processing.__aexit__ (success path) calls complete_event_and_get_followers(S) — Lua script atomically:
    - SET seen:S 1 EX 1800
    - SMEMBERS followers:S → [P.id]
    - DEL followers:S
    - DEL processing:S
    - followers_by_id([P.id]) → GET event:P → [P]
  5. Processes followers:
  for waiter in completion.followers:       # waiter = P
      await handler(waiter, depth=1)        # record_task_run_event(P) — DB write only
      await self.forget_follower(waiter)    # cleans up event:P, waitlist entry
  6. S.follows is None → skip final forget_follower

  Redis state:
  ┌────────┬───────┬───────┐
  │  Key   │ Value │  TTL  │
  ├────────┼───────┼───────┤
  │ seen:S │ 1     │ 30min │
  └────────┴───────┴───────┘
  seen:P was never set. P was processed via record_task_run_event — a plain DB write. It never went through event_is_processing, which is the only path that calls the Lua script to SET seen.

  ---
3) Event R arrives (RUNNING, follows P)

  handle_task_run_events([R]) enters preceding_event_confirmed(record_task_run_event, R, depth=0):

  1. event_is_processing(R): SET processing:R 1 EX 16 NX → True, yields completion
  2. wait_for_leader(R):
    - event_has_been_seen(P) → EXISTS seen:P → False (never set!)
    - event_has_started_processing(P) → EXISTS processing:P → False
    - record_follower(R) → SET event:R <json>, SADD followers:P R.id, ZADD waitlist R.id <ts>
    - Double-check: event_has_been_seen(P) → still False
    - raise EventArrivedEarly(R)
  3. event_is_processing.__aexit__: success=False → DEL processing:R

  Redis state:
  ┌─────────────┬────────────┬───────┐
  │     Key     │   Value    │  TTL  │
  ├─────────────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ seen:S      │ 1          │ 30min │
  ├─────────────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ event:R     │ R's JSON   │ none  │
  ├─────────────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ followers:P │ {R.id}     │ none  │
  ├─────────────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ waitlist    │ {R.id: ts} │ none  │
  └─────────────┴────────────┴───────┘
  R is orphaned. P was already processed and will never re-enter the system. Nothing will ever check followers:P.

  ---
4) Event C arrives (COMPLETED, follows R)

  handle_task_run_events([C]) enters preceding_event_confirmed(record_task_run_event, C, depth=0):

  1. event_is_processing(C): SET processing:C 1 EX 16 NX → True, yields completion
  2. wait_for_leader(C):
    - event_has_been_seen(R) → EXISTS seen:R → False (never set!)
    - event_has_started_processing(R) → EXISTS processing:R → False
    - record_follower(C) → SET event:C <json>, SADD followers:R C.id, ZADD waitlist C.id <ts>
    - Double-check: event_has_been_seen(R) → still False
    - raise EventArrivedEarly(C)
  3. event_is_processing.__aexit__: success=False → DEL processing:C

  Redis state:
  ┌─────────────┬──────────────────────┬───────┐
  │     Key     │        Value         │  TTL  │
  ├─────────────┼──────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ seen:S      │ 1                    │ 30min │
  ├─────────────┼──────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ event:R     │ R's JSON             │ none  │
  ├─────────────┼──────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ followers:P │ {R.id}               │ none  │
  ├─────────────┼──────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ event:C     │ C's JSON             │ none  │
  ├─────────────┼──────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ followers:R │ {C.id}               │ none  │
  ├─────────────┼──────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ waitlist    │ {R.id: ts, C.id: ts} │ none  │
  └─────────────┴──────────────────────┴───────┘
  Both R and C are orphaned. A single out-of-order pair at the head of the chain cascades to every subsequent event.

  ---
5) 15 minutes later (lost follower recovery)

  periodically_process_followers calls record_lost_follower_task_run_events():

  1. get_lost_followers(): ZRANGESTORE + ZREMRANGEBYSCORE on waitlist → finds R.id and C.id → removes both from waitlist → GET event:R, GET event:C → returns [R, C]
  2. For each event:
    - Not >24h old → calls record_task_run_event(event) → writes to DB
    - forget_follower is never called

  Final Redis state (permanent, no TTL):
  ┌─────────────┬──────────┬──────┐
  │     Key     │  Value   │ TTL  │
  ├─────────────┼──────────┼──────┤
  │ event:R     │ R's JSON │ none │
  ├─────────────┼──────────┼──────┤
  │ followers:P │ {R.id}   │ none │
  ├─────────────┼──────────┼──────┤
  │ event:C     │ C's JSON │ none │
  ├─────────────┼──────────┼──────┤
  │ followers:R │ {C.id}   │ none │
  └─────────────┴──────────┴──────┘
  These keys are never cleaned up:
  - get_lost_followers only scans the waitlist sorted set — R and C were already removed
  - No other code path ever references followers:P, followers:R, event:R, or event:C
  - None of these keys have a TTL
```


### Version info

```Text
Version:            3.6.17
Helm chart version: 2026.1.20181753
Image tag:          3.6.17-python3.11
Python version:     3.11.14
OS/Arch:            linux/aarch64
```

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#20954.title: Redis causal ordering -- processing follower out of order does not mark `seen:X` key causing subsequent followers to be parked until `PRECEDING_EVENT_LOOKBACK` expiration

PrefectHQ/prefect#21060.body:
### Bug summary

### Description

When using `ConcurrencyLimitConfig(limit=1, collision_strategy=ConcurrencyLimitStrategy.CANCEL_NEW)`,
runs that enter `Late` state are never cancelled. 

`CANCEL_NEW` only acts on the Scheduled → Pending transition. If the work queue is fully occupied,
runs never reach that transition — they stay in `Late` state and accumulate indefinitely.

### Reproduction

1. Create a deployment with `concurrency_limit=1` and `CANCEL_NEW` strategy
2. Fill the work queue to 100% capacity so no new runs can be picked up
3. Trigger several runs of the same flow (or let the cron schedule create them)
4. All runs enter `Late` state instead of being cancelled
5. Once the queue frees up, all accumulated runs start executing one by one —
   only then does `CANCEL_NEW` kick in and cancel the extras

### Expected behavior

Runs should be cancelled immediately when the concurrency limit is reached,
regardless of whether they are in `Late` or `Pending` state.

### Workaround

We query the Prefect API at the start of each flow to find and cancel
other Late/Scheduled runs for the same deployment.

### Version

Prefect 3.4.25, Python 3.12

### Version info

```Text
Prefect 3.4.25, Python 3.12
```

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#21060.title: CANCEL_NEW concurrency strategy does not cancel Late runs, causing queue buildup

PrefectHQ/prefect#21064.body:
### Bug summary

  ### Description

  The `_lease_renewal_loop` in `src/prefect/concurrency/_leases.py` has no `try/except` around the renewal call. A single transient failure (network blip, server restart, 5xx error) that survives the HTTP-level retries kills the loop
  permanently. Because the flow engine hardcodes `raise_on_lease_renewal_failure=True` for deployment concurrency leases (`flow_engine.py:834`), this injects a `CancelledError` into the flow thread — crashing the flow run.

  Additionally, the actual exception that caused the renewal failure is never logged — only a generic message is printed, making diagnosis difficult.

  ### Minimal reproducible example

  Any flow with a deployment concurrency limit will crash if the Prefect server is temporarily unavailable during a lease renewal cycle:

  ```python
  from prefect import flow

  @flow
  def my_flow():
      import subprocess
      subprocess.run(["tar", "xzf", "assets.tgz"])  # or any long-running work
  ```

  Deploy with a concurrency limit, then restart the Prefect server (or introduce a network partition) while the flow is running. The lease renewal fails, and the flow is immediately killed.

  ### Stack trace

  ```
  Engine execution exited with unexpected exception
  Traceback (most recent call last):
    File "prefect/flow_engine.py", line 1566, in run_flow
      ret_val = run_flow_sync(**kwargs)
    File "prefect/flow_engine.py", line 1409, in run_flow_sync
      engine.call_flow_fn()
    File "prefect/flow_engine.py", line 808, in call_flow_fn
      result = call_with_parameters(self.flow.fn, self.parameters)
    File "prefect/utilities/callables.py", line 210, in call_with_parameters
      return fn(*args, **kwargs)
    ...
    File "subprocess.py", line 2115, in _communicate
      ready = selector.select(timeout)
    File "selectors.py", line 415, in select
      fd_event_list = self._selector.poll(timeout)
  prefect._internal.concurrency.cancellation.CancelledError

  Concurrency lease renewal failed - slots are no longer reserved.
  Terminating execution to prevent over-allocation.
  ```

  ### Additional details

  The problematic code (`src/prefect/concurrency/_leases.py:17-35`):

  ```python
  async def _lease_renewal_loop(lease_id, lease_duration):
      async with get_client() as client:
          while True:
              await client.renew_concurrency_lease(...)  # no try/except
              await asyncio.sleep(lease_duration * 0.75)
  ```

  `PREFECT_CLIENT_RETRY_EXTRA_CODES` does not help — even after adding 500/504 to the HTTP retry set, a single failure that exhausts all HTTP retries kills the loop with no application-level retry.

  ### Environment

  - **Prefect version**: 3.x (latest)
  - **Python**: 3.11
  - **Infrastructure**: Kubernetes worker, self-hosted Prefect server

### Version info

```Text
Version:              3.5.0
API version:          0.8.4
Python version:       3.11.14
Git commit:           7d86c932
Built:                Fri, Oct 31, 2025 04:59 PM
OS/Arch:              linux/x86_64
Profile:              ephemeral
Server type:          server
Pydantic version:     2.12.5
Server:
  Database:           sqlite
  SQLite version:     3.51.2
Integrations:
  prefect-docker:     0.0.1.dev32+g450c9c392.d20260116
  prefect-redis:      0.2.8
  prefect-azure:      0.0.1.dev23+g3724d73b2.d20260117
  prefect-kubernetes: 0.0.1.dev40+gff7a2a328.d20260213
```

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#21064.title: Concurrency lease renewal loop has no error handling — a single transient failure crashes the flow

PrefectHQ/prefect#21084.body:
### Bug summary

Running v2 of the UI. When moving between tabs of the “flow runs” container on the Dashboard page, the flow run information from the previous tab is still displayed until the new data is loaded. This causes quite a lot of confusion, as  (1) the displayed flows are all uniform color and (2) the flow runs tabs take quite some time to load (~10 seconds per tab, for around 55 total flow runs). 

As a fix, I would suggest (a) add a loading screen inside the flow runs container, (b) add a splash of color to each flow run corresponding to its state, similar to the v1 UI, and (c) reduce the amount of blocking server requests (I see 72 requests, mostly to /api/flow_runs/count and /api/flow_runs/filter, for one tab switch from Completed -> Late. There were 0 Late flow runs).


PrefectHQ/prefect#21114.body:
### Describe the current behavior

In newer versions of prefect (probably since v3.2), the /deployments/paginate endpoint has gotten much slower, and serves as one of the most blocking requests for the /deployments page load, for both ui v1/v2.

### Describe the proposed behavior

Looking at the query generated by the request, it seems like this query could be optimized a bit. /api/deployments/paginate matches empty `flow_or_run_deployment_name` string “” into the like comparator (ie `ilike(“%%”)`). We should patch the handling of empty filters/empty array filters in building the final query.

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#21114.title: Speed up /api/deployments/paginate endpoint

PrefectHQ/prefect#21187.body:
## Context

While building a self-hosted Prefect + dbt + DuckDB pipeline using `PrefectDbtOrchestrator`, we hit a confusing runtime error:

```
Error importing adapter: No module named 'dbt.adapters.duckdb'
Runtime Error: Could not find adapter type duckdb!
```

This error comes from `dbt-core` (`dbt/adapters/factory.py`) — not from `prefect-dbt` — so there's no good place to intercept or improve it at the library level.

## Suggestion

Add a note to the `prefect-dbt` docs/README making it explicit that users are responsible for installing their own dbt adapter alongside `prefect-dbt`:

```
# install prefect-dbt + your adapter separately
uv add prefect-dbt dbt-duckdb   # for DuckDB
uv add prefect-dbt dbt-snowflake # for Snowflake
```

The existing extras (`[snowflake]`, `[bigquery]`, `[postgres]`) exist because those integrations ship Prefect Block types that depend on the connector libraries — DuckDB doesn't have that, so a `[duckdb]` extra isn't warranted. But a clear prerequisite note would save users from a confusing dbt-level error with no obvious fix.

Related discussion: https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/discussions/21185
PrefectHQ/prefect#21187.title: prefect-dbt: document that users must install their own dbt adapter (e.g. dbt-duckdb)

PrefectHQ/prefect#21253.body:
## motivation

`deployment run --watch` uses `watch_flow_run()` from `flow_runs_watching.py` to stream events until a terminal state. but there's no way to watch a flow run you didn't create yourself — e.g. runs triggered by automations/event triggers.

this matters for agents and CI scripts that trigger a chain and need to wait for downstream runs to finish before verifying results. right now the only option is polling `flow-run ls` in a loop or `python -c`

## proposal

expose the existing `watch_flow_run()` as a `flow-run watch` subcommand:

```
prefect flow-run watch <id> [--timeout 300]
```

the implementation is ~10 lines since `watch_flow_run()` already does the heavy lifting:

```python
@flow_run_app.command()
@with_cli_exception_handling
async def watch(
    id: UUID,
    *,
    timeout: Annotated[
        Optional[int],
        cyclopts.Parameter("--timeout", help="Timeout in seconds."),
    ] = None,
):
    """Watch a flow run until it reaches a terminal state."""
    from prefect.cli.flow_runs_watching import watch_flow_run

    finished = await watch_flow_run(id, _cli.console, timeout=timeout)
    state = finished.state
    if state and state.is_completed():
        exit_with_success(f"Flow run finished successfully in '{state.name}'.")
    else:
        exit_with_error(f"Flow run finished in '{state.name}'.")
```

## usage

```bash
# watch an automation-triggered run
prefect flow-run watch 2b916d29-8183-4d27-aff8-24ef90f96544

# scripted: trigger ingest, then watch the compact run it eventually triggers
prefect deployment run ingest/ingest --watch
COMPACT_ID=$(prefect flow-run ls --flow-name compact --limit 1 -o json | jq -r '.[0].id')
prefect flow-run watch "$COMPACT_ID"
```
PrefectHQ/prefect#21253.title: add `flow-run watch` CLI command

PrefectHQ/prefect#21254.body:
### Bug summary

When I read [prefect Contribute Docs](https://docs.prefect.io/contribute/dev-contribute), I noticed the following pip command won't install the `[dev]` dependency group as docs expected. Then I verify with `pip 26.0.1` and it warns `WARNING: prefect 0.0.0 does not provide the extra 'dev'`
```python
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

# Installs the package with development dependencies
pip install -e ".[dev]"
```
Indeed, the [Extra syntax](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#extras) of dependency specifiers, just like `.[dev]` indicates a group named `dev` in the [optional-dependency](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/pyproject-toml/#dependencies-optional-dependencies) table  of `pyproject.toml`.
To install the `dev` dependency group specified in `[dependency-groups]` table of `pyproject.toml`, this command works
```bash
pip install --group dev -e .
```
While most contributor would like to use the amazing tool `uv`, I think it's better to fix the small docs error.

### Version info

```Text
Prefect version: latest in main branch
Pip version: 26.0.1
```

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#21254.title: Contribute Docs: wrong pip command to setup development environment

PrefectHQ/prefect#21256.body:
### Bug summary

Hi Team,

We’ve observed that labels defined in the job configuration for the Prefect Kubernetes work pool are being converted to lowercase.

For example, we expect the label enableIdentityHelper (which is case-sensitive and required by the sidecar injector). However, during job creation, it is being transformed to enableidentityhelper. Since the injector relies on the exact case, this mismatch is preventing the sidecars from being injected when the flow jobs start.

Expectation:
Prefect should preserve the original case of labels provided in the job configuration and avoid modifying their case during Kubernetes resource creation.

### Version info

```Text
Prefect : 3.6.23
Prefect-kubernetes: 0.6.3
```

### Additional context

_No response_
PrefectHQ/prefect#21256.title: Prefect kubernetes job configuration case sensitivity issue

PrefectHQ/prefect#21301.body:
### Description

  PR #20822 upgraded FastAPI from 0.128.0 to 0.132.0 and changed the client's `create_task_run` to send `json=` instead of `content=`. However, no server-side backward compatibility was added. Since the server's FastAPI pin is
  `>=0.111.0,<1.0.0`, any server upgrade to 3.6.19+ pulls in FastAPI 0.132+, which rejects the old client's request body with a 422.

  The root cause is that httpx's `content=` parameter (used by the old client) sends the body **without a `Content-Type` header**, which old FastAPI tolerated but FastAPI 0.132.0 does not.

  ### Reproduction

  ~~~python
  import httpx
  import json

  # Simulates what the old client (<3.6.19) does in create_task_run
  task_run_data = {
      "flow_run_id": "4d647261-5d2f-429c-8b66-a5fc39a505e8",
      "task_key": "my-task-key",
      "dynamic_key": "0",
  }

  # Old client uses content= (no Content-Type header)
  response = httpx.post(
      "http://<prefect-server>:4200/api/task_runs/",
      content=json.dumps(task_run_data),
  )
  print(response.status_code)  # 422
  print(response.json())
  # {'exception_message': 'Invalid request received.',
  #  'exception_detail': [{'type': 'model_attributes_type', 'loc': ['body'],
  #   'msg': 'Input should be a valid dictionary or object to extract fields from', ...}]}

  # New client uses json= (sets Content-Type: application/json) — works fine
  response = httpx.post(
      "http://<prefect-server>:4200/api/task_runs/",
      json=task_run_data,
  )
  print(response.status_code)  # 201
  ~~~

  ### Stack Trace

  ~~~
  prefect.exceptions.PrefectHTTPStatusError: Client error '422 Unprocessable Entity' for url 'http://prefect-server.prefect-dev.svc.cluster.local:4200/api/task_runs/'
  Response: {'exception_message': 'Invalid request received.', 'exception_detail': [{'type': 'model_attributes_type', 'loc': ['body'], 'msg': 'Input should be a valid dictionary or object to extract fields from', 'input':
  '{"state":{"type":"PENDING","name":"Pending",...}'}]}
  ~~~

  Full traceback originates from `prefect/client/orchestration/__init__.py` line 834:
  ~~~python
  response = await self._client.post("/task_runs/", content=content)
  ~~~

  ### Details

  - **Client version:** 3.5.0 (affects all clients <3.6.19)
  - **Server version:** 3.6.19+ (any version containing PR #20822)
  - **FastAPI version on server:** 0.132.0
  - **Affected endpoint:** `POST /task_runs/` (the only endpoint that used `content=` instead of `json=`)
  - **Introduced by:** https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/pull/20822

  ### Suggested Fix

  Add server-side middleware to default `Content-Type: application/json` when the header is missing on POST/PUT/PATCH requests. This is a ~10 line change in `src/prefect/server/api/server.py`. I have a working fix with a regression test at
  https://github.com/chuqCTC/prefect/tree/fix/fastapi-content-type-backward-compat.
PrefectHQ/prefect#21301.title: Upgrading Prefect server to 3.6.19+ breaks older clients (< 3.6.19) with 422 on POST /task_runs/

PrefectHQ/prefect#21319.body:
### Bug summary

Prefect loads `.env` through pydantic-settings’ dotenv source and via a few direct `dotenv_values(".env")` calls. When `.env` is a **FIFO** or otherwise **not a regular file**, `python-dotenv` can treat it like a normal file and **`open()` may block indefinitely**. `python-dotenv` already supports `dotenv_values(..., stream=...)`, but Prefect/pydantic-settings do not use that for non-regular paths today.

### Version info

```Text
Version:              3.6.23
API version:          0.8.4
Python version:       3.13.5
Git commit:           db5763e0
Built:                Thu, Mar 19, 2026 08:56 PM
OS/Arch:              darwin/arm64
Profile:              local
Server type:          cloud
Pydantic version:     2.12.5
Server:
  Database:           sqlite
  SQLite version:     3.49.1
```

### Additional context

**[1Password CLI](https://developer.1password.com/docs/cli/)** offers support for managing `.env` files through the software, instead of storing a real `.env` file on disk.

This produces a “`.env`” path that is not a regular file (FIFO, pipe, or fd). Tools that always do a normal `open(path)` on that path can block or behave badly.

See: 
* [Introducing new .env file support in 1Password environments](https://1password.com/blog/1password-environments-env-files-public-beta).
* [Docs: 1Password Environments](https://developer.1password.com/docs/environments/)
* [Docs: Access secrets from 1Password through local .env files](https://developer.1password.com/docs/environments/local-env-file)
PrefectHQ/prefect#21319.title: Prefect hangs on startup when `.env` is not a regular file (FD stream)

prefecthq/prefect#6777.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue.
- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

For our use case, we dynamically load modules based on the modules name. These modules can sometimes be installed as system packages and are not relative to the current directory for creating a deployment.

Introduced in 2.3.0, when attempting to use a module that is not relative to the current directory, Prefect fails with a value error.

### Reproduction

```python
import importlib

module, method = module_name.rsplit('.', 1)
mod = importlib.import_module(module)
workflow_flow = getattr(mod, method)

deployment = Deployment.build_from_flow(
    flow=workflow_flow,
    name=schedule.id,
    version=1,
    work_queue_name="default",
    schedule=deployment_schedule,
)
deployment.apply()
```

```
ValueError: '/home/user' does not start with '/Users/user/test_dir'
```

### First check

- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue.
- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

When the block schema version of a block subclass is updated, the expected behavior is that a new block schema is registered for the corresponding block type. It appears when a block schema with an existing checksum and new version is registered then the already registered block schema is found and returned instead of creating a new block schema and the block schema version is not being respected by the API.

### Reproduction

```python
from prefect import get_client
from prefect.blocks.core import block

class Test(Block):
    a: str
    b: str
    c: int

Test.register_type_and_schema(0

Test._block_schema_version = "new_version"

Test.register_type_and_schema()

async with get_client() as client:
    block_schema = await client.read_block_schema_by_checksum(                      
        checksum=NewBlock._calculate_schema_checksum(),
        version=NewBlock.get_block_schema_version(),
     )

assert block_schema.version == "new_version" # Fails because block_schema.version == "non-versioned"
```


### Error

```

# Copy complete stack trace and error message here, including log output if applicable.

```


### Versions

```
Version:             2.3.2
API version:         0.8.0
Python version:      3.9.13
Git commit:          6e931ee9
Built:               Tue, Sep 6, 2022 12:36 PM
OS/Arch:             darwin/x86_64
Profile:             prod
Server type:         cloud
```


### Additional context

_No response_

prefecthq/prefect#7033.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue.
- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

There is the option to add an extra logger with this ENV variable: `PREFECT_LOGGING_EXTRA_LOGGERS`

Using Prefect as a Pipeline Flow Manager, a lot of logs are being produced in functions called from within a Task.
Thus, I want all the logs from these functions visible in the Prefect UI console.

This seems to be working well when the function called is just being executed as a single proces.
When calling functions that are using multiprocessing you can't see the logs in the UI console nor in the console itself.

After some digging, I noticed there is a difference when I start multiprocessing on `fork` or `spawn`.
When using `spawn` you can't see any logs, when using `fork` you can see the logs in the console but NOT in the UI.

### Reproduction

Having this **simplified** example:

```python3
@task(name='Download')
def download() -> None:
    my_shared_download_function_accross_repos(
        num_of_processes=2,
    )

def my_shared_download_function_accross_repos(
    num_of_processes: int = 1,
) -> None:
     years = (2021,2022)

     multiprocess(
        my_download_function_that_logs_somthing,
        years,
        num_of_processes=num_of_processes,
    )


def multiprocess(
    func: Callable,
    iterable: Iterable,
    num_of_processes: int = 1,
) -> None:
     with spawn_scope(): # as fork
         with multiprocessing.Pool(processes=num_of_processes) as p:
              for iter_chunk in chunks(iterable, 1):
                  for _ in p.imap_unordered(
                      func, iter_chunk, chunksize=1
                  ):
                      pass


```

Results in the console as this:


```
14:03:49.936 | INFO    | Flow run 'omicron-carema' - Created task run 'Merge Projects-5cdf2ded-0' for task 'Merge Projects'
14:03:49.936 | INFO    | Flow run 'omicron-carema' - Submitted task run 'Merge Projects-5cdf2ded-0' for execution.
14:03:50.005 | INFO    | my_logger - [Downloading]: 2022.zip --> ORION LOGGER FORMAT: 
14:03:50.005 | INFO    | file_system.py                 | [Downloading]: 2022.zip --> MY PROGRAMS LOGGER FORMAT: 
14:03:50.019 | INFO    | Flow run 'omicron-carema' - Created task run 'Link Organisations Projects-5d886e86-0' for task
```

**LINE 3: ORION LOGGER FORMAT:**
Not shown in the UI while multiprocessing on `fork`
Not visible at all while multiprocessing on `spawn`
BUT visible without multiprocessing


### Error

```python3
No exception, you would expect that the logs are shown into the UI
```


### Versions

```Text
2.4.2
```


### Additional context

Tasks are executed with the default TaskRunner

I also tried to expand my programs logger module with this library -> https://github.com/jruere/multiprocessing-logging
When doing this I get the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".removed/venvlib/python3.10/site-packages/prefect/logging/handlers.py", line 273, in emit
    self.get_worker(profile).enqueue(self.prepare(record, profile.settings))
```

prefecthq/prefect#7747.body:
<!-- 
Thanks for opening a pull request to Prefect! We've got a few requests to help us review contributions:

- Make sure that your title neatly summarizes the proposed changes.
- Provide a short overview of the change and the value it adds.
- Share an example to help us understand the change in user experience.
- Confirm that you've done common tasks so we can give a timely review.

Happy engineering!
-->

Co-authored with @peytonrunyan 
Closes https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/7739

Converts Kubernetes job PIDs from `{cluster_name}:{job_name}` to `{cluster_uid}:{namespace}:{job_name}`. Cluster names can be arbitrarily set in the Kubernetes config and are not a reliable unique identifier. Additionally, the config does not exist when using an in-cluster agent or in cases like #7739. We generate a stable cluster UID from the `kube-system` namespace UID as described in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/44954 — which tracks the lack of a Kubernetes API for retrieving a cluster identifier. I attempted to use the job UID instead of the job name because it is guaranteed to be unique across namespaces. However, there is not a clear way to look up a job by its UID later without listing all jobs. Instead, we include the namespace in the PID to ensure we do not kill jobs in the wrong namespace.

Additionally, in #7701 the `KubernetesJobResult` returns were not updated to include the new identifier. Here, I do some minor refactoring to fix this.

<!-- Include an overview here -->

### Example
<!-- 
Share an example of the change in action.

A code blurb is best. Changes to features should include an example that is executable by a new user.
If changing documentation, a link to a preview of the page is great.
 -->
 
 ```python
 from prefect.infrastructure import KubernetesJob
import anyio


async def main():
    async with anyio.create_task_group() as tg:
        job = KubernetesJob(
            command=["sleep", "1000"], pod_watch_timeout_seconds=5
        )

        pid = await tg.start(job.run)
        print("PID:", pid)

        await job.kill(pid)
        print("Killed :)")


anyio.run(main)
```

### Checklist
<!-- These boxes may be checked after opening the pull request. -->

- [x] This pull request references any related issue by including "closes `<link to issue>`"
	- If no issue exists and your change is not a small fix, please [create an issue](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/new/choose) first.
- [x] This pull request includes tests or only affects documentation.
- [x] This pull request includes a label categorizing the change e.g. `fix`, `feature`, `enhancement`
  <!--  If you do not have permission to add a label, a maintainer will add one for you and check this box. -->
prefecthq/prefect#7747.title: Use cluster uid and namespace instead of cluster "name" for Kubernetes job identifiers

prefecthq/prefect#7868.body:
### First check

- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue.
- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

Testing Github storage locally I found unexpected behavior when my repo contained symlinked folders.

Absolute path symlinks were arriving with the git clone, but then the `copytree` process is copying all of the files to the destination. An error is thrown when `DirEntry` files are the same as the files followed from symlinked directories.

Relative path symlinks are failing to copy and throw an error.

Absolute path symlinks to files are being resolved and the actual files are being copied into the deployed/cloned repo. **For example, a repo symlink to a local credential token file was copied to the new (local) location.**

**Expected behavior:**
I think for Github storage we expect the flow code repo to be cloned into its new location, and symlinks pointing outside of the repo should not be followed and definitely not be recursively copied into the new location if for some reason they are found on the same system.

I expect the result of `get_directory()` for a github repo to be the same as if I did a `git clone https://github...etcetera.git`

In my example, on local machine I have:

```bash
ls -la test_prefect_repo
drwxr-xr-x 15  480 Dec 12 22:01 .git
lrwxr-xr-x  1   30 Dec 12 21:52 data -> /Users/mithral/toodata/zoobar
drwxr-xr-x 12  384 Dec 12 21:58 src
lrwxr-xr-x  1   44 Dec 12 21:58 gh_keys.env -> /Users/mithral/.keys/gh_keys.env

ls -la src
drwxr-xr-x  4  128 Dec 12 21:58 tasks
lrwxr-xr-x  1   19 Dec 12 21:52 tables -> ../../sql-tables             # this points to repo parent folder
```



### Reproduction
1. Create a project with Github storage
2. Create a symlink to another folder on local machine
3. Create a symlink file, for example `ln -s ~/.keys/github_credentials.env .`
4. Create a Github block like this example

```python3
# at python repl
>>>import os
from prefect.filesystems import GitHub

os.chdir("/path/to/anywhere/")

gh_storage = GitHub(
    repository=GITHUB_REPO_HTTPS
    , access_token=GITHUB_TOKEN

gh_storage.get_directory()
```



### Additional context

I presume this doesn't happen when Github clone is copied to S3, or a clone is copied to working directory on a remote machine or docker container.

It may seem like an edge case, but I'm testing the behavior of Prefect as I try to adopt it at work, and this unexpected behavior could be prevented if symlinks aren't followed out of the repo.

I can't see a sane use-case where we want the code deployment to depend on files that are symlinked to other places on the local machine, so I expect putting a limitation on `_copytree()` to not follow symlinks makes the most sense:

* If the linked file exists in the repo, the link will be preserved. 
* If links go outside the repo, if the desired files are provided by the user on the remote machine, links will be preserved.
* Otherwise, the symlink exists just as a dead link as it should be (for example when I'm linking to a local credential file to create deployments.)
prefecthq/prefect#7868.title: Github.get_directory() is resolving symlinks and copying files when deploy to local filesystem

prefecthq/prefect#8281.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar request and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this feature.

### Prefect Version

2.x

### Describe the current behavior

Right now, there is no way for annotations to be added to a KubernetesJob manifest from my understanding. This sits similarly next to labels in the metadata block of the job manifest. 

### Describe the proposed behavior

I would like to be able to specify annotations in my job template manifest and have them populated to my kubernetes job (prefect flow)

### Example Use

I need to put an annotation on my kubernetes job to tell an admission controller to populate secrets into the job environment

### Additional context

Annotations are pretty straightforward to implement but aren't totally trivial. I implemented a quick fix that I would be happy to iterate on :) it's a little more subtle than labels because annotations aren't in the default JobManifest, so the json application of shortcuts errors out when it is trying to apply an annotation to a doc that doesn't exist. I made a little intermediate function to inject noop defaults into kubernetes job manifests, but I'm not sure if I'm just missing this functionality somewhere else so feel free to point me!
prefecthq/prefect#8281.title: Ability to add kubernetes annotations to prefect KubernetesJobs

prefecthq/prefect#9245.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar request and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this feature.

### Prefect Version

2.x

### Describe the current behavior

Currently `prefect project init` generates two yaml files (deployment.yaml & prefect.yaml). These files "as is" will be flagged in any generic yaml linter (like [yamllint](https://yamllint.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)):
```
prefect.yaml
  4:1       warning  missing document start "---"  (document-start)
  10:81     error    line too long (91 > 80 characters)  (line-length)
  13:81     error    line too long (94 > 80 characters)  (line-length)
  15:1      error    wrong indentation: expected 2 but found 0  (indentation)
```

### Describe the proposed behavior

Any generated yaml files should confirm with common yaml standards to avoid being flagged by end-users ci pipelines. Specifically, there should be indents on lists and lines should not exceed 80 chars:
```
# File for configuring project / deployment build, push and pull steps

# Generic metadata about this project
name: test
prefect-version: 2.10.4

# build section allows you to manage and build docker images
build: null

# push section allows you to manage if and how this project is uploaded to
# remote locations
push: null

# pull section allows you to provide instructions for cloning this project in
# remote locations
pull:
  - prefect.projects.steps.git_clone_project:
      repository: git@github.com:PrefectHQ/test.git
      branch: main
      access_token: null
```

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#9245.title: Update yaml files to conform with common yaml linting standards

prefecthq/prefect#10832.body:
### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the Prefect documentation for this issue.
- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

We are using the Git clone pull step with our self-hosted Gitlab instance, and have been using a [Deploy token](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/deploy_tokens/) for the access token for Prefect to clone our code.  This has been working until the most recent Gitlab [update](https://about.gitlab.com/releases/2023/09/18/security-release-gitlab-16-3-4-released/#attacker-can-abuse-scan-execution-policies-to-run-pipelines-as-another-user), which fixed a CVE by preventing deploy tokens from working in an OAuth2 context.

This update, combined with the fact that Prefect automatically prepends `oauth2:` to any access token associated with a Gitlab repository ([code](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/prefect/deployments/steps/pull.py#L49)) leads to Gitlab rejecting our deploy token used in conjunction with the prefix, while cloning without the `oauth2:` prefix works fine.

At the moment, we are working around the issue by using a Personal Access Token, however I'd appreciate if there was a way to configure Prefect to not add the prefix so we can switch back to deploy tokens, which are less brittle than personal access tokens.

### Reproduction

```python3
# prefect.yaml excerpt
pull:
  - prefect.deployments.steps.git_clone:
      id: clone-source
      repository: "{{ prefect.variables.source_git_repo }}"
      branch: "{{ prefect.variables.source_git_ref }}"
      access_token: "{{ prefect.blocks.secret.source-repo-token }}"
```


### Error

```python3
remote: HTTP Basic: Access denied. The provided password or token is incorrect or your account has 2FA enabled and you must use a personal access token instead of a password.
```


### Versions

```Text
Version:             2.13.2
API version:         0.8.4
Python version:      3.8.18
Git commit:          dcd8a58f
Built:               Thu, Sep 21, 2023 2:33 PM
OS/Arch:             linux/x86_64
Profile:             default
Server type:         server
```


### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#10832.title: Addition of `oauth2:` prefix in access token breaks Gitlab deploy tokens in Git clone step

### First check

- [X] I added a descriptive title to this issue.

- [X] I checked that this issue is related to Prefect and not one of its dependencies.

### Bug summary

A pull_step might install its own requirements, which might contain conflicting versions of already installed dependencies (e.g. prefect itself).
Version constraints of a pull_step requirement might change even for minor versions (e.g. https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect-aws/compare/v0.4.1...v0.4.2, prefect-aws 0.4.1 requires prefect>=2.10.11, prefect-aws 0.4.2 requires prefect>=2.13.5).

This can lead to the situation where:
- Prefect version A is installed on machine
- pull_step requirements installs Prefect version B while Prefect is running
- added functionality from version B is not available in already running version A, leading to import errors or possibly unexpected behavior.

While pinning the requirements versions of a pull step to a specific version solves the issue, I would expect that Prefect checks if the installation of pull_step requirements would overwrite already installed code and refuse to install, instead of running unexpected errors later on.




### Reproduction

```python3
- install prefect==2.13.4
- create deployment with a pull_step that requires prefect-aws>=0.4.0
- start an agent and run deployment

This shows that in the process of installing prefect-aws>=0.4.0, prefect-aws==0.4.2 is chosen,  installs prefect-2.14.3 and uninstalls prefect-2.13.4.

This causes an error related to some newly introduced functionality that is not yet available in prefect-2.13.4
```

### First check

### Bug summary

Following the ECR build example available [here](https://prefecthq.github.io/prefect-aws/ecs_guide/#Optional-Next-Steps), I came up with the following step in my `prefect.yaml` file : 
```
push:
- prefect.deployments.steps.run_shell_script:
    id: ecr-auth-step
    script: aws ecr get-login-password --region eu-west-2 --profile test-test-test | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 12345678910.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
    stream_output: true
```
Which then fails silently with the following output : 
```
Running deployment push steps...
 > Running run_shell_script step...

Unknown options: |,docker,login,--username,AWS,--password-stdin,12345678910.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com
```

### Reproduction

### Describe the current behavior

User-chosen names are static. If not given, defaults to `flow_run.name`.

### Describe the proposed behavior

Allow using any `flow` or `flow_run` parameter to set the infra name.

### Example Use

Specifying a name.

```python
from prefect_docker.worker import DockerWorkerJobConfiguration
DockerWorkerJobConfiguration(name="worker-1/{flow.name}/{flow_run.name}")
```

The main advantage is that it's easier to tell at a glance what's running, e.g. with `docker stats`:

```console
CONTAINER ID   NAME                                  CPU %     MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %
b46329f94fbf   worker-1_small-flow_phi310-wezn       34.69%    2.828GiB / 50.94GiB   5.55%
b3e8c1db8da5   worker-1_big-flow_mu5-argana-conflux  769.65%   3.128GiB / 50.94GiB   6.14%
```


### Additional context

This seems pretty easy to implement, see https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/compare/main...rsundqvist:prefect:main

prefecthq/prefect#15008.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Currently, Prefect supports many user interfaces that we attempt to maintain as compatible with both *synchronous* execution as well as *asynchronous* execution. [A popular example of this is `Block.load`](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/cc4ba866dad93b0fd62315d5b6487f574f874f8e/src/prefect/blocks/core.py#L822). The way this is achieved is through [an internal utility](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/cc4ba866dad93b0fd62315d5b6487f574f874f8e/src/prefect/utilities/asyncutils.py#L329) that "magically" - and more critically: **quietly** - attempts to decide on the user's behalf whether the user will be able to await the coroutine or not.

This works well in certain situations such as methods / utilities called *within* Prefect tasks and flows - this is because we explicitly track whether that task or flow is synchronous or asynchronous.

However, this can turn out *very poorly* in situations where something about the runtime environment changes between local development and production. A few examples to make the point:

* [https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14712](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14712)
* [https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14625](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14625)
* [https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/13181](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/13181)

Prefect 3.0 exposes a new keyword argument on these special methods / functions for explicitly setting the behavior a user wants: to continue with the block loading example, users can now specify `Block.load(**kwargs, _sync=True)` for enforcing synchronous execution and `await Block.load(**kwargs, _sync=False)` for enforcing asynchronous execution. This is useful as an escape hatch, but under the hood it still engages with complex event loop and threading logic that risks performance degradation and more difficult to inspect failure modes.

Clearly there is room for improvement here along a few dimensions:

* warning users who are relying on implicit magic so they can more easily self-debug / harden their code
* improving Prefect performance by not managing its own event loops / threads for running coroutines
* exposing `_sync=True/False` behavior in a more first class way for discoverability

### Describe the proposed behavior

To achieve the goals outlined above, I propose first expanding the `sync_compatible` interface in two ways:

* allowing for `sync_compatible(sync_version=sync_method)` that explicitly provides an alternative synchronous implementation to dispatch between; note this *does* mean expanding the codebase into both synchronous and asynchronous implementations. The decorator and our current "magic" will allow us to take [a strangler fig](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strangler_fig_pattern) approach and incrementally add these duplicate implementations as we develop
* exposing a mirror utility `async_compatible(async_version=async_method)` (the need for this will become clear below when I discuss naming conventions)

For any decorated function / method that has a dual implementation, Prefect can begin issuing a warning whenever the user relies on behavior for which that function / method is dispatching to another implementation. For example:

```python
await Block.load(**kwargs)
## warning is issued that directs the user to use `Block.aload(**kwargs)` explicitly
```

To make sure this part is not glossed over: this will ultimately result in Prefect maintaining *two* implementations for a large class of user interfaces (primarily those that interact with the Prefect client / API).

### Naming Convention

To achieve this, we will rely on the following naming conventions:

* classes for which the core implementation will change between sync / async will be sychronous by default, and the async versions will prefix their class name with `Async`; e.g., `PrefectClient` vs. `AsyncPrefectClient`
* whenever the class remains the same, synchronous methods will be the default and asynchronous methods will implemented with an `a` prefix: e.g., `Block.load` vs. `Block.aload`
* for top-line functions, the same will be true: implementations will be synchronous first, and async implementations will have an `a` prefix: e.g., `run_deployment` vs `arun_deployment`

There is one edge case with this, which is `.wait` methods on Prefect futures; for this we will make the corresponding *class* awaitable for async waits.

# contributing
for anyone willing to contribute to this effort, here's roughly how you can help

## removing `@sync_compatible`

`sync_compatible` breaks typing, so we want to introduce explicit (type-safe) async methods. the typing will still be broken on the `async_dispatch` function, but only until we can remove that compatibility layer (then it will be an explicit sync function)

## before
```python
@sync_compatible
async def some_fn_or_method(...): ...
```

## after
```python
async def some_async_fn_or_method(...): ...


@async_dispatch(some_async_fn_or_method)
def some_fn_or_method(...): ...
```



### Remaining uses of `@sync_compatible`

#### `prefect`

`prefect.artifacts`

- [x] `Artifact.create`
- [x] `Artifact.get`
- [x] `Artifact.get_or_create`
- [x] `create_link_artifact`
- [x] `create_markdown_artifact`
- [x] `create_table_artifact`
- [x] `update_progress_artifact`

`prefect.filesystems`

prefecthq/prefect#15512.body:
### Describe the current behavior

The open-source Prefect UI is built with Vue, with its functionality spread across multiple Prefect-managed packages. Key aspects of the UI, including the component system, data management, and state handling, are custom-developed. However, the Prefect UI faces several challenges:

- **Limited test coverage:** Insufficient tests reduce developers' confidence in making changes safely and increase the risk of introducing regressions.
- **High maintenance burden:** Custom-built systems are harder to maintain and update.
- **Barriers to contributions:** The complexity and lack of testing make it difficult for community members to contribute effectively.

These issues collectively hinder the UI’s maintainability and slow down the development process. To create a more sustainable and open platform that supports rapid iteration and ensures high quality, we have decided to replatform the Prefect UI using modern frameworks, libraries, and tooling.

### Describe the proposed behavior

We intend to "replatform" the open-source Prefect UI in the following way:

1. **Adopt React**: Transitioning to React will give us access to a robust ecosystem of libraries and a larger community.
2. **Improve data management**: Implementing [TanStack Query](https://github.com/TanStack/query) will enhance data fetching while providing a more consistent developer experience. TanStack Query’s capabilities to prefetch and cache data across routes, along with optimistic updates for mutations, will improve application responsiveness.
    
    Adopting [React Hook Form](https://github.com/react-hook-form/react-hook-form) and [Zod](https://github.com/colinhacks/zod) will improve the surface area of our forms by providing consistent form creation and data validation patterns throughout the application.
    
3. **Enhance routing**: [TanStack Router](https://github.com/tanstack/router)'s type-safe approach with built-in data loading will improve our cross-route data fetching patterns, improving responsiveness and developer productivity. TanStack Router supports [preloading routes](https://tanstack.com/router/latest/docs/framework/react/guide/preloading) and [data loading for routes](https://tanstack.com/router/latest/docs/framework/react/guide/data-loading) out of the box; both will be key to improving navigation responsiveness.
4. **Use modern, accessible components**: By leveraging [shadcn/ui](https://github.com/shadcn-ui/ui) built on [Radix UI primitives](https://github.com/radix-ui/primitives), we can create accessible, consistent components built with best practices. Components from `shadcn/ui` will be hosted directly in the project, are customizable, and are available for reuse. This will make it easier to contribute, speed up development, and improve the overall user experience.
5. **Automate API type generation**: Generating TypeScript types and data fetching code from our OpenAPI specification (with tools like [openapi-fetch](https://github.com/openapi-ts/openapi-typescript/tree/main/packages/openapi-fetch) will ensure consistency between our frontend and backend, reduce errors, and improve our development workflow.
6. **Create a robust test suite:** Since the UI replatform will involve rewriting nearly the entire UI codebase, we can start with solid testing practices and maintain them long term. We plan on using [vitest](https://vitest.dev/) and [react-testing-library](https://testing-library.com/docs/react-testing-library/intro/) for unit testing and [Playwright](https://playwright.dev/) for end-to-end tests covering both the UI and the API.

Some parts of the UI codebase will remain unchanged. For example, we will continue to use [Vite](https://vitejs.dev/) for our dev server and build system, and we will continue to use [eslint](https://eslint.org/) for static type checking. The Prefect UI codebase will continue to live alongside the rest of the `prefect` package, but our goal is to have the entire codebase contained in the PrefectHQ/prefect repository without any other Prefect-maintained dependencies.

# How we’ll get there

Until the new UI is ready, self-hosted Prefect instances will continue to serve the current UI version. The new UI will be available in experimental preview once enough functionality has been reimplemented, and users will be able to switch between the new and old UI as they see fit. Once we’ve fully reimplemented the UI and addressed feedback from the community, we will remove the old UI, and the new UI will become the default.

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#15512.title: Replatform the UI

prefecthq/prefect#15760.body:
### Bug summary

Task timeouts do not appear to actually be enforced. A `TimeoutError` is eventually raised but not when it's supposed to be.

The [docs](https://docs-2.prefect.io/latest/concepts/tasks/#timeouts) contain the following example:

```py
from prefect import task
import time

@task(timeout_seconds=1, log_prints=True)
def show_timeouts():
    print("I will execute")
    time.sleep(5)
    print("I will not execute")
```

Inspect the logs to see that 5 seconds actually pass before the `TimeoutError` occurs. Increase the seconds to a higher number to make it more noticeable.

prefecthq/prefect#15963.body:
### Bug summary

When interacting with the prefect client, documentation does not clarify that you must paginate via the offset parameter even when limit is set to None. The limit in reality is 200, even when set to None. Our environment has >2000 deployments and I ran the code below to find out this limit. This is present in all client functions that have the limit parameter.
 
```python
from prefect import get_client
from asyncio import run

async def main():
    client =  get_client()
    deployments = await client.read_deployments(limit=None)
        
    assert len(deployments) == 200 # passes
    assert len(deployments) > 200 # fails

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run(main())
```

### Version info

```Text
Version:             2.19.3
API version:         0.8.4
Python version:      3.11.9
Git commit:          ce378efe
Built:               Thu, May 30, 2024 11:59 AM
OS/Arch:             linux/x86_64
Profile:             default
Server type:         server

There is also no reference to this in prefect 3.x
```


### Additional context

From the python docs:
```python
    async def read_deployments(
        self,
        *,
        flow_filter: FlowFilter = None,
        flow_run_filter: FlowRunFilter = None,
        task_run_filter: TaskRunFilter = None,
        deployment_filter: DeploymentFilter = None,
        work_pool_filter: WorkPoolFilter = None,
        work_queue_filter: WorkQueueFilter = None,
        limit: int = None,
        sort: DeploymentSort = None,
        offset: int = 0,
    ) -> List[DeploymentResponse]:
        """
        Query the Prefect API for deployments. Only deployments matching all
        the provided criteria will be returned.

        Args:
            flow_filter: filter criteria for flows
            flow_run_filter: filter criteria for flow runs
            task_run_filter: filter criteria for task runs
            deployment_filter: filter criteria for deployments
            work_pool_filter: filter criteria for work pools
            work_queue_filter: filter criteria for work pool queues
            limit: a limit for the deployment query
            offset: an offset for the deployment query

        Returns:
            a list of Deployment model representations
                of the deployments
        """
```
There is no additional info in the online docs either.
prefecthq/prefect#15963.title: Client functions have limit even when set to None

prefecthq/prefect#16054.body:
I figured out that our Prefect server deployment was running slow over time and we had trouble scheduling new jobs or updating data in the UI. The issue was the Prefect internal database that was overflowing with logs from old runs and using a script suggested by @Arthurhussey helped mitigate the problem by removing logs older than a week. 

While this solution worked in my case, having a scheduled flow tampering with the Prefect database directly might be a source of issues downhill. 

It would be very nice if Prefect server had a way of cleaning its logs automatically. For instance, an environment variable similar to `PREFECT_EVENTS_RETENTION_PERIOD` for flow runs and task runs.

The initial discussion : 

> @rmnvncnt the Prefect server doesn't have any auto clean-up features right now, but if that's something you'd like, please open an issue so we can discuss it further!
> 
> It looks like the issue of deployments not being displayed has been solved by reducing the amount of data in your DB so the scheduler can insert scheduled runs, so I'm going close this issue.
> 
> _Originally posted by @desertaxle in https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/15919#issuecomment-2483931935_
prefecthq/prefect#16054.title: Auto clean-up feature for the Prefect internal database

prefecthq/prefect#16302.body:
### Bug summary

The following is set in profiles.toml
PREFECT_TASKS_DEFAULT_PERSIST_RESULT = "False"

When I run the following, it works as expected, there is no need to persist a result so no result get persisted:

```python
from prefect import flow, task

@task
def tasky(value):
    return value
    
@flow
def do_not_want_persistance():
    a = tasky.submit(56)
    a.wait()
    
if __name__=='__main__':
    do_not_want_persistance()
```

If I use the "with_options" method to set a custom name on the task, then a result gets persisted, however it should not be:

```python
from prefect import flow, task

@task
def tasky(value):
    return value
    
@flow
def do_not_want_persistance():
    a = tasky.with_options(name='something').submit(56)
    a.wait()
    
if __name__=='__main__':
    do_not_want_persistance()
```

The ECS Worker Guide instructs users to assign the `arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AmazonECSTaskExecutionRolePolicy` policy to their worker ECSTask, but this role is insufficient for actually starting flow runs ECSTasks. The role should include the following permissions (also defined [here](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect-recipes/blob/main/devops/infrastructure-as-code/aws/tf-prefect2-ecs-worker/main.tf#L101-L117)):

```
"ec2:DescribeSubnets",
"ec2:DescribeVpcs",
"ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability",
"ecr:BatchGetImage",
"ecr:GetAuthorizationToken",
"ecr:GetDownloadUrlForLayer",
"ecs:DeregisterTaskDefinition",
"ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition",
"ecs:DescribeTasks",
"ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition",
"ecs:RunTask",
"ecs:StopTask",
"iam:PassRole",
"logs:CreateLogGroup",
"logs:CreateLogStream",
"logs:GetLogEvents",
"logs:PutLogEvents"
```
