prefecthq/prefect#20121.body:
### Bug summary

When an AWS Credentials block is configured with `assume_role_arn`, Prefect does not attempt to assume the role when using `prefect_aws.deployments.steps.pull_from_s3` or `prefect_aws.deployments.steps.push_to_s3` deployment steps.

Block configuration:

```python
AwsCredentials(
    region_name="us-east-2",
    assume_role_arn="arn:aws:iam::12345678890:role/test-role",
    aws_client_parameters=AwsClientParameters()
).save("aws-assume-role-test")
```

`prefect.yaml`:

```yaml
pull:
  - prefect_aws.deployments.steps.pull_from_s3:
      requires: "prefect-aws"
      bucket: "example"
      folder: "example"
      credentials: "{{ prefect.blocks.aws-credentials.aws-assume-role-test }}"

deployments:
  - name: "pull-test"
    entrypoint: "example/example.py:hello_world"
```

`pull_from_s3` calls `get_s3_client`, which does not contain assume role logic. https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/b440c7084fb0247c4c4a942d64b3a9715fbbe214/src/integrations/prefect-aws/prefect_aws/deployments/steps.py#L81

We should either include (duplicate) the assume role logic in this method, or we should refactor to use the AwsCredential `get_boto3_session` logic.

I'd personally like to see the second option. This is used for example in the `batch_submit` method:

https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/b440c7084fb0247c4c4a942d64b3a9715fbbe214/src/integrations/prefect-aws/prefect_aws/batch.py#L133

### Version info

```Text
prefect-aws==0.7.2
```

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#20121.title: [prefect-aws] pull_from_s3 does not attempt to assume role

prefecthq/prefect#20131.body:
### Bug summary

## Description

When using a Kubernetes worker with a base job template that defines a `cluster_config` with `insecure-skip-tls-verify: true` (we run on microk8s that does not have SSL), the SSL bypass is not applied to flow runs if those deployments specify any other `job_variables` (like `cpu_limit`, `memory_limit`).

The `cluster_config` default in the template is being ignored/not merged when the deployment has its own `job_variables`.

## Setup

We run a self-hosted Prefect Server as a Kubernetes deployment:

```yaml
# Prefect Server accessible at http://aidora-prefect-server:4200/api
```

Our Kubernetes worker polls this server and creates Jobs for flow runs:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: aidora-prefect-worker
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: prefect-worker
          image: prefecthq/prefect:3-python3.13-kubernetes
          command: ["prefect", "worker", "start", "--pool", "kubernetes", "--type", "kubernetes"]
          env:
            - name: PREFECT_API_URL
              value: "http://aidora-prefect-server:4200/api"
```

## Base Job Template Configuration

Our work pool has a base job template with `cluster_config` that includes SSL bypass (required for MicroK8s which has CA cert issues):

```json
{
  "variables": {
    "definitions": {
      "KubernetesClusterConfig": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "config": {"type": "object"},
          "context_name": {"type": "string"}
        }
      }
    },
    "properties": {
      "cluster_config": {
        "anyOf": [{"$ref": "#/definitions/KubernetesClusterConfig"}, {"type": "null"}],
        "default": {
          "config": {
            "apiVersion": "v1",
            "kind": "Config",
            "clusters": [{"name": "default", "cluster": {"server": "https://kubernetes.default.svc", "insecure-skip-tls-verify": true}}],
            "contexts": [{"name": "default", "context": {"cluster": "default", "user": "default"}}],
            "current-context": "default",
            "users": [{"name": "default", "user": {"tokenFile": "/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token"}}]
          },
          "context_name": "default"
        }
      },
      "cpu_limit": {"type": "string", "default": "2000m"},
      "memory_limit": {"type": "string", "default": "3Gi"}
    }
  }
}
```

## Deployment Code

```python
from prefect import flow

@flow
def my_flow():
    pass

my_flow.deploy(
    name="my-deployment",
    work_pool_name="kubernetes",
    image="my-image:latest",
    job_variables={
        "cpu_limit": "1000m",
        "memory_limit": "2Gi",
    }
)
```

## The Problem

When a flow run is submitted, the worker fails with SSL errors:

```
aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientConnectorCertificateError: Cannot connect to host kubernetes.default.svc:443 ssl:True [SSLCertVerificationError: (1, '[SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed: CA cert does not include key usage extension (_ssl.c:1032)')]
```

## What's Confusing - Manual Tests Work!

When I exec into the worker pod and test the same configuration manually, **it works perfectly**:

**Test 1: Using `new_client_from_config_dict` (what the worker uses internally)**

```python
import asyncio
from kubernetes_asyncio import config, client as k8s_client

config_dict = {
    'apiVersion': 'v1',
    'kind': 'Config',
    'clusters': [{'name': 'default', 'cluster': {'server': 'https://kubernetes.default.svc', 'insecure-skip-tls-verify': True}}],
    'contexts': [{'name': 'default', 'context': {'cluster': 'default', 'user': 'default'}}],
    'current-context': 'default',
    'users': [{'name': 'default', 'user': {'tokenFile': '/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token'}}],
}

async def test():
    client = await config.new_client_from_config_dict(config_dict=config_dict, context='default')
    v1 = k8s_client.BatchV1Api(client)
    jobs = await v1.list_namespaced_job('aidora', limit=1)
    print(f'Success! Found {len(jobs.items)} jobs')  # This works!
    await client.close()

asyncio.run(test())
# Output: Success! Found 1 jobs
```

**Test 2: Using `KubernetesClusterConfig.get_api_client()`**

```python
import asyncio
from prefect_kubernetes import KubernetesClusterConfig

cluster_config = {
    'config': {... same as above ...},
    'context_name': 'default',
}

async def test():
    kcc = KubernetesClusterConfig(**cluster_config)
    client = await kcc.get_api_client()
    # ... use client - this also works!

asyncio.run(test())
# Output: Success!
```

**But when checking the actual job configuration:**

```python
async with KubernetesWorker(work_pool_name='kubernetes') as worker:
    config = await worker._get_configuration(flow_run)
    print(f'cluster_config: {config.cluster_config}')
# Output: cluster_config: None  <-- THIS IS THE PROBLEM!
```

## Root Cause

The `cluster_config` default from the template is **not being merged** into the job configuration when the deployment specifies other `job_variables`.

I verified:
1. The work pool template has `cluster_config` with correct default ✓
2. `KubernetesClusterConfig` works correctly when instantiated manually ✓
3. `new_client_from_config_dict` works correctly ✓
4. But `worker._get_configuration(flow_run).cluster_config` returns `None` ✗

## Workaround

Adding `cluster_config` explicitly to each deployment's `job_variables` works:

```python
my_flow.deploy(
    name="my-deployment",
    work_pool_name="kubernetes",
    job_variables={
        "cpu_limit": "1000m",
        "memory_limit": "2Gi",
        "cluster_config": {
            "config": {...},
            "context_name": "default"
        }
    }
)
```

But this defeats the purpose of having defaults in the base job template.

## What We Tried That Didn't Help

1. **Setting `KUBECONFIG` environment variable on worker** - Worker's K8s client doesn't use it
2. **Various SSL env vars** (`KUBERNETES_VERIFY_SSL`, `PYTHONHTTPSVERIFY`) - No effect
3. **Mounting kubeconfig file** - Not used by the worker


### Version info

```Text
## Environment
- **Prefect**: 3.x (using `prefecthq/prefect:3-python3.13-kubernetes` image)
- **prefect-kubernetes**: 0.7.2
- **Kubernetes**: MicroK8s
- **Setup**: Self-hosted Prefect Server in same K8s cluster as worker
```

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#20131.title: cluster_config from base job template not applied when deployment has other job_variables

prefecthq/prefect#20161.body:
### Bug summary

## Problem

When using custom job configurations with multiple container definitions, the Prefect ECS worker re-registers task definitions even when they are identical. This occurs when the `essential` field is not explicitly set on container definitions.

## Root Cause

AWS ECS sets `essential` to True for containers if it's not specified at Task Definition registration time.

When comparing task definitions:
1. The generated task definition from Prefect may not have `essential` set on all containers
2. The task definition retrieved from AWS has `essential` values set by AWS defaults
3. The comparison logic in `_task_definitions_equal()` wasn't normalizing the `essential` field correctly
4. This mismatch causes the comparison to fail, leading to unnecessary re-registration

## Additional Issue

**`essential: False` is removed from container definitions**

The `_drop_empty_keys_from_dict` function removes boolean `False` values from task definitions, causing `essential: False` to be stripped from container definitions before registration with AWS. AWS then defaults `essential` to `True`, changing the intended behavior.



### Version info

```Text
prefect-aws<=0.7.2
```

### Additional context

## Example Configuration

This issue affects customers using custom job configurations like:

```
task_definition:
  containerDefinitions:
    - name: "prefect"
      image: "{{ image }}"
    - name: "datadog-agent"
      image: "public.ecr.aws/datadog/agent:latest"
```
prefecthq/prefect#20161.title: [prefect-aws] ECS Worker - excessive task definition re-registration when multiple containers are used

prefecthq/prefect#20174.body:
## Bug Description

When using `GcsBucket` with `bucket_folder` configured and running tasks on remote Ray workers, results are stored with double-nested paths:
- Expected: `gs://bucket/folder/file`
- Actual: `gs://bucket/folder/folder/file`

## Root Cause

1. When context is serialized to remote Ray workers via `model_dump()`, `Block.model_validate()` strips `_block_document_id`
2. Without `_block_document_id`, `result_storage_block_id` becomes `null`
3. Prefect `create_result_record()` adds `bucket_folder` to `storage_key` when `storage_block_id` is null
4. `GcsBucket.write_path()` calls `_resolve_path()` which adds `bucket_folder` **again**

## The Fix

`_join_bucket_folder` already has a duplicate check, but `_resolve_path` does not:

```python
# Current _resolve_path (no check):
def _resolve_path(self, path: str) -> str:
    path = str(PurePosixPath(self.bucket_folder, path)) if self.bucket_folder else path
    return path

# Fixed _resolve_path (with duplicate check):
def _resolve_path(self, path: str) -> str:
    if self.bucket_folder and path.startswith(self.bucket_folder):
        return path  # Already prefixed, do not double!
    return str(PurePosixPath(self.bucket_folder, path)) if self.bucket_folder else path
```

## Minimal Reproduction

```python
from prefect import flow, task
from prefect_ray import RayTaskRunner

REMOTE_RAY = "ray://your-ray-cluster:10001"
STORAGE_BLOCK = "gcs-bucket/my-block"  # bucket_folder: "results"

@task(log_prints=True)
def my_task() -> str:
    return "hello"

@flow(
    task_runner=RayTaskRunner(address=REMOTE_RAY),
    result_storage=STORAGE_BLOCK,
    persist_result=True,
)
def my_flow():
    return my_task()  # Result stored at gs://bucket/results/results/... (double-nested!)
```

## Environment

- prefect: 3.6.9 (also tested 3.6.1)
- prefect-gcp: 0.6.12
- prefect-ray: 0.4.5
- Python: 3.10

## Evidence

Files in double-nested path have:
```json
{
  "metadata": {
    "storage_block_id": null,
    "storage_key": "results/abc123..."
  }
}
```

Files in correct path have:
```json
{
  "metadata": {
    "storage_block_id": "010dbcd1-...",
    "storage_key": "abc123..."
  }
}
```
prefecthq/prefect#20174.title: [prefect-gcp] GcsBucket._resolve_path causes double-nesting when storage_block_id is null

prefecthq/prefect#20189.body:
## Summary

As part of #15008 (Synchronous and asynchronous compatibility within Prefect), the `BaseRunInput` class and related classes in `prefect/input/run_input.py` still use the `@sync_compatible` decorator. These should be migrated to the `@async_dispatch` pattern.

## Methods to migrate

The following methods in `run_input.py` use `@sync_compatible`:

- `BaseRunInput.save()` (line 167)
- `BaseRunInput.load()` (line 195)
- `BaseRunInput.respond()` (line 257)
- `BaseRunInput.send_to()` (line 281)
- `AutomaticRunInput.load()` (line 339)
- `GetInputHandler.next()` (line 500)
- `GetAutomaticInputHandler.next()` (line 579)
- `send_input()` (line 627)

## Migration approach

Following the pattern established in other migrations:
1. Create explicit async versions (e.g., `aload()`, `asave()`, `arespond()`, etc.)
2. Convert the current methods to sync versions that use `@async_dispatch`
3. Update tests to use the appropriate sync/async versions

## Context

This issue was identified during #20178 (migrating `pause_flow_run` and `suspend_flow_run`). In that PR, helper functions were created to bypass the `@sync_compatible` methods, but the underlying `BaseRunInput` methods still use the old pattern.

## Related

- Part of #15008
- Follow-up from #20178
prefecthq/prefect#20189.title: Migrate BaseRunInput methods from @sync_compatible to @async_dispatch

When I run `prefect work-pool create --type ecs:push --provision-infra ecs-push-pool` it asks me some questions about the infrastructure to create:

```
Would you like to customize the resource names for your infrastructure? This includes an IAM user, IAM policy, ECS cluster, VPC, ECS security group, and ECR repository. [y/n]: y
? Enter a name for the IAM user (manages ECS tasks) (prefect-ecs-user):

? Enter a name for the ECS cluster (hosts ECS tasks) (prefect-ecs-cluster):

? Enter a name for the ECS security group (controls task network traffic) (prefect-ecs-security-group):

╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮

│ ecs-push-pool2:                                         │
│                                                         │
│ - IAM user: prefect-ecs-user                            │
│ - IAM policy: prefect-ecs-policy                        │
│ - ECS cluster: prefect-ecs-cluster                      │
│ - AWS credentials block: ecs-push-pool-aws-credentials │
│ - VPC: prefect-ecs-vpc                                  │
│ - ECS security group: prefect-ecs-security-group        │
│ - ECR repository: prefect-flows                         │
│                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

It turns out that the `AWS credentials block` item is actually a configuration block in prefect cloud, rather than a resource in my AWS account. The way that this is presented seems a bit confusing and at first I thought it was created in AWS, but I couldn't find it. Perhaps this could be shown separately to indicate that it's part of a different platform

prefecthq/prefect#20246.body:
### Bug summary

This is a more generic version of https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/18476

We want to set ContainerDefinition secrets in our ECS TaskDefinition so that we can load environment variables from Parameter Store and Secrets Manager.  

#18476 suggests updating the task definition in the advanced settings of the work pool. This works, but for me I don't like the solution as I want to override these values on a per-deployment basis (for example a new deployment may have more environment variables that I don't want to set on runs from a previous deployment).

I was also able to make this work by creating my own ECS Task Definition which contains this value and setting and setting the work pool Task Definition Arn, but in this case I needed to hard-code the image name and resource limits, meaning that I can't take advantage of overriding these in the deployment configurations in prefect.yaml. I also see value in using prefect's task definition caching which is something that already exists and I'd probably need to uselessly re-implement myself anyway.

In the case of secrets I see that you're [already special casing a few values here](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/4220e18e56dd79f829601db111f806f580ff3eee/src/integrations/prefect-aws/prefect_aws/workers/ecs_worker.py#L1202-L1228), so the capability is available in the template system, but perhaps as there are multiple people asking for different parameters from the same section it might be a good idea to make it generically configurable.

prefecthq/prefect#20247.body:
## Summary

The `prefect-dbt` integration tests are consistently failing on Python 3.12 across all branches (including main) due to ephemeral Prefect API server connection timeouts

## Error

```
RuntimeError: Timed out while attempting to connect to ephemeral Prefect API server.
```

All 306 tests in the suite fail with this same error at setup (the `prefect_db` session-scoped fixture times out).

## Timeline

- **Last passing on main**: 2026-01-13 ~7:13pm UTC
- **First failures**: Started appearing on 2026-01-14
- **Pattern**: Only Python 3.12 fails; 3.10, 3.11, and 3.13 all pass
- **Failure rate**: 93 out of 100 recent workflow runs have failed

## Affected workflow

- **Workflow**: Integrations Packages Tests  
- **Job**: Run Tests for prefect-dbt on Python 3.12
- **Example run (main)**: https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/actions/runs/21004706995


## Notes

- The ephemeral server startup/connection logic may have a race condition or resource issue specific to Python 3.12
- pytest-asyncio 1.0.0 is in use with `asyncio_default_fixture_loop_scope=session`
- The timeout is 30 seconds, which should be plenty for server startup
prefecthq/prefect#20247.title: prefect-dbt integration tests failing on Python 3.12 - ephemeral server timeout

prefecthq/prefect#20271.body:
### Bug summary

For cancelled tasks, retrying them on the Prefect UI doesn't actually work. After clicking the retry button, the page shows a success message "Retrying run" without any errors, but the API returns an error:

```
{
    "state": {
        "id": "019bc600-2cc9-7379-8863-ae9a8cfc7974",
        "type": "CANCELLED",
        "name": "Cancelled",
        "timestamp": "2026-01-16T08:50:58.889293Z",
        "message": "Flow run was cancelled successfully.",
        "data": null,
        "state_details": {
            "flow_run_id": "5d5a5231-f423-456c-bca2-9ba793bebbf3",
            "task_run_id": null,
            "child_flow_run_id": null,
            "scheduled_time": null,
            "cache_key": null,
            "cache_expiration": null,
            "deferred": false,
            "untrackable_result": false,
            "pause_timeout": null,
            "pause_reschedule": false,
            "pause_key": null,
            "run_input_keyset": null,
            "refresh_cache": null,
            "retriable": null,
            "transition_id": "77be3a98-cd4e-40be-9eea-157d4ce83b88",
            "task_parameters_id": null,
            "traceparent": null,
            "deployment_concurrency_lease_id": null
        }
    },
    "status": "REJECT",
    "details": {
        "type": "reject_details",
        "reason": "Cannot transition flows that are cancelling to a state other than Cancelled."
    }
}
```

After investigation, I discovered this is because the `EnforceCancellingToCancelledTransition` rule's `FROM_STATES = {StateType.CANCELLED, StateType.CANCELLING}` includes `CANCELLED`, but I think this is a bug, because

1. Based on its name, `EnforceCancellingToCancelledTransition` expresses that a cancelling state cannot transition to a cancelled state, but does not indicate that a cancelled state cannot transition to awaiting retry.
2. When the status is cancelled in the UI, there is a retry button available.
3. [The unit tests only cover cases where the initial state is cancelling.](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/89fc3d4a3330bac5cf5678d18ddb395720d0b2a6/tests/server/orchestration/test_core_policy.py#L3100)

What are your thoughts on this?

prefecthq/prefect#20297.body:
### Bug summary

### Description
The `run_dbt_build` function in `prefect_dbt.cli.commands` has the `@sync_compatible` and `@task` decorators applied in the wrong order. This causes the function to lose its identity as a Prefect Task, resulting in an `AttributeError` when trying to call task methods like `.with_options()`.

---
### Minimal Reproduction
Running the following code snippet results in a failure:

```python
from prefect_dbt.cli.commands import run_dbt_build

# Attempting to configure the task options
configured_task = run_dbt_build.with_options(retries=3)
```

---
### Actual Behavior
The code raises the following exception because the object returned is a standard function, not a Task object:

```
AttributeError: 'function' object has no attribute 'with_options'
```

---
### Expected Behavior
Calling `.with_options()` on `run_dbt_build` (or other CLI commands) should return a new task with the specified options, similar to how standard Prefect tasks function.

---
### Root Cause
In `prefect_dbt/cli/commands.py`, the `@sync_compatible` decorator is placed outside the `@task` decorator.

When `@sync_compatible` is the outermost decorator, it wraps the Task object and returns a regular function wrapper to handle async/sync compatibility. Consequently, the underlying Task object methods (like `.with_options()`, `.map()`, etc.) are not accessible on the decorated function.

#### Current (Incorrect) Implementation
```python
# ~lines 403-404 in prefect_dbt/cli/commands.py
@sync_compatible  # <--- Outer decorator hides the Task object
@task             # <--- Inner decorator
async def run_dbt_build(...):
```

#### Reference (Correct) Implementation
For comparison, `trigger_dbt_cli_command` works correctly because the order is reversed:
```python
# ~lines 23-25
@task             # <--- Outer decorator returns the Task object
@sync_compatible  # <--- Inner decorator
async def trigger_dbt_cli_command(...):
```

---
### Suggested Fix
Swap the decorator order so that `@task` is the outermost decorator.

```python
# Fixed Implementation
@task
@sync_compatible
async def run_dbt_build(...):
```
---
### Affected Functions
The following functions appear to have the incorrect decorator ordering and need to be fixed:

- [ ] run_dbt_build (line ~403)
- [ ] run_dbt_test
- [ ] run_dbt_snapshot
- [ ] run_dbt_seed
- [ ] run_dbt_run
- [ ] run_dbt_source_freshness


### Version info

### Bug summary

I’m trying to run the Prefect self-hosted server using an IPv6 address, but it only accepts IPv4. This stops me from using it on an IPv6-only network.

Steps to Reproduce:

- Install Prefect self-hosted server (e.g., with Docker).
- Set the environment variable PREFECT_SERVER_API_HOST=:: to use IPv6.
- Start the server.

Expected Behavior:
The server should start and listen on the IPv6 address (::) so it can be accessed over IPv6.

prefecthq/prefect#20372.body:
Fixes a security vulnerability where the `/api/events/in` WebSocket endpoint bypasses authentication even when `PREFECT_SERVER_API_AUTH_STRING` is configured.

## Changes

**Server-side** (`src/prefect/server/api/events.py`): 
- Always require the "prefect" subprotocol and auth handshake via `subscriptions.accept_prefect_socket()`
- Removed conditional auth logic - authentication flow is now consistent with other WebSocket endpoints

**Client-side** (`src/prefect/events/clients.py`):
- Always connect with "prefect" subprotocol
- Always send auth message (token can be None when auth is not configured)
- Use `get_current_settings().api.auth_string` instead of `PREFECT_API_AUTH_STRING.value()`
- Updated both `PrefectEventsClient` and `PrefectEventSubscriber` classes

**Tests** (`tests/events/server/gateway/test_gateway_in.py`): Add comprehensive authentication tests covering:
- Subprotocol requirement (always required)
- Auth message requirement (always required as first message)
- Invalid token rejection
- Missing token rejection
- Successful event streaming with and without auth configured

**Test fixtures** (`src/prefect/testing/fixtures.py`): Update `events_server` fixture to:
- Always require auth handshake (matching server behavior)
- Accept "prefect" subprotocol via `select_subprotocol` callback

## Breaking Change

This is a breaking change for clients that don't send the auth handshake. The "prefect" subprotocol and auth message are now always required, even when `PREFECT_SERVER_API_AUTH_STRING` is not configured. When auth is not configured, the server accepts any token (including None).

## Review Checklist for Humans
- [ ] Verify server always uses `accept_prefect_socket()` (no conditional fallback)
- [ ] Verify client always sends auth message with token (even if None)
- [ ] Confirm `PrefectCloudEventsClient` still works (inherits from `PrefectEventsClient`)
- [ ] Verify unauthenticated connections are rejected with appropriate error codes

closes OSS-7548

### Checklist

- [x] This pull request references any related issue by including "closes `<link to issue>`"
- [x] If this pull request adds new functionality, it includes unit tests that cover the changes
- [ ] If this pull request removes docs files, it includes redirect settings in `mint.json`.
- [ ] If this pull request adds functions or classes, it includes helpful docstrings.

Link to Devin run: https://app.devin.ai/sessions/944c7cadf6b74d188ccde99d5a4ac575
Requested by: @desertaxle

prefecthq/prefect#20381.body:
### Bug summary

When using `prefect_redis.messaging` for background services, task run state updates show ~15 minute delays due to a race condition in `prefect_redis/ordering.py` between concurrent async coroutines processing related events.

**Key finding:** The race occurs when multiple background-services replicas process related events concurrently. The Redis consumer group distributes events without partitioning by task-run-id, allowing leader and follower events to be processed by different replicas in parallel.

### Observed Behavior

1. Flow completes successfully (flow run shows "Completed")
2. Task run shows intermediate state (e.g., "AwaitingRetry") in UI
3. After exactly 15 minutes, task run state updates to "Completed"
4. All events ARE in the events table with correct timestamps
5. Reproduced with 2+ background-services replicas

### Root Cause Investigation

The race condition occurs in `CausalOrdering.preceding_event_confirmed()` when two related events (leader and follower) are processed across replicas.

### Race Window

The race occurs between two operations on different replicas:

1. **Leader replica:** `get_followers(leader_event)` - SMEMBERS from Redis
2. **Follower replica:** `record_follower(follower_event)` - SADD to Redis

The sequence `SET seen → DEL processing → SMEMBERS followers` is **not atomic**. Between `DEL processing` and `SMEMBERS`, a different replica processing the follower event can:

1. Check `EXISTS seen:{leader}` → False (stale read or timing)
2. Check `EXISTS processing:{leader}` → False
3. Decide the leader is unavailable
4. Call `SADD followers:{leader}` to record itself as waiting
5. But the leader's `SMEMBERS` to get followers already returned empty

### Timeline

We instrumented `prefect_redis/ordering.py` with detailed logging and captured this race:

**Events:**
- `df5d4810` = AwaitingRetry event (leader)
- `198882a8` = Retrying event (follower)

**Timeline from instrumented logs (2 replica deployment):**

```
TIME       | OPERATION                                      | DETAILS
---------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------
.818        | START AwaitingRetry                     | Begin processing df5d4810
.821        | SET processing:df5d4810             | Acquired lock
.823       | Leader check passed                     | Proceeding with handler
.825       | START Retrying                              | Begin processing 198882a8 (CONCURRENT!)
.826       | SET seen:df5d4810                       | AwaitingRetry marks itself SEEN
.826       | SET processing:198882a8            | Retrying acquired lock
.827       | Retrying checks leader                  | WAIT_FOR_LEADER_START for df5d4810
.828       | DEL processing:df5d4810             | AwaitingRetry releases lock
.829       | GET_FOLLOWERS df5d4810         | AwaitingRetry calls SMEMBERS
.830       | EXISTS seen:df5d4810 = FALSE   | Retrying checks (STALE READ!)
.831        | SMEMBERS → EMPTY []                | AwaitingRetry finds no followers
.832       | EXISTS processing:df5d4810=FALSE | Retrying checks - already deleted!
.832       | ORPHAN DECISION                        | Retrying decides leader unavailable
.833       | RECORD_FOLLOWER_START       | Retrying begins SADD pipeline
.839      | SADD COMPLETE                           | Retrying added - TOO LATE! (8ms after SMEMBERS)
```

**Result:** Retrying event is orphaned in the waitlist until `get_lost_followers()` recovers it after 15 minutes.

### Reproduction

1. Deploy Prefect server with Redis messaging:

Note: All ENVs set for prefect_redis causal ordering across both server and background services:
```
- name: PREFECT_API_DATABASE_MIGRATE_ON_START
  value: "false"
- name: PREFECT_MESSAGING_BROKER
  value: prefect_redis.messaging
- name: PREFECT_MESSAGING_CACHE
  value: prefect_redis.messaging
- name: PREFECT_SERVER_EVENTS_CAUSAL_ORDERING
  value: prefect_redis.ordering
- name: PREFECT_SERVER_CONCURRENCY_LEASE_STORAGE
  value: prefect_redis.lease_storage
- name: PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_HOST
  value: <HOST>
- name: PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_PORT
  value: "6379"
- name: PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_SSL
  value: "true"
- name: PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_DB
  value: "0"
- name: PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_USERNAME
  value: <USER>
- name: PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_PASSWORD
  value: <PW>
- name: PREFECT_SERVER_DOCKET_URL
  value: <constructed from above>
```

```yaml
backgroundServices:
  runAsSeparateDeployment: true
  replicaCount: 2  # or more
  messaging:
    broker: prefect_redis.messaging
    cache: prefect_redis.messaging
    ...
```

2. Run 20+ (typically seen at least 1 time within 20-30 flow runs) flow runs with tasks that have retries or rapid state transitions:

```python
from prefect import flow, task
import random

@task(retries=2, retry_delay_seconds=0)
def flaky_task():
    if random.random() < 0.7:
        raise ValueError("Simulated failure")
    return "success"

@flow
def test_flow():
    return flaky_task()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    for _ in range(10):
        test_flow()
```

3. Observe task runs stuck in intermediate states while flow shows COMPLETED
4. Wait ~15 minutes - states eventually update via slow recovery path

### Evidence from Redis

After reproducing (task-run `019bec73-899e-71d7-a49a-c777b88027af` stuck at AwaitingRetry), Redis shows:

```
Pending (3031bb02):      seen=1, followers=[]                        # processed
Running (967b8d72):      seen=1, followers=[]                       # processed
AwaitingRetry (eb034606): seen=1, followers=['Retrying']  # ORPHANED FOLLOWER
Retrying (09e65ddd):     seen=0, followers=['Completed']  # NOT PROCESSED
Completed (f65a774c):    seen=0, followers=[]                     # NOT PROCESSED

Waitlist: [
  ('019bec73-8ea7-77d8-be53-95e209e65ddd', 1769198554.81),  # Retrying
  ('019bec73-8fb7-7e3a-bb7e-ffd2f65a774c', 1769198555.07)        # Completed
]
```

**Explanation:**
1. AwaitingRetry was processed (seen=1) and called `SMEMBERS` -> returned empty
2. Retrying arrived, checked AwaitingRetry: `seen=False`, `processing=False` -> did SADD
3. Retrying's SADD completed AFTER AwaitingRetry's SMEMBERS
4. Retrying is now orphaned in waitlist, with Completed stuck behind it
5. Both will recover after 15 minutes via `get_lost_followers()`

### Version info

```Text
Version:            3.6.12
Helm chart version: 2026.1.20181753
Image tag:          3.6.12-python3.11
Python version:     3.11.14
OS/Arch:            linux/aarch64
```

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#20381.title: Task run recorder redis operations race condition causes eventual consistency fallback on UI

prefecthq/prefect#20416.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Environment variable PREFECT_API_URL includes `/api` prefix:
`PREFECT_API_URL=http://127.0.0.1:4200/api` 
https://docs.prefect.io/v3/how-to-guides/configuration/manage-settings

API documentation also includes '/api' prefix
https://docs.prefect.io/v3/api-ref/rest-api/server/flow-runs/create-flow-run 
`POST /api/flow_runs/`

So logical concatenation of both produces **wrong** URL:
`f"{PREFECT_API_URL}{endpoint_path}" == "http://127.0.0.1:4200/api/api/flow_runs/"`

### Describe the proposed behavior

So probably makes sense to exclude `/api` prefix from endpoint documentation or exclude it from `PREFECT_API_URL` convention.

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#20416.title: Inconsistent URL documentation

prefecthq/prefect#20424.body:
## Description

When a task runs outside of a flow, `prefect.runtime.flow_run.id` returns the string `"None"` instead of Python's `None`.

## Reproduction

```python
from prefect import task
from prefect.runtime import flow_run

@task
def my_task():
    frid = flow_run.id
    print(f"flow_run.id value: {frid!r}")
    print(f"Is string 'None': {frid == 'None'}")
    return frid

if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = my_task()
    # Expected: None
    # Actual: 'None' (the string)
```

Output:
```
flow_run.id value: 'None'
flow_run.id type:  <class 'str'>
Is None:           False
Is string 'None':  True
BUG CONFIRMED: flow_run.id is the string 'None' instead of None
```

## Root Cause

In `src/prefect/runtime/flow_run.py`, the `get_id()` function unconditionally calls `str()` on `task_run_ctx.task_run.flow_run_id` (line 142):

```python
def get_id() -> Optional[str]:
    flow_run_ctx = FlowRunContext.get()
    task_run_ctx = TaskRunContext.get()
    if flow_run_ctx is not None:
        return str(flow_run_ctx.flow_run.id)
    if task_run_ctx is not None:
        return str(task_run_ctx.task_run.flow_run_id)  # str(None) == "None"
    else:
        return os.getenv("PREFECT__FLOW_RUN_ID")
```

When a task runs outside a flow, `task_run.flow_run_id` is `None`, and `str(None)` produces `"None"`.

The same pattern exists in `src/prefect/logging/loggers.py` line 214:
```python
"flow_run_id": str(task_run.flow_run_id),  # str(None) == "None"
```

## Expected Behavior

`prefect.runtime.flow_run.id` should return `None` (Python's None literal) when there is no flow run context.

## Version

Observed on the latest `main` branch.
prefecthq/prefect#20424.title: prefect.runtime.flow_run.id returns string "None" instead of None when task runs outside a flow

prefecthq/prefect#20523.body:
## Summary

Add CLI commands for basic asset management operations: listing and deleting assets. Since assets are a Cloud-only feature, these commands belong under the `prefect cloud` subgroup.

## Motivation

Currently, assets can only be managed through the UI or by hitting the API directly. Users need CLI support for:
- Scripting and automation workflows
- Cleaning up old/stale assets programmatically  
- Quick inspection of assets in a workspace without opening the UI

## Proposed Commands

### `prefect cloud asset list`

List assets in the workspace.

```bash
prefect cloud asset list                    # List all assets
prefect cloud asset list --limit 50         # Limit results
prefect cloud asset list -o json            # JSON output for scripting
```

### `prefect cloud asset delete`

Delete an asset by key.

```bash
prefect cloud asset delete "s3://bucket/data.csv"           # Delete single asset
prefect cloud asset delete "s3://bucket/data.csv" --force   # Skip confirmation
```

## API Endpoints

The API already supports these operations:
- `GET /assets/` - List assets with pagination
- `DELETE /assets/key/{encoded_key}` - Delete asset by key

## Reference Implementation

A standalone script demonstrating this functionality: https://gist.github.com/zzstoatzz/c8a4ad709876c44cefd40e65ac983485

## Additional Context

This is a first step toward broader CLI support for asset management. Future iterations could add filtering, bulk operations, or additional CRUD commands as needed.
prefecthq/prefect#20523.title: Add `prefect cloud asset list` and `prefect cloud asset delete` CLI commands

prefecthq/prefect#20564.body:
## Summary

`PrefectFutureList.result()` currently processes futures sequentially, which means if future 5 fails immediately but future 0 is slow, you won't see the error until futures 0-4 complete. This wastes time and resources when a failure has already occurred.

## Current Behavior

```python
def result(self, timeout: float | None = None, raise_on_failure: bool = True) -> list[R]:
    # ...
    return [future.result(raise_on_failure=raise_on_failure) for future in self]
```

Sequential iteration means failures aren't surfaced until all preceding futures complete.

## Proposed Change

Update `PrefectFutureList.result()` to use `as_completed()` internally so that:

1. Futures are processed as they complete (not sequentially)
2. Exceptions are raised as soon as they're encountered (fail-fast)
3. Results are still returned in the original order (not completion order)

```python
def result(self, timeout: float | None = None, raise_on_failure: bool = True) -> list[R]:
    try:
        with timeout_context(timeout):
            future_to_index = {f: i for i, f in enumerate(self)}
            results: list[R | None] = [None] * len(self)
            
            for future in as_completed(list(self), timeout=timeout):
                idx = future_to_index[future]
                results[idx] = future.result(raise_on_failure=raise_on_failure)
            
            return results  # type: ignore
    except TimeoutError as exc:
        # existing timeout handling...
```

## Benefits

- **Fail-fast**: Errors surface immediately instead of waiting for slow futures
- **More efficient**: No wasted waiting when something has already failed
- **Backwards compatible**: Results still returned in original order

## Considerations

- Need to handle the edge case of duplicate futures in the list (same future added multiple times)
prefecthq/prefect#20564.title: Make `PrefectFutureList.result()` fail-fast using `as_completed`

### Bug summary

1. The `timeout_seconds_override` attribute in the `TriggerJobRunOptions` class isn't being correctly set when passed into the `trigger_dbt_cloud_job_run_and_wait_for_completion` function.  All jobs timeout at 900 seconds which seems to be set in the `DbtCloudJob` class.
2. The snippet shown below is a class method used that creates a `TriggerJobRunOptions` object that is passed into the `trigger_dbt_cloud_job_run_and_wait_for_completion` function where the `timeout_seconds_override` argument is set to 3600 as a default value for the method.
```python
# imports
from prefect_dbt.cloud import DbtCloudCredentials
from prefect_dbt.cloud.models import TriggerJobRunOptions
from prefect_dbt.cloud.jobs import trigger_dbt_cloud_job_run_and_wait_for_completion

# class method (i can share the full class definition if helpful)
    async def run_job_async(
        self,
        description: str,
        steps_override: list = None,
        timeout_seconds_override: int = 3600,
        trigger_request_kwargs: dict = None,
    ) -> dict:
        """
        Public async method that runs dbt commands specified in the steps_override argument and polls the job status endpoint
        until the job is complete

        Args:
            description (str): description of commands being run
            steps_override (list, optional): list of dbt commands to run. If None, uses the job's pre-configured commands from dbt Cloud. Defaults to None.
            timeout_seconds_override (int, optional): number of seconds before the job times out.  Defaults to 3600 (1 hour).
            trigger_request_kwargs (dict, optional): optional request arguments to be passed to the job trigger api. Defaults to None.

        Returns:
            dict: dict containing the job trigger response
        """

        trigger_request_kwargs = trigger_request_kwargs or {}

        request_body = {
            "cause": description,
            "timeout_seconds_override": timeout_seconds_override,
        }

        # Only include steps_override if it's explicitly provided
        if steps_override is not None:
            request_body["steps_override"] = steps_override

        request_body = request_body | trigger_request_kwargs
        trigger_job_run_options = TriggerJobRunOptions(**request_body)
        
        logger.info(f"Running job - {description}...")
        response = await trigger_dbt_cloud_job_run_and_wait_for_completion(
            dbt_cloud_credentials=self.dbt_cloud_credentials,
            job_id=self.job_id,
            trigger_job_run_options=trigger_job_run_options
        )
        logger.info(f"Job complete")
        
        return response

4. As mentioned in the description, it seems the default timeout is set as 900 seconds in the `DbtCloudJob` class which is located in the `prefect_dbt/cloud/jobs.py` file.  The class definition is shown below for reference.
```python
class DbtCloudJob(JobBlock):
    """
    Block that holds the information and methods to interact with a dbt Cloud job.

    Attributes:
        dbt_cloud_credentials: The credentials to use to authenticate with dbt Cloud.
        job_id: The id of the dbt Cloud job.
        timeout_seconds: The number of seconds to wait for the job to complete.
        interval_seconds:
            The number of seconds to wait between polling for job completion.
        trigger_job_run_options: The options to use when triggering a job run.

    Examples:
        Load a configured dbt Cloud job block.
        ```python
        from prefect_dbt.cloud import DbtCloudJob

        dbt_cloud_job = DbtCloudJob.load("BLOCK_NAME")
        ```

        Triggers a dbt Cloud job, waits for completion, and fetches the results.
        ```python
        from prefect import flow
        from prefect_dbt.cloud import DbtCloudCredentials, DbtCloudJob

        @flow
        def dbt_cloud_job_flow():
            dbt_cloud_credentials = DbtCloudCredentials.load("dbt-token")
            dbt_cloud_job = DbtCloudJob.load(
                dbt_cloud_credentials=dbt_cloud_credentials,
                job_id=154217
            )
            dbt_cloud_job_run = dbt_cloud_job.trigger()
            dbt_cloud_job_run.wait_for_completion()
            dbt_cloud_job_run.fetch_result()
            return dbt_cloud_job_run

        dbt_cloud_job_flow()
        ```
    """

    _block_type_name = "dbt Cloud Job"
    _logo_url = "https://images.ctfassets.net/gm98wzqotmnx/5zE9lxfzBHjw3tnEup4wWL/9a001902ed43a84c6c96d23b24622e19/dbt-bit_tm.png?h=250"  # noqa
    _documentation_url = "https://docs.prefect.io/integrations/prefect-dbt"  # noqa

    dbt_cloud_credentials: DbtCloudCredentials = Field(
        default=...,
        description="The dbt Cloud credentials to use to authenticate with dbt Cloud.",
    )  # noqa: E501
    job_id: int = Field(
        default=..., description="The id of the dbt Cloud job.", title="Job ID"
    )
    timeout_seconds: int = Field(
        default=900,
        description="The number of seconds to wait for the job to complete.",
    )
    interval_seconds: int = Field(
        default=10,
        description="The number of seconds to wait between polling for job completion.",
    )
    trigger_job_run_options: TriggerJobRunOptions = Field(
        default_factory=TriggerJobRunOptions,
        description="The options to use when triggering a job run.",
    )

    @sync_compatible
    async def get_job(self, order_by: Optional[str] = None) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """
        Retrieve information about a dbt Cloud job.

        Args:
            order_by: The field to order the results by.

        Returns:
            The job data.
        """
        try:
            async with self.dbt_cloud_credentials.get_administrative_client() as client:
                response = await client.get_job(
                    job_id=self.job_id,
                    order_by=order_by,
                )
        except HTTPStatusError as ex:
            raise DbtCloudGetJobFailed(extract_user_message(ex)) from ex
        return response.json()["data"]

    @sync_compatible
    async def trigger(
        self, trigger_job_run_options: Optional[TriggerJobRunOptions] = None
    ) -> DbtCloudJobRun:
        """
        Triggers a dbt Cloud job.

        Returns:
            A representation of the dbt Cloud job run.
        """
        try:
            trigger_job_run_options = (
                trigger_job_run_options or self.trigger_job_run_options
            )
            async with self.dbt_cloud_credentials.get_administrative_client() as client:
                response = await client.trigger_job_run(
                    job_id=self.job_id, options=trigger_job_run_options
                )
        except HTTPStatusError as ex:
            raise DbtCloudJobRunTriggerFailed(extract_user_message(ex)) from ex

        run_data = response.json()["data"]
        run_id = run_data.get("id")
        run = DbtCloudJobRun(
            dbt_cloud_job=self,
            run_id=run_id,
        )
        self.logger.info(
            f"dbt Cloud job {self.job_id} run {run_id} successfully triggered. "
            f"You can view the status of this run at "
            f"https://{self.dbt_cloud_credentials.domain}/#/accounts/"
            f"{self.dbt_cloud_credentials.account_id}/projects/"
            f"{run_data['project_id']}/runs/{run_id}/"
        )
        return run
```

prefecthq/prefect#20649.body:
**Related:** #19068 ([comment](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/19068#issuecomment-3885173933))

### Problem

When a `PrefectFuture` is passed as a parameter to a downstream task, Prefect recursively traverses the resolved value via `visit_collection` to discover nested futures, states, and task run inputs for dependency tracking. This traversal is CPU-bound — for large results (large dicts, DataFrames, objects hundreds of MB in size), it blocks the event loop for an extended period. This starves all background work that Prefect runs on the event loop (concurrency lease renewal, heartbeats, etc.) and can cause cascading failures.

The existing `quote` annotation prevents traversal, but it also prevents the wrapped future from being resolved. This forces users to materialize the result themselves before passing it along:

```python
cities_future = load_cities.submit(tk, auth_header)
cities = cities_future.result()  # manually block and resolve
filling_cities = process_cities.submit(conf, quote(cities))
```

This defeats the purpose of deferred execution with `.submit()` and loses the ergonomic future-chaining pattern. The `wait_for` parameter has the same problem — it triggers recursive traversal via `collect_task_run_inputs_sync`, so even explicit dependency declarations don't avoid the expensive walk:

```python
cities = load_cities.submit(tk, auth_header)
# quote prevents traversal of the parameter, but wait_for still traverses cities
filling_cities = process_cities.submit(conf, quote(cities), wait_for=cities)
```

### Proposed solution

Add an `opaque` annotation that resolves the top-level value (e.g., resolves a `PrefectFuture` to its result) but does **not** recursively traverse into the resolved value.

```python
from prefect.utilities.annotations import opaque

cities = load_cities.submit(tk, auth_header)
filling_cities = process_cities.submit(conf, opaque(cities))
```

Semantically:
- **No annotation** — resolve the value and recursively traverse it for nested futures/states (current default)
- **`quote`** — do not resolve, do not traverse (existing)
- **`opaque`** — resolve the top-level value, but do not traverse into its contents

### Implementation notes

`opaque` would be a new `BaseAnnotation` subclass in `src/prefect/utilities/annotations.py`, alongside `quote`, `unmapped`, and `allow_failure`.

The traversal visitors in `src/prefect/utilities/engine.py` (`resolve_inputs`, `resolve_input`, `collect_task_run_inputs_sync`, etc.) and `src/prefect/task_engine.py` (`_wait_for_dependencies`) would need to handle `opaque` by:
1. Resolving the annotated value one level (i.e., if it's a `PrefectFuture`, wait for and return its result)
2. Raising `StopVisiting` to prevent recursion into the resolved value

This gives users a middle ground between full traversal and `quote`'s total bypass, without sacrificing the `.submit()` chaining pattern.
prefecthq/prefect#20649.title: Add `opaque` annotation to prevent recursive traversal of resolved task inputs

prefecthq/prefect#20748.body:
## Description

`prefect-dbt >= 0.7.9` hangs indefinitely when executing a `dbt build` run that includes dbt models with **docs blocks whose individual line lengths exceed 40 characters**. dbt continues executing in the background (e.g., queries run successfully in the data warehouse), but Prefect never receives completion signals and the flow/tasks remain stuck in a `Running` state indefinitely.

The hang does not occur with `prefect-dbt == 0.7.8`, indicating a regression introduced in `0.7.9`.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a dbt model that includes a docs block with at least one line longer than 40 characters, e.g.:
   ```sql
   -- models/my_model.sql
   {% docs my_model %}
   This is a description that exceeds forty characters in length.
   {% enddocs %}
   ```
2. Run a `dbt build` via `prefect-dbt >= 0.7.9`
3. Observe that Prefect hangs indefinitely at the affected model(s)

## Expected Behavior

The flow and tasks complete normally, reflecting dbt's actual execution state.

## Actual Behavior

Prefect hangs indefinitely. dbt continues running in the background but Prefect never receives or processes the completion event. The flow must be manually cancelled.

## Workarounds

- Downgrade to `prefect-dbt == 0.7.8`
- Remove or shorten lines in dbt docs blocks to stay under 40 lines (2500 Characters)

## Version Information

- **prefect-dbt**: >= 0.7.9 (regression; 0.7.8 is unaffected)
- **Prefect**: 3.x

## Additional Context

This appears to be related to how `prefect-dbt >= 0.7.9` reads or parses dbt artifact/node metadata. The 40-character line threshold suggests a possible buffer or parsing limit introduced in that version.
prefecthq/prefect#20748.title: prefect-dbt hangs indefinitely when dbt models contain docs blocks with lines exceeding 2500 characters

prefecthq/prefect#20765.body:
### Bug summary

ECS Managed Instances is a "newish" offering by AWS ([link](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/announcing-amazon-ecs-managed-instances-for-containerized-applications/)). It's similar to ECS on EC2, but there are no instances to manage.

Our workload is such that we start ~1000 sub flowruns every 5-10 minutes (depending on our concurrency settings).

Because ECS Managed Instances starts additional instances in batches and each batch takes 2-5 minutes to start up, eventually the ECS RunTask API starts responding with a failure `RESOURCE:MEMORY` in our case, meaning there is no available capacity left in the ECS cluster.

Looking at the source code, specifically the `@retry` decorator on the `_create_task_run` function ([link](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/integrations/prefect-aws/prefect_aws/workers/ecs_worker.py#L1524-L1531)), there are some constants being used that allow retry of tasks.

What we would need is the ability to customize those numbers and find the sweet spot that would allow us to run the flows without failures.

Numbers in question:
- `MAX_CREATE_TASK_RUN_ATTEMPTS`
- `CREATE_TASK_RUN_MIN_DELAY_SECONDS`
- `CREATE_TASK_RUN_MIN_DELAY_JITTER_SECONDS`
- `CREATE_TASK_RUN_MAX_DELAY_JITTER_SECONDS`

### Version info

```Text
Using the `prefect-aws:0.7.4-python3.14-prefect3.6.16` Docker image on amd64
```

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#20765.title: ECS Worker: running on ECS Managed Instances some flow runs fail to start due to resource constraints

prefecthq/prefect#20864.body:
## Summary
This PR routes subprocess-emitted artifacts through a single parent-side forwarding path in `ProcessPoolTaskRunner`:

- forwards `emit_event(...)` calls from subprocess workers to the parent `EventsWorker`
- forwards API logs from subprocess workers to the parent `APILogWorker`
- initializes worker processes with queue-backed forwarding sinks
- uses one subprocess message queue/thread in the parent to dispatch both events and API logs

This improves the per-tak performance by removing the need for an `EventWorker` and `LogWorker` to start in each process.

Implementation details:
- adds `ProcessPoolForwardingEventsClient` and `EventsWorker.set_client_override(...)`
- adds `set_api_log_sink(...)` / `emit_api_log(...)` and wires `APILogHandler` through them
- updates `ProcessPoolTaskRunner` worker initializer and parent forwarder to handle typed queue messages (`event` / `log`)
- fixes a forwarder shutdown race that caused intermittent `PytestUnhandledThreadExceptionWarning` failures in task-runner CI

## Benchmark context
These measurements are from a targeted **log-heavy** local harness intended to stress subprocess->parent forwarding overhead (not a representative end-to-end flow benchmark):

- harness shape: `layers=5`, `width=10` (50 task runs total)
- each task emits `100` API log records (`5000` records total)
- runner config: `ProcessPoolTaskRunner(max_workers=10)`
- protocol: `1` warmup run + `3` measured runs per branch
- comparison method: same machine, sequentially run `main` then candidate

Results:

| Branch | Runs (s) | Median (s) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `main` | `2.8027, 2.7769, 2.8254` | `2.8027` |
| this PR | `2.2026, 2.0086, 1.9545` | `2.0086` |

Interpretation:
- In this log-saturated shape, median runtime improved by ~`28%`.
- In lighter/noisier workloads, the delta was smaller and less stable.
prefecthq/prefect#20864.title: Route `ProcessPoolTaskRunner` subprocess events/logs through parent queue

prefecthq/prefect#20887.body: ### Bug summary I am investigating some false-positive triggers of the "Zombie flow" automation in our Prefect Cloud instance. We are using the "zombie" automation as defined in the docs [here](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/advanced/detect-zombie-flows#create-the-automation), with a minor adjustment of setting `within=timedelta(seconds=150)` (2.5mins, rather than the 90s in the docs). We are seeing the zombie automation being triggered, inconsistently, in undesired scenarios. Consider for example the following two flows runs. These are two scheduled runs of the same flow deployment, half an hour apart: You can see in both cases that the flow runs to completion. The logs and events appear in the same order, and in the same structure. However, after completion (and exactly 2.5mins (`within=timedelta(seconds=150)`) after the first heartbeat), you can see that the zombie flow automation runs against the flow run in the left-hand panel (in this particular case the automation fails, as it cannot change the state since the flow run is COMPLETED). The automation doesn't run against the same flow run 30mins later (right-hand flow run in screenshot). We are also seeing unexpected triggers of this automation for longer flow runs, where the flow run is transitioned to CRASHED but keeps running, and its state eventually changes back to RUNNING. ### Version info ```Text Version:              3.6.15 API version:          0.8.4 Python version:       3.10.19 Git commit:           757a089b Built:                Fri, Jan 30, 2026 12:27 AM OS/Arch:              linux/x86_64 Profile:              local Server type:          cloud Pydantic version:     2.12.5 Server:  Database:           sqlite  SQLite version:     3.46.1 Integrations:  prefect-dbt:        0.7.16  prefect-shell:      0.3.1  prefect-dask:       0.3.6 ``` ### Additional context Potentially related to: https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/14692 and https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/18838
prefecthq/prefect#20887.title: Zombie flow automation false-positives

prefecthq/prefect#20901.body:
### Bug summary


I am using the Python Class to write and configure my deployments. I noticed a mismatch between what's doable in python and what seems to be doable with YAML/JSON. Given the documentation [here](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/concepts/event-triggers#example), specially this section:

"""
match_related also supports an array of objects, each of which can contain multiple labels. This allows for more complex matching on related resources, such as filtering for events that are related to a specific work pool or tag.
"""

However, the python class only accept for the input `match_related` the class `ResourceSpecification`, which itself only takes one argument, root, and is of type `Dict[str, str | List[str]]` (Subclass of pydantic RootModel)

Then, it is impossible to configure a rule that will need to match all tag of a set of tags to fire. The possible list, value of the root dictionnary, seems to be a logical OR between all elements that compose the list, according to what I have tested.

### Version info

```Text
## Environment

prefect-server: 3.6.19
postgres backend, v18
self hosted docker setup
reproduced on arch linux
```

### Additional context


I'm building a multi-tenant event driven ELT pipeline. I want to fire subset of computation in [dbt](https://www.getdbt.com) based on tags, with tags defined in prefect matching tags defined in dbt, all targeting the same conceptual component. I have defined my own event grammar for this use case.

Here's the deployer I'm using:
```python
DeploymentEventTrigger(
    name=f"dbt-reactive-{provider}-{tenant_name.lower()}",
    description=f"Trigger on {chosen_threshold_value} {provider} events",
    type="event",
    posture=Posture.Reactive,
    expect={f"elt.{provider}.*"},
    for_each={f"prefect.resource.id"},
    threshold=chosen_threshold_value,
    within=timedelta(seconds=1200),
    match=ResourceSpecification(
        {"prefect.resource.id": f"elt.{provider}.*"}
    ),
    match_related=ResourceSpecification(
        {"prefect.resource.id": [f"prefect.tag.bronze", f"prefect.tag.{tenant_name.lower()}"]},
    ),
    parameters={
        "tenant_id": tenant_id,
        "config": {
            "commands": ["seed", "snapshot", "run"],
            "selects": [f"+tag:{provider}+"],
        }
    },
    schedule_after=timedelta(
        minutes=20
)
```

This trigger is part of a for loop, each provider being an independent source of data. The objective is, when sufficient amount of bronze-flows-events for that specific provider and that specific tenant are emitted, I fire the trigger. Currently, it's not working, a tenant A can fire the trigger for an tenant B, because the list is a treated as a OR. If I can define the following:
```python
match_related = [
    {"prefect.resource.id": "prefect.tag.bronze"},
    {"prefect.resource.id": f"prefect.tag.{provider}"},
]
```
The problem should disappear.

I'm probably doing something wrong here, following some anti-pattern. Although I'd appreciate some help for this, the main point is to give some context on why I would need this and to point out the mismatch between the doc and the python implementation.

Switching to yaml is not really an option, as I define these triggers dynamically based on my database. I don't really want to glue code something with jinja, specially when prefect propose the wonderful feature to write IaC in python.

Happy to provide additional information!
prefecthq/prefect#20901.title: match_related attribute of class DeploymentEventTrigger doesn't accept the type List[Dict], but the docs suggest it should

```python
@flow
def main() -> list[str]:

    return results

main.serve(
        name="my-first-deployment",

)

prefecthq/prefect#21031.body:
### Description

`QueueService._queue` (the base class for `EventsWorker`) uses `queue.Queue()` with no `maxsize`:

```python
# src/prefect/_internal/concurrency/services.py:62
self._queue: queue.Queue[Optional[T]] = queue.Queue()  # no maxsize
```

During transient Prefect server outages, events are produced by flows/tasks faster than the `EventsWorker` can drain them. Since the queue has no upper bound, it grows without limit and can eventually OOM the worker process.

### Use Case

We run long-running batch ingest jobs (10+ hours per job, processing thousands of files). During a server restart or brief outage, every task completion, state change, and concurrency event continues to be enqueued. On a large job this can mean thousands of events accumulating per minute with no backpressure.

We currently work around this by disabling event emission entirely via monkey-patching (`should_emit_events = lambda: False`), but this is a blunt instrument — ideally we'd keep events enabled with a safety net against unbounded growth.

### Proposed Solution

Add a `maxsize` parameter to `QueueService` (or specifically to `EventsWorker`) with a sensible default and a configurable setting:

```python
# Option A: configurable via settings
self._queue: queue.Queue[Optional[T]] = queue.Queue(
    maxsize=PREFECT_EVENTS_QUEUE_MAX_SIZE.value()  # default e.g. 10000
)
```

When the queue is full, new events could be dropped with a warning log rather than blocking the caller:

```python
def send(self, item: T) -> None:
    with self._lock:
        if self._stopped:
            raise RuntimeError("Cannot put items in a stopped service instance.")
        try:
            self._queue.put_nowait(self._prepare_item(item))
        except queue.Full:
            logger.warning(
                "EventsWorker queue is full (%d items), dropping event",
                self._queue.qsize(),
            )
```

This provides backpressure without blocking flow/task execution.

### Impact

This affects any self-hosted Prefect deployment where the server may experience transient unavailability. Cloud users may also be affected during API outages, though the impact is likely less severe due to shorter job durations.

### Additional Context

Related to the `PREFECT_EMIT_EVENTS` setting proposal — that allows disabling events entirely, while this provides a safety net for when events are enabled but the server is temporarily unreachable.

Happy to submit a PR for this.
prefecthq/prefect#21031.title: EventsWorker uses unbounded queue — can cause OOM during server outages

prefecthq/prefect#21083.body:
### Bug summary

### Bug summary

In a self-hosted Prefect 3.6.18 deployment backed by PostgreSQL, restarting PostgreSQL without restarting `prefect-server-background-services` appears to leave the reactive trigger service with stale or missing automation listener state.

Automations created or redeployed after the PostgreSQL restart exist in the database and their trigger definitions match incoming events, but they do not trigger actions or create flow runs until the `prefect-server-background-services` pod is restarted.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Start Prefect Server with PostgreSQL.
2. Ensure `prefect-server-background-services` is running.
3. Restart PostgreSQL.
4. Do **not** restart any Prefect pods.
5. Create or redeploy one or more reactive automations after PostgreSQL comes back.
6. Emit events that should match those automations.
7. Observe that the events are persisted but the automations do not fire.
8. Restart `prefect-server-background-services`.
9. Observe that newly created automations begin working again.

### Expected behavior

Reactive automations created or updated after a PostgreSQL restart should be picked up automatically by the running trigger service and should fire for matching events.

### Actual behavior

Reactive automations created or updated after the PostgreSQL restart were present in the DB, enabled, and had matching events in the `events` table, but they did not create any flow runs or actions until the background-services pod was restarted.

### Validation from `pg_stat_activity`

We validated this at the PostgreSQL level.

In the broken staging environment, we checked `pg_stat_activity` for the running `prefect-server-background-services` pod and found **no active `LISTEN "prefect_automation_changes"` session** from that pod.

For comparison, in a healthy production environment we *did* observe an active session in `pg_stat_activity` for the background-services pod with:

`LISTEN "prefect_automation_changes"`

This strongly suggests that, in the broken case, the automation `LISTEN/NOTIFY` loop was no longer active in the running background-services process after the PostgreSQL restart.

### This looks like a server-side cache/listener issue

Reactive automations are kept in an in-memory trigger registry and incoming events are matched only against that in-memory set.

On PostgreSQL, automation create/update/delete uses `NOTIFY`, and the trigger service listens for those changes via `LISTEN`.

This suggests a failure mode where:
- PostgreSQL restarts
- the trigger service loses or misses automation change notifications
- the in-memory trigger cache is never fully reconciled afterward
- newly created automations remain in the DB but are not loaded into the running trigger service

The `pg_stat_activity` validation above appears consistent with that explanation.

### Suspected fix direction

After the PostgreSQL `LISTEN/NOTIFY` connection errors or reconnects, the trigger service should probably do a full automation reconciliation / reload from the database instead of relying only on future notifications.

A `reset()` + `load_automations(...)` on reconnect may be sufficient, or some other full resync mechanism.

### Additional notes

I did not find an exact duplicate issue, but this looked related:
https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/19080

### Version info

```Text
Prefect server: 3.6.18
API version: 0.8.4
Python: 3.14.3
Deployment: Kubernetes / self-hosted Prefect Server
Database: PostgreSQL
Trigger service running in separate prefect-server-background-services deployment
```

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#21083.title: Automations created after PostgreSQL restart are not triggered until `prefect-server-background-services` is restarted

prefecthq/prefect#21109.body:
### Describe the current behavior

Prefect currently only supports trailing wildcards while matching resources with an `EventTrigger` ([docs](https://docs.prefect.io/v3/concepts/event-triggers#event-triggers), [code](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/447059a67fdbc3ccf1fbbb7c1bf19d09bab7b7b2/src/prefect/server/events/schemas/events.py#L250-L267)).

This limits the capabilities of automations, especially for asset materializations, because the following would not work:

```python3
import prefect.events.schemas.automations as automation_schema


automation_schema.EventTrigger(
    expect={"prefect.asset.materialization.succeeded"},
    match={"prefect.resource.id": "*.parquet"},
    for_each={"prefect.resource.id"},
)
```

### Describe the proposed behavior

`EventTrigger.match` should work with expressions that start with leading wildcards.

### Example Use

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_
prefecthq/prefect#21109.title: Support leading wildcards in resource matching of event triggers

prefecthq/prefect#21117.body:
### Bug summary

When a task submitted with `wait_for=[upstream_future]` runs under `ProcessPoolTaskRunner`, it executes even if the upstream task failed. Under `ThreadPoolTaskRunner`, the downstream task never runs.  I suspect that the `ThreadPoolTaskRunner` behavior is correct, but I'm not totally sure -- I didn't see it specified in the documentation.

So I'd suggest doing the following:
1. make the behavior uniform across task runners
2. document the behavior of a task submitted with `wait_for` in the case when any upstream task fails.

Here's an example that reproduces the issue:
```python
from prefect import flow
from prefect import task
from prefect.task_runners import ProcessPoolTaskRunner


@task
def failing_task() -> str:
  raise RuntimeError("I failed!")


@task
def downstream_task() -> str:
  print("downstream task started!")
  return "downstream completed"


@flow(task_runner=ProcessPoolTaskRunner())  # type: ignore[no-matching-overload]
def repro_processpool() -> None:
  upstream = failing_task.submit()
  downstream = downstream_task.submit(wait_for=[upstream])
  print(f"upstream state:   {upstream.state}")
  print(f"downstream state: {downstream.state}")


@flow
def repro_threadpool() -> None:
  upstream = failing_task.submit()
  downstream = downstream_task.submit(wait_for=[upstream])
  print(f"upstream state:   {upstream.state}")
  print(f"downstream state: {downstream.state}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
  print("=== ThreadPoolTaskRunner (correct: downstream should be NotReady) ===")
  repro_threadpool()
  print(
    "\n=== ProcessPoolTaskRunner (bug: downstream runs despite upstream failure) ==="
  )
  repro_processpool()
```

Here's the full output that I see when I run it:
```
=== ThreadPoolTaskRunner (correct: downstream should be NotReady) ===

upstream state:   Pending()
downstream state: Pending()
11:49:08.595 | ERROR   | Task run 'failing_task-a7d' - Task run failed with exception: RuntimeError('I failed!')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/viraj/code/ml2-local__worktrees/viraj-devops-prefect-failed-controller/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/prefect/task_engine.py", line 1014, in run_context
    yield self
  File "/home/viraj/code/ml2-local__worktrees/viraj-devops-prefect-failed-controller/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/prefect/task_engine.py", line 1673, in run_task_sync
    engine.call_task_fn(txn)
  File "/home/viraj/code/ml2-local__worktrees/viraj-devops-prefect-failed-controller/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/prefect/task_engine.py", line 1031, in call_task_fn
    result = call_with_parameters(self.task.fn, parameters)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/viraj/code/ml2-local__worktrees/viraj-devops-prefect-failed-controller/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/prefect/utilities/callables.py", line 210, in call_with_parameters
    return fn(*args, **kwargs)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

prefecthq/prefect#21119.body:
## Summary

The base worker's `prepare_for_flow_run()` populates `self.labels` with Prefect metadata (flow run ID, flow name, deployment ID, etc.), but the Cloud Run workers in `prefect-gcp` never apply these labels to the Cloud Run job body. This makes it difficult to correlate Cloud Run logs in GCP Cloud Logging with Prefect flow runs.

## Current behavior

The base `JobConfiguration.prepare_for_flow_run()` in `prefect/workers/base.py` assembles labels including:

```python
self.labels = {
    "prefect.io/flow-run-id": str(flow_run.id),
    "prefect.io/flow-run-name": flow_run.name,
    "prefect.io/version": prefect.__version__,
    # + deployment, flow, work pool labels
    ...
}
```

Both `CloudRunWorkerJobV2Configuration.prepare_for_flow_run()` ([cloud_run_v2.py](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/integrations/prefect-gcp/prefect_gcp/workers/cloud_run_v2.py)) and `CloudRunWorkerJobConfiguration.prepare_for_flow_run()` ([cloud_run.py](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/blob/main/src/integrations/prefect-gcp/prefect_gcp/workers/cloud_run.py)) call `super().prepare_for_flow_run()`, so `self.labels` is populated — but neither worker writes those labels into the `job_body` sent to GCP.

As a result, Cloud Run job logs in GCP Cloud Logging have no Prefect metadata labels, making it hard to trace logs back to specific flow runs, deployments, or flows.

## Expected behavior

The Cloud Run workers should inject `self.labels` into the Cloud Run job body, similar to how the Kubernetes worker applies them to K8s Job manifests. This would make Prefect metadata available as GCP resource labels, enabling Cloud Logging queries like:

```
resource.labels.job_name="green-coati"
labels."prefect-flow-run-id"="069b2be6-88cf-7155-8000-fb8956c7ebf3"
```

## Implementation notes

GCP labels have stricter naming rules than Kubernetes labels — keys must be lowercase letters, digits, underscores, and hyphens only (no dots or slashes). The `prefect.io/` namespace used by the base worker would need to be sanitized, e.g.:

| Base worker label key | GCP-compatible key |
|---|---|
| `prefect.io/flow-run-id` | `prefect-io-flow-run-id` |
| `prefect.io/flow-run-name` | `prefect-io-flow-run-name` |
| `prefect.io/deployment-id` | `prefect-io-deployment-id` |

GCP label values are also limited to 63 characters of lowercase alphanumerics, hyphens, and underscores. UUIDs fit within this constraint, but flow run names and deployment names may need truncation.

The fix would be a `_populate_labels()` method (or similar) in each worker's `prepare_for_flow_run()`:

- **V2**: `self.job_body["labels"] = sanitized_labels`
- **V1**: `self.job_body["metadata"]["labels"] = sanitized_labels`

## Motivation

Without these labels, correlating Prefect flow runs with their Cloud Run execution logs requires manually matching the Cloud Run job name (which is the Prefect flow run name, e.g. `green-coati`) via the Prefect API. Adding labels would enable direct filtering in Cloud Logging and programmatic correlation between the two systems.
prefecthq/prefect#21119.title: Cloud Run workers should inject Prefect labels onto Cloud Run jobs

prefecthq/prefect#21130.body:
Currently `run_shell_script` splits commands with `shlex.split` and passes the result as a list to the subprocess. This means shell operators like pipes (`|`), redirects (`>`), and logical operators (`&&`, `||`) get treated as literal arguments instead of being interpreted by the shell.

This adds a `shell` parameter (defaults to `False`) that, when set to `True`, runs the command through `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell` instead. This matches the `subprocess.run(shell=True)` interface that was suggested in #21021.

Example usage in `prefect.yaml`:

```yaml
push:
  - prefect.deployments.steps.run_shell_script:
      script: aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
      shell: true
```

Default behavior is unchanged. Existing scripts that don't use `shell: true` work exactly as before.

Tests added for pipes, redirects, logical operators, env vars, directory support, multiline scripts, error propagation, and backward compatibility.

Fixes #11287
prefecthq/prefect#21130.title: fix: add shell mode to run_shell_script for pipe and redirect support

### Bug summary

In Prefect version - 3.6.22, when we pass multi-hosts in DB connection string, Postgres Listener failed to establish PostgreSQL connection, as it is not able to determine parameter - "target_session_attrs=primary", which is part of the DB connection string.

**ERROR** : 
prefect.server.utilities.postgres_listener - Failed to establish raw asyncpg connection for LISTEN/NOTIFY: None of the hosts match the target attribute requirement <SessionAttribute.primary: 'primary'>

asyncpg.exception._base.TargetServerAttributeNotMatched: None of the hosts match the target attribute requirement <SessionAttribute.primary: 'primary'>

**DB Connection String Example** : "postgresql+asyncpg://user@/dbname?host=HostA:5432&host=HostB:5432&host=HostC:5432&gsslib-gssapi&krbsrvname=postgresql&ssl=require&target_session_attrs=primary"

Asyncpg ( https://magicstack.github.io/asyncpg/current/api/index.html) support parameter - target_session_attrs=primary.

### Version info

```Text
Prefect Version - 3.6.22
```

### Additional context

_No response_
