prefecthq/prefect#21223.body:
### Describe the current behavior

  Connecting prefect-redis to a Redis instance today means setting up to six separate environment variables:
```bash
  export PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_HOST=my-redis.example.com
  export PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_PORT=6379
  export PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_DB=0
  export PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_USERNAME=default
  export PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_PASSWORD=supersecret
  export PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_SSL=true
```
 
Every managed Redis provider (AWS ElastiCache, Redis Cloud, Upstash, Railway) gives you a single connection URL. 

Right now you have to manually pick that URL apart and map each piece to its own variable. 

That's annoying, easy to get wrong, and doesn't match how the rest of Prefect works. As I figured out :D 



### Describe the proposed behavior

  Add a single `PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_URL` environment variable (and a matching url field on RedisMessagingSettings). 

When it's set, Prefect uses the upstream Redis.from_url() to connect. 

The existing separate fields keep working as a fallback, nothing breaks for current users.

  This brings Redis messaging configuration in line with how Prefect already handles other service connections:

  - Database: `PREFECT_API_DATABASE_CONNECTION_URL`
  - Docket (also Redis-backed): `PREFECT_DOCKET_URL`

Docket connects to Redis and uses a single URL. 

The Redis messaging layer should follow the same pattern rather than requiring users to decompose the same kind of URL into six separate fields. 

### Example Use

#### One line instead of six
```
  # One line instead of six
  export PREFECT_REDIS_MESSAGING_URL=rediss://default:supersecret@my-redis.example.com:6379/0
```
 
####  Picks up the URL from the environment
```
  from prefect_redis.client import RedisMessagingSettings

  settings = RedisMessagingSettings()
```

#### Or pass it directly
```
  from prefect_redis.client import RedisMessagingSettings

  settings = RedisMessagingSettings(url="redis://localhost:6379/0")
```

### Additional context

**redis-py's `Redis.from_url()`** already handles **all** the **heavy lifting**: it parses `redis://`, `rediss://` **(TLS)**, and `unix://` schemes.

There's no custom URL parsing needed in prefect-redis directly
prefecthq/prefect#21223.title: prefect-redis: support Redis URL for connection configuration

prefecthq/prefect#21258.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes `prefect deploy --all` so work-pool options are applied during multi-deploy, including `PREFECT_DEFAULT_WORK_POOL_NAME`/`--pool`, instead of being dropped. 

It preserves existing behavior where unrelated CLI options (like `--cron`) are still ignored for multi-deploy and continue to show the warning.

## What changed

- Updated `deploy()` in `src/prefect/cli/deploy/_commands.py` to:
  - Filter multi-deploy pass-through options to `work_pool_name`, `work_queue_name`, and `variables`
  - Warn only when non-pass-through options are provided
  - Pass filtered options into `_run_multi_deploy`
- Updated `_run_multi_deploy` in `src/prefect/cli/deploy/_core.py` to accept and forward `options` to `_run_single_deploy`
- Added regression test in `tests/cli/test_deploy.py`:
  - `test_deploy_all_uses_default_work_pool_from_settings`
  - Verifies `deploy --all` uses `PREFECT_DEFAULT_WORK_POOL_NAME` for multiple deployments without explicit `work_pool` in YAML

## Test plan

- `uv run pytest tests/cli/test_deploy.py::TestMultiDeploy::test_deploy_all_uses_default_work_pool_from_settings tests/cli/test_deploy.py::TestMultiDeploy::test_deploy_multiple_with_cli_options -x`
- Confirmed both tests pass.

Closes [#15791](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/15791)

### Checklist
<!-- These boxes may be checked after opening the pull request. -->

- [X] This pull request references any related issue by including "closes `<link to issue>`"
  - If no issue exists and your change is not a small fix, please [create an issue](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/issues/new/choose) first.
- [X] If this pull request adds new functionality, it includes unit tests that cover the changes
- [ ] If this pull request removes docs files, it includes redirect settings in `mint.json`.
- [ ] If this pull request adds functions or classes, it includes helpful docstrings.

prefecthq/prefect#21261.body:
### Bug summary

Upgraded Prefect (worker role) from `3.6.0` to `3.6.23`.

Observed behavior: executing docker's `entrypoint.sh` with worker bootstrap fails on version API call:

> httpx.HTTPStatusError: Client error '401 Unauthorized' for url 'https://scrubber.stage.my-server.com/api/admin/version'

As no other changes were added on our side, started tracking back Prefect version changes.

Found this PR that seemed relevant to the problem: [Add PREFECT_CLIENT_SERVER_VERSION_CHECK_ENABLED setting](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/pull/20973), added in [3.6.21 release](https://github.com/PrefectHQ/prefect/releases/tag/3.6.21).

To verify, switched off `PREFECT_CLIENT_SERVER_VERSION_CHECK_ENABLED`.

And the error is gone, prefect worker connected successfully to the server. To me this looks as a regression.

My `entrypoint.sh`, only relevant parts:

```sh
# Set API key for Prefect server API authentication
if [ -n "${PREFECT_SERVER_API_KEY}" ]; then
  export PREFECT_CLIENT_CUSTOM_HEADERS="{\"apikey\": \"${PREFECT_SERVER_API_KEY}\"}"
else
  echo "⚠️  PREFECT_SERVER_API_KEY is not set. Skipping configuration of PREFECT_CLIENT_CUSTOM_HEADERS."
fi

echo "🟢 Starting Prefect worker..."
prefect worker start --pool "scrubber" --type "process" --with-healthcheck
```

prefecthq/prefect#21272.body:
Automated documentation update triggered by commit e76e94f8227975dfb897db2aec9b854d3296e105.

This PR was generated by Claude Code analyzing source code changes and updating any stale documentation.

<details>
<summary>Source files changed in triggering push</summary>

```
src/prefect/_internal/concurrency/services.py
src/prefect/events/worker.py
src/prefect/settings/models/events.py
src/prefect/settings/models/root.py
```
</details>

## Missing coverage recommendations

The following new features have no documentation yet and may need new pages or sections:

## Missing Coverage Recommendations

### Change summary
PR "Add bounded queue support to QueueService to prevent OOM during server outages" introduces:
- New `PREFECT_EVENTS_WORKER_MAX_QUEUE_SIZE` setting (default: 0 = unbounded)
- When the event worker queue is full, new events are silently dropped with a warning log
- This prevents OOM crashes when the Prefect server is unreachable for extended periods

### Docs applied automatically
- `docs/v3/api-ref/settings-ref.mdx` — regenerated via `scripts/generate_settings_ref.py` to include the new `EventsSettings` section with `worker_max_queue_size`

### Recommendations for new coverage

1. **`docs/v3/advanced/self-hosted.mdx` or a new troubleshooting/tuning page**
   Mention `PREFECT_EVENTS_WORKER_MAX_QUEUE_SIZE` as an operational knob for deployments where the Prefect server may be intermittently unavailable. Operators should understand the trade-off: unbounded (default) risks OOM under prolonged outages; bounded drops events but protects memory. Suggest a conservative value (e.g., 10000) for long-running worker deployments.

2. **`docs/v3/concepts/events.mdx`**
   Add a note that events are buffered in memory before delivery to the server. Under a sustained server outage, this buffer grows unboundedly by default. Link to the `PREFECT_EVENTS_WORKER_MAX_QUEUE_SIZE` setting as the way to cap memory usage.

3. **`docs/v3/advanced/server-helm.mdx`** (if self-hosted Helm deployments are the primary audience)
   If running workers with Helm, the setting can be set as an environment variable in the worker deployment spec.
prefecthq/prefect#21272.title: Update docs for e76e94f

prefecthq/prefect#21344.body:
### Bug summary

**Bug summary**

In Prefect 3.3.4, Latvian diacritic characters in `task_run_name` / `flow_run_name` render correctly in logs and standard UI views, but are displayed incorrectly in the graphical flow run view.

**Environment**

* Prefect 3.3.4
* Python 3.12.10
* Docker base image: `prefecthq/prefect:3.3.4-python3.12`

**What works**

* Terminal output shows `Āķīši` correctly
* Prefect logs show `Āķīši` correctly
* Regular UI log view shows `Āķīši` correctly

**What fails**

* In the graphical flow/task visualization, Latvian characters are displayed incorrectly

**Minimal reproduction**

```python
from prefect import flow, task, get_run_logger

@task(task_run_name="Āķīši")
def t():
    logger = get_run_logger()
    logger.info("LOG TEST Āķīši")
    return "Āķīši"

@flow(flow_run_name="FLOW Āķīši")
def test_flow():
    t()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_flow()
```

**Observed result**
Logs and text output are correct, but the graph view displays the task/flow name incorrectly.

**Expected result**
Unicode characters with Latvian diacritics should render consistently in the graph view as well.


### Version info

prefecthq/prefect#21351.body:
### Describe the current behavior

In the event of a ECS task definition registration failure, the workflow fails to run. This is usually due to rate limits around `RegisterTaskDefinition` operations.

```
Encountered exception during execution: RuntimeError("Deployment '<example> ended with StateType.CRASHED: Failed to submit flow run to infrastructure: ClientException: An error occurred (ClientException) when calling the RegisterTaskDefinition operation: Too many concurrent attempts to create a new revision of the specified family.")
```

### Describe the proposed behavior

In the event of a failure, we should retry the task definition registration using exponential backoff. Ideally, the number of retries should be configurable.

pydantic/pydantic#8791.body:
### Initial Checks

- [X] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [X] I have read and followed [the docs](https://docs.pydantic.dev) and still think this feature is missing

### Description

When a `@model_serializer` is added to a model, it will use the type hints on the function to define the the serialization schema: `.model_json_schema(mode="serialization")`. Unfortunately, there does not appear to be a nice way to tell pydanic that this serializer function is not changing the schema. 

Something like: `@model_serializer(..., update_serialization_schema=False)` would be very nice to have.

When there is no custom serializer the validation and serialization schemas are, of course, the same:

```python
class SomeModel(BaseModel):
    foo: str

SomeModel.model_json_schema(mode="validation")
# {'properties': {'foo': {'title': 'Foo', 'type': 'string'}}, 'required': ['foo'], 'title': 'SomeModel', 'type': 'object'}
SomeModel.model_json_schema(mode="serialization")
# {'properties': {'foo': {'title': 'Foo', 'type': 'string'}}, 'required': ['foo'], 'title': 'SomeModel', 'type': 'object'}
```

When a a model serializer, with type hints, is added the serialization schema changes. 
```python
class CleanModel(SomeModel):
    @model_serializer(when_used="always", mode="wrap")
    def clean_model(
        self, serializer: SerializerFunctionWrapHandler, info: SerializationInfo
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        data: Dict[str, Any] = serializer(self)
        # Do some cleaning
        return data
    
CleanModel.model_json_schema(mode="serialization")
# {'title': 'CleanModel', 'type': 'object'}
```

The best way I have found to get the desired output of the original schema is not defining a return type at all. But it is a bit hacky, and you no longer benefit from type checking for the return. 
```python
class DifferentCleanModel(SomeModel):
    @model_serializer(when_used="always", mode="wrap")
    def clean_model(
        self, serializer: SerializerFunctionWrapHandler, info: SerializationInfo
    ):  # type: ignore [no-untyped-def]:
        data: Dict[str, Any] = serializer(self)
        # Do some cleaning
        return data

DifferentCleanModel.model_json_schema(mode="serialization")
# {'properties': {'foo': {'title': 'Foo', 'type': 'string'}}, 'required': ['foo'], 'title': 'DifferentCleanModel', 'type': 'object'}
```

While writing this up, and digging through the pydantic code again, I did find the `json_schema_mode_override` config, but I have been unable to get it to work as I would expect it. The `.model_json_schema()` still uses whatever is specified in the function call. This seems like it may be a bug, but I am not sure.
```python
class AnotherCleanModel(SomeModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(json_schema_mode_override="validation")

    @model_serializer(when_used="always", mode="wrap")
    def clean_model(
        self, serializer: SerializerFunctionWrapHandler, info: SerializationInfo
    ) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        data: Dict[str, Any] = serializer(self)
        # Do some cleaning
        return data

AnotherCleanModel.model_json_schema(mode="validation")
# {'properties': {'foo': {'title': 'Foo', 'type': 'string'}}, 'required': ['foo'], 'title': 'AnotherCleanModel', 'type': 'object'}
AnotherCleanModel.model_json_schema(mode="serialization")
# {'title': 'AnotherCleanModel', 'type': 'object'}
```

Even if this config worked as I expected, I would still like to make the case for a clean way to specify that this specific serializer does not modify the schema in any way. In the case of a shared library, a down stream user may modify the config and then run into the same issue again. Whereas addressing it at the serializer level would hopefully not be affected by those downstream config changes. 

For a bit more background, this came up because FastAPI calls `.model_json_schema(mode="serialization")` under the hood when generating schemas. Which makes sense, you want to tell your users how you are sending them data. And [geojson-pydantic](https://github.com/developmentseed/geojson-pydantic) uses a custom serializer to handle some cleanup necessary to output data according to the GeoJSON Spec. So anyone using, or inheriting from, those models were just getting `'type': 'object'` in their schemas in FastAPI (https://github.com/developmentseed/geojson-pydantic/issues/150). So the workaround removing the type hint was implemented (https://github.com/developmentseed/geojson-pydantic/pull/153) to get past the issue, but it feels like there should be a better defined, and well documented, solution to the issue. 

Also a related FastAPI discussion: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/10661

### Affected Components

- [ ] [Compatibility between releases](https://docs.pydantic.dev/changelog/)
- [ ] [Data validation/parsing](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#basic-model-usage)
- [X] [Data serialization](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/serialization/) - `.model_dump()` and `.model_dump_json()`
- [X] [JSON Schema](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/json_schema/)
- [ ] [Dataclasses](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/dataclasses/)
- [ ] [Model Config](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/config/)
- [ ] [Field Types](https://docs.pydantic.dev/api/types/) - adding or changing a particular data type
- [ ] [Function validation decorator](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/validation_decorator/)
- [ ] [Generic Models](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/#generic-models)
- [ ] [Other Model behaviour](https://docs.pydantic.dev/concepts/models/) - `model_construct()`, pickling, private attributes, ORM mode
- [ ] [Plugins](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) and integration with other tools - mypy, FastAPI, python-devtools, Hypothesis, VS Code, PyCharm, etc.
pydantic/pydantic#8791.title: Request: Ability to specify a model_serializer does not change the serialization JSON Schema.

recharts/recharts#172.body: ResponsiveContainer computes the size of the chart based on the current window size. It also seems to respond when you make the browser window larger, however if you make the browser window smaller, the chart stays large. Tested on chrome.
recharts/recharts#172.title: ResponsiveContainer does not respond to resizing from large to small
